By combining operando characterization with theoretical calculations, we demonstrate that triggering electron spin polarization decreases insertion-conversion energy barrier, accelerate charge transport at Co/Li<sub>2</sub>S interfaces, and construct a LiF-enriched SEI, enabling ultrafast charging of MoS<sub>2</sub> electrodes in lithium-ion batteries. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>The development of ultrafast-charging MoS<sub>2</sub> anodes is fundamentally constrained by sluggish charge-transfer kinetics. This study addresses this limitation by triggering electron spin polarization through atomic-scale Co doping within open-hollow MoS<sub>2</sub> nanospheres. Structurally, the open-hollow design minimizes ion diffusion distances and accommodates volume changes of MoS<sub>2</sub>, providing a robust foundation for rapid ion flux. Electronically, Co incorporation enhances S 3p–Mo 4d–Co 3d orbital hybridization, triggering a marked electron spin polarization that reduces kinetic barriers for insertion and conversion reactions. Post-conversion stage, in situ generated metallic Co nanoparticles (Co<sup>0</sup>) act as dynamic mediators for spin-polarized electron transfer. Specifically, the injection of spin-polarized electrons into Co° creates a spin-polarized surface capacitance, boosting charge storage at the Co<sup>0</sup>/Li<sub>2</sub>S interfaces. Conversely, the release of these electrons promotes a Co<sup>0</sup>-catalyzed construction of solid electrolyte interphase, prioritizing conductive LiF species while suppressing Li<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>, facilitating ion transport at the electrode-electrolyte interfaces. Consequently, the MoS<sub>2</sub>-based anode exhibits an impressive ultrafast-charging capability of 30 C (1044.1 mAh g<sup>−1</sup>) and maintains stability over 10 000 cycles at 15 C with a final capacity of 874.7 mAh g<sup>−1</sup>. This work demonstrates that triggering electron spin polarization represents a transformative approach to overcoming kinetic barriers in next-generation ultrafast-charging batteries.</p> Kunxiong Zheng, Hengyuan Hu, Zhiyu Zou, Yuankai Huang, Haolin Ju, Yongbiao Mu, Wenjia Li, Lei Wei, Lin Zeng, Tianshou Zhao, Meisheng Han RESEARCH ARTICLE Accelerating Charge‐Transfer Kinetics via Triggering Electron Spin Polarization in Open‐Hollow MoS2 Nanospheres for Ultrafast Lithium Storage 10.1002/adma.73551 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73551 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73551?af=R RESEARCH ARTICLE https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73582?af=R Wed, 03 Jun 2026 00:06:47 -0700 2026-06-03T12:06:47-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents 10.1002/adma.73582 Switching Water Oxidation Pathway via NiFe Dual‐Atoms on BiVO4: An *O─O* Coupling Mechanism Route to Bypass Adsorbate Evolution Mechanism Limitations Advanced Materials, EarlyView. The NiFe DAs/TiOx/BiVO4 photoanode demonstrates a high photocurrent of 6.13 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE for 150 h, which shifts the oxygen evolution mechanism from adsorbate evolution mechanism (AEM) in Ni (Fe) SAs TiOx/BiVO4 to *O─O* coupling mechanism (OCM) pathway, providing atomic‐level mechanistic insights for efficient photoelectrochemical water oxidation. ABSTRACT Dual‐atom catalysts (DACs) with heterogeneous active sites represent an emerging frontier in photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting. However, the corresponding reaction mechanism on DACs is still unclear. Herein, we present a photoanode architecture comprising NiFe dual atoms (DAs) anchored on a TiOx‐coated BiVO4 photoanode (NiFe DAs/TiOx/BiVO4), which delivers an impressive photocurrent density of 6.13 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE, sustained stability exceeding 150 h, and an applied bias photon‐to‐current efficiency of 2.2%. Our investigation reveals a dual functionality of the NiFe DAs which serve as an efficient oxygen evolution cocatalyst and enhance charge separation—an aspect largely overlooked in previous studies. Through in situ spectroscopic investigations combined with density functional theory calculations, we elucidate that the NiFe DAs/TiOx/BiVO4 enables a mechanistic shift from the conventional adsorbate evolution mechanism (AEM) observed in single atom‐modified‐TiOx/BiVO4 to an *O─O* coupling mechanism (OCM). Specifically, this OCM pathway bypasses the formation of *OOH and produces the *O─O* bridging species, broking the *OOH/*OH scaling limitation in the AEM pathway. This work uncovers PEC water oxidation mechanism at the atomic level, establishing a foundational framework for designing high‐performance photoelectrodes through precise atomic‐scale engineering. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/b161ee38-d860-4cc2-a64c-b4143754f587/adma73582-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Switching Water Oxidation Pathway via NiFe Dual-Atoms on BiVO4: An *O─O* Coupling Mechanism Route to Bypass Adsorbate Evolution Mechanism Limitations"/> <p>The NiFe DAs/TiO<sub>x</sub>/BiVO<sub>4</sub> photoanode demonstrates a high photocurrent of 6.13 mA cm<sup>−2</sup> at 1.23 V<sub>RHE</sub> for 150 h, which shifts the oxygen evolution mechanism from adsorbate evolution mechanism (AEM) in Ni (Fe) SAs TiO<sub>x</sub>/BiVO<sub>4</sub> to *O─O* coupling mechanism (OCM) pathway, providing atomic-level mechanistic insights for efficient photoelectrochemical water oxidation. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Dual-atom catalysts (DACs) with heterogeneous active sites represent an emerging frontier in photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting. However, the corresponding reaction mechanism on DACs is still unclear. Herein, we present a photoanode architecture comprising NiFe dual atoms (DAs) anchored on a TiO<sub>x</sub>-coated BiVO<sub>4</sub> photoanode (NiFe DAs/TiO<sub>x</sub>/BiVO<sub>4</sub>), which delivers an impressive photocurrent density of 6.13 mA cm<sup>−2</sup> at 1.23 V<sub>RHE</sub>, sustained stability exceeding 150 h, and an applied bias photon-to-current efficiency of 2.2%. Our investigation reveals a dual functionality of the NiFe DAs which serve as an efficient oxygen evolution cocatalyst and enhance charge separation—an aspect largely overlooked in previous studies. Through in situ spectroscopic investigations combined with density functional theory calculations, we elucidate that the NiFe DAs/TiO<sub>x</sub>/BiVO<sub>4</sub> enables a mechanistic shift from the conventional adsorbate evolution mechanism (AEM) observed in single atom-modified-TiO<sub>x</sub>/BiVO<sub>4</sub> to an *O─O* coupling mechanism (OCM). Specifically, this OCM pathway bypasses the formation of *OOH and produces the *O─O* bridging species, broking the *OOH/*OH scaling limitation in the AEM pathway. This work uncovers PEC water oxidation mechanism at the atomic level, establishing a foundational framework for designing high-performance photoelectrodes through precise atomic-scale engineering.</p> Yan Zhang, Zhongrui Min, Hao Jin, Ru Wan, Jiaming Zhang, Fengtao Fan, Sheng Ye RESEARCH ARTICLE Switching Water Oxidation Pathway via NiFe Dual‐Atoms on BiVO4: An *O─O* Coupling Mechanism Route to Bypass Adsorbate Evolution Mechanism Limitations 10.1002/adma.73582 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73582 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73582?af=R RESEARCH ARTICLE https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73577?af=R Wed, 03 Jun 2026 00:05:14 -0700 2026-06-03T12:05:14-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents 10.1002/adma.73577 Uncovering Local Piezoelectric Field Effect in Mechanoluminescent Materials Advanced Materials, EarlyView. By exploring the differential luminescence responses under various stimuli, this study quantitatively links macroscopic piezoelectric fields to local crystal structure, thereby providing crystallographic experimental evidence for uncovering local piezoelectric fields in mechanoluminescent materials. ABSTRACT Elastic mechanoluminescent (ML) materials have significant potential in intelligent sensing, dynamic displays, and artificial intelligence. However, concrete experimental evidence of localized piezoelectric fields has long been missing in piezoelectrically controlled elastic ML materials. In this work, we propose a strategy for uncovering the local piezoelectric field effect in ML materials and demonstrate that crystallographic site symmetry and local structure determine the local piezoelectric field, which further profoundly influences the ML properties of the material. We screen and design multisite deep‑red to near‑infrared (NIR) self‑recoverable ML materials, Mn2+‐activated MGa2S4 (M = Ca, Sr). Beyond the report of ultrabroadband ML performance, we have defined and calculated, for the first time, the centrosymmetry deviation degree (CDD) of a polyhedron, and elucidated the correlation among site symmetry, CDD, polyhedral distortion degree, local piezoelectric responses, and ML intensity. With the assistance of DFT and DFPT calculations, we have clarified the contribution of local polarization at specific sites and established a dynamic model for local‑piezoelectric‑field‑induced ML in multisite materials. Furthermore, leveraging the ultrabroadband ML emission of this material series, we fabricated various flexible devices, which demonstrated promising application potential in fields such as rehabilitation medicine, deep‐tissue bioimaging, and NIR information encryption. This work holds potential to offer valuable insights for discovering, designing, and yielding novel ML materials. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/417d8ae0-a226-4cd1-91a8-f67a2f6f613c/adma73577-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Uncovering Local Piezoelectric Field Effect in Mechanoluminescent Materials"/> <p>By exploring the differential luminescence responses under various stimuli, this study quantitatively links macroscopic piezoelectric fields to local crystal structure, thereby providing crystallographic experimental evidence for uncovering local piezoelectric fields in mechanoluminescent materials. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Elastic mechanoluminescent (ML) materials have significant potential in intelligent sensing, dynamic displays, and artificial intelligence. However, concrete experimental evidence of localized piezoelectric fields has long been missing in piezoelectrically controlled elastic ML materials. In this work, we propose a strategy for uncovering the local piezoelectric field effect in ML materials and demonstrate that crystallographic site symmetry and local structure determine the local piezoelectric field, which further profoundly influences the ML properties of the material. We screen and design multisite deep‑red to near‑infrared (NIR) self‑recoverable ML materials, Mn<sup>2+</sup>-activated MGa<sub>2</sub>S<sub>4</sub> (M = Ca, Sr). Beyond the report of ultrabroadband ML performance, we have defined and calculated, for the first time, the centrosymmetry deviation degree (<i>CDD</i>) of a polyhedron, and elucidated the correlation among site symmetry, <i>CDD</i>, polyhedral distortion degree, local piezoelectric responses, and ML intensity. With the assistance of DFT and DFPT calculations, we have clarified the contribution of local polarization at specific sites and established a dynamic model for local‑piezoelectric‑field‑induced ML in multisite materials. Furthermore, leveraging the ultrabroadband ML emission of this material series, we fabricated various flexible devices, which demonstrated promising application potential in fields such as rehabilitation medicine, deep-tissue bioimaging, and NIR information encryption. This work holds potential to offer valuable insights for discovering, designing, and yielding novel ML materials.</p> Yuhe Shao, Jia Shi, Hongzhen Liu, Zhen Song, Quanlin Liu RESEARCH ARTICLE Uncovering Local Piezoelectric Field Effect in Mechanoluminescent Materials 10.1002/adma.73577 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73577 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73577?af=R RESEARCH ARTICLE https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73520?af=R Wed, 03 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 2026-06-03T12:00:00-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents 10.1002/adma.73520 Observation of Moiré Trapped Biexciton Through Sub‐Diffraction‐Limit Probing Using Hetero‐Bilayer on Nanopillar Advanced Materials, EarlyView. This study demonstrates stable moiré biexciton within a strained hetero‐bilayer system, observation of which is made possible by engineering a sub‐diffraction‐limit probing technique. The exciton–exciton exchange interaction overcomes the repulsive dipolar interaction at close inter‐exciton distances, which stabilizes two interacting excitons within a single moiré pocket to a stable biexciton state. ABSTRACT The ability to tune the degree of interaction among particles at the nanoscale is highly intriguing. The spectroscopic signature of such interaction is often subtle and requires special probes to observe. To this end, inter‐layer excitons trapped in the periodic potential wells of a moiré superlattice offer rich interaction physics, specifically due to the presence of both attractive and repulsive components in the interaction. Here we show that the Coulomb force between two inter‐layer excitons switches from repulsive to attractive when the length scale reduces from inter‐moiré‐pocket to intra‐moiré‐pocket in a WS2/WSe2 hetero‐bilayer ‐ thanks to the complex competition between direct and exchange interaction. The finding is a departure from the usual notion of repelling inter‐layer excitons due to layer polarization. This manifests as the simultaneous observation of an anomalous superlinear power‐law of moiré exciton and a stabilization of moiré trapped biexciton. The experimental observation is facilitated by placing the hetero‐bilayer on a polymer‐nanopillar/gold‐film stack which significantly reduces the inhomogeneous spectral broadening by selectively probing a smaller ensemble of moiré pockets compared with a flat sample. This creates an interesting platform to explore interaction among moiré trapped excitons and higher order quasiparticles. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/dfd517ac-5ee1-4e11-bd47-19240a690589/adma73520-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Observation of Moiré Trapped Biexciton Through Sub-Diffraction-Limit Probing Using Hetero-Bilayer on Nanopillar"/> <p>This study demonstrates stable moiré biexciton within a strained hetero-bilayer system, observation of which is made possible by engineering a sub-diffraction-limit probing technique. The exciton–exciton exchange interaction overcomes the repulsive dipolar interaction at close inter-exciton distances, which stabilizes two interacting excitons within a single moiré pocket to a stable biexciton state. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>The ability to tune the degree of interaction among particles at the nanoscale is highly intriguing. The spectroscopic signature of such interaction is often subtle and requires special probes to observe. To this end, inter-layer excitons trapped in the periodic potential wells of a moiré superlattice offer rich interaction physics, specifically due to the presence of both attractive and repulsive components in the interaction. Here we show that the Coulomb force between two inter-layer excitons switches from repulsive to attractive when the length scale reduces from inter-moiré-pocket to intra-moiré-pocket in a WS<sub>2</sub>/WSe<sub>2</sub> hetero-bilayer - thanks to the complex competition between direct and exchange interaction. The finding is a departure from the usual notion of repelling inter-layer excitons due to layer polarization. This manifests as the simultaneous observation of an anomalous superlinear power-law of moiré exciton and a stabilization of moiré trapped biexciton. The experimental observation is facilitated by placing the hetero-bilayer on a polymer-nanopillar/gold-film stack which significantly reduces the inhomogeneous spectral broadening by selectively probing a smaller ensemble of moiré pockets compared with a flat sample. This creates an interesting platform to explore interaction among moiré trapped excitons and higher order quasiparticles.</p> Mayank Chhaperwal, Suman Chatterjee, Suchithra Puliyassery, Jyothsna Konkada Manattayil, Rabindra Biswas, Patrick Hays, Seth Ariel Tongay, Varun Raghunathan, Kausik Majumdar RESEARCH ARTICLE Observation of Moiré Trapped Biexciton Through Sub‐Diffraction‐Limit Probing Using Hetero‐Bilayer on Nanopillar 10.1002/adma.73520 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73520 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73520?af=R RESEARCH ARTICLE https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73545?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 07:54:56 -0700 2026-06-02T07:54:56-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents 10.1002/adma.73545 Ordered–Disorder Transition Induced Stretchable Collagen Ionic Gels Toward Flexible Sensors Advanced Materials, EarlyView. This work employs Hofmeister‐ion engineering to disrupt the molecular ordered structure of collagen in its aggregation structure. This entropy‐driven disordering strategy transforms natural collagen into a highly stretchable and conductive ionic gel. The resulting material exhibits exceptional elasticity, durable ionic conductivity, and reliable sensing performance, providing a versatile platform for flexible electronics and implantable systems. ABSTRACT Natural structural proteins such as collagen offer exceptional biocompatibility and mechanical versatility, making them promising candidates for wearable and implantable sensing applications. However, achieving integrated high stretchability, stable electrical performance, and long‐term durability in protein‐based sensing materials remains a critical challenge. Conventional modification strategies are largely confined to molecular‐scale adjustments, which often fail to simultaneously address these functional demands. This study introduces a fundamentally different approach by leveraging Hofmeister‐ion engineering to directly regulate the aggregation structure of tropocollagen, which enables a structural transformation from long‐range order to entropy‐driven disorder. Unlike previous methods that rely on chemical crosslinking or exogenous additives, our strategy selectively disrupts collagen's supramolecular order while preserving its triple‐helical integrity, resulting in a highly stretchable and conductive ionic gel with exceptional elasticity (fracture strain 560.00%, elastic range 300.53%, resilience 97.36%), durable ionic conductivity (0.56 S m−1), and reliable sensing performance over 1500 tensile cycles. By establishing a new paradigm of mesoscale disordering, this work not only deepens the understanding of ion‐modulated hierarchical assembly but also provides a versatile, biocompatible platform for next‐generation flexible electronics, soft robotics, and implantable sensing systems. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/81ab6628-39c0-4c1d-b260-dfa4a1f08e88/adma73545-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Ordered–Disorder Transition Induced Stretchable Collagen Ionic Gels Toward Flexible Sensors"/> <p>This work employs Hofmeister-ion engineering to disrupt the molecular ordered structure of collagen in its aggregation structure. This entropy-driven disordering strategy transforms natural collagen into a highly stretchable and conductive ionic gel. The resulting material exhibits exceptional elasticity, durable ionic conductivity, and reliable sensing performance, providing a versatile platform for flexible electronics and implantable systems. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Natural structural proteins such as collagen offer exceptional biocompatibility and mechanical versatility, making them promising candidates for wearable and implantable sensing applications. However, achieving integrated high stretchability, stable electrical performance, and long-term durability in protein-based sensing materials remains a critical challenge. Conventional modification strategies are largely confined to molecular-scale adjustments, which often fail to simultaneously address these functional demands. This study introduces a fundamentally different approach by leveraging Hofmeister-ion engineering to directly regulate the aggregation structure of tropocollagen, which enables a structural transformation from long-range order to entropy-driven disorder. Unlike previous methods that rely on chemical crosslinking or exogenous additives, our strategy selectively disrupts collagen's supramolecular order while preserving its triple-helical integrity, resulting in a highly stretchable and conductive ionic gel with exceptional elasticity (fracture strain 560.00%, elastic range 300.53%, resilience 97.36%), durable ionic conductivity (0.56 S m<sup>−1</sup>), and reliable sensing performance over 1500 tensile cycles. By establishing a new paradigm of mesoscale disordering, this work not only deepens the understanding of ion-modulated hierarchical assembly but also provides a versatile, biocompatible platform for next-generation flexible electronics, soft robotics, and implantable sensing systems.</p> Ying Pei, Jiaqi Li, Lele Liu, Yuwei Zhao, Weifang Yang, Keyong Tang, Ang Lu RESEARCH ARTICLE Ordered–Disorder Transition Induced Stretchable Collagen Ionic Gels Toward Flexible Sensors 10.1002/adma.73545 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73545 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73545?af=R RESEARCH ARTICLE https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73473?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 07:45:06 -0700 2026-06-02T07:45:06-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents 10.1002/adma.73473 Breaking the Voltage‐Loss Bottleneck in Organic Photovoltaics via Interfacial Molecular Orientation Engineering Advanced Materials, EarlyView. A precisely controlled bilayer system using a polydimethylsiloxane film‐transfer method reveals the intrinsic role of donor‐acceptor interfacial molecular orientation in governing voltage loss (Vloss) in organic photovoltaics (OPVs). Non‐radiative voltage loss (ΔV3) increases monotonically face‐on/face‐on, edge‐on/face‐on, face‐on/edge‐on to edge‐on/edge‐on configurations, highlighting donor‐acceptor interfacial orientation as a crucial factor for minimizing ΔV3 and Vloss in OPVs. ABSTRACT Benefiting from non‐fullerene acceptors, organic photovoltaics (OPVs) have achieved power conversion efficiencies exceeding 21%. However, further progress critically depends on suppressing voltage losses (Vloss), particularly non‐radiative voltage losses (ΔV3). Here, we employ a precisely controlled donor/acceptor bilayer system, fabricated via a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) film‐transfer method, to systematically investigate the intrinsic role of interfacial molecular orientation in governing Vloss. Devices with a face‐on/face‐on (P5TCN‐F0/Y6) configuration exhibit markedly reduced ΔV3 and overall Vloss compared with edge‐on/face‐on (P5TCN‐F50/Y6), face‐on/edge‐on (P5TCN‐F0/BTP‐2T), and edge‐on/edge‐on (P5TCN‐F50/BTP‐2T) counterparts. Transient absorption spectroscopy and time‐resolved photoluminescence measurements reveal that the face‐on/face‐on interface enables efficient charge transfer while suppressing charge‐transfer (CT) states recombination. Quantitative analysis further demonstrates that the markedly reduced non‐radiative recombination rate of the CT states is responsible for the enhanced electroluminescence quantum efficiency and the reduced ΔV3 and Vloss. These findings establish interfacial molecular orientation as a key lever for minimizing ΔV3 without compromising high photocurrent and provide material design principles and morphology control requirements for further breaking the performance bottleneck in bulk heterojunction OPVs. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/f2ab00d8-b5b8-4f2f-838f-9636bf46ddf3/adma73473-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Breaking the Voltage-Loss Bottleneck in Organic Photovoltaics via Interfacial Molecular Orientation Engineering"/> <p>A precisely controlled bilayer system using a polydimethylsiloxane film-transfer method reveals the intrinsic role of donor-acceptor interfacial molecular orientation in governing voltage loss (<i>V</i> <sub>loss</sub>) in organic photovoltaics (OPVs). Non-radiative voltage loss (Δ<i>V</i> <sub>3</sub>) increases monotonically face-on/face-on, edge-on/face-on, face-on/edge-on to edge-on/edge-on configurations, highlighting donor-acceptor interfacial orientation as a crucial factor for minimizing Δ<i>V</i> <sub>3</sub> and <i>V</i> <sub>loss</sub> in OPVs. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Benefiting from non-fullerene acceptors, organic photovoltaics (OPVs) have achieved power conversion efficiencies exceeding 21%. However, further progress critically depends on suppressing voltage losses (<i>V</i> <sub>loss</sub>), particularly non-radiative voltage losses (Δ<i>V</i> <sub>3</sub>). Here, we employ a precisely controlled donor/acceptor bilayer system, fabricated via a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) film-transfer method, to systematically investigate the intrinsic role of interfacial molecular orientation in governing <i>V</i> <sub>loss</sub>. Devices with a face-on/face-on (P5TCN-F0/Y6) configuration exhibit markedly reduced Δ<i>V</i> <sub>3</sub> and overall <i>V</i> <sub>loss</sub> compared with edge-on/face-on (P5TCN-F50/Y6), face-on/edge-on (P5TCN-F0/BTP-2T), and edge-on/edge-on (P5TCN-F50/BTP-2T) counterparts. Transient absorption spectroscopy and time-resolved photoluminescence measurements reveal that the face-on/face-on interface enables efficient charge transfer while suppressing charge-transfer (CT) states recombination. Quantitative analysis further demonstrates that the markedly reduced non-radiative recombination rate of the CT states is responsible for the enhanced electroluminescence quantum efficiency and the reduced Δ<i>V</i> <sub>3</sub> and <i>V</i> <sub>loss</sub>. These findings establish interfacial molecular orientation as a key lever for minimizing Δ<i>V</i> <sub>3</sub> without compromising high photocurrent and provide material design principles and morphology control requirements for further breaking the performance bottleneck in bulk heterojunction OPVs.</p> Weiwei Wu, Rui Sun, Xiaohei Wu, Biao Xiao, Yunlong Ma, Xiyue Yuan, Chunhui Duan, Chaohua Cui, Qingdong Zheng, Jie Min RESEARCH ARTICLE Breaking the Voltage‐Loss Bottleneck in Organic Photovoltaics via Interfacial Molecular Orientation Engineering 10.1002/adma.73473 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73473 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73473?af=R RESEARCH ARTICLE https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73600?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 04:06:45 -0700 2026-06-02T04:06:45-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents 10.1002/adma.73600 Stabilization of Unconventional Body‐Centered Tetragonal Phase in Copper Nanowires for Efficient Carbon Dioxide Electroreduction to Multi‐Carbon Products Advanced Materials, EarlyView. The internal strain in five‐fold twinned copper nanowires, induced by the need to fill a solid angle deficiency of 7.35°, triggers a phase transformation from face‐centered cubic to body‐centered tetragonal. This phase transformation alters the electronic state of copper nanowires, thereby enhancing their catalytic performance in carbon dioxide electroreduction to high‐value multi‐carbon products compared with common face‐centered cubic copper nanostructures. ABSTRACT Copper nanomaterials with the common face‐centered cubic (fcc) phase have been widely used in the electrocatalytic carbon dioxide (CO2) reduction reaction (CO2RR). However, copper with an unconventional phase is rarely reported as it is thermodynamically unfavorable. Here, through analyzing the strain within copper nanowires, we reveal the phase transition of copper from fcc to body‐centered tetragonal (bct)/fcc heterophase. By systematically investigating copper nanowires with different diameters and copper nanocubes in CO2RR, we explain the relationship between their crystal phase and catalytic performance. Compared with the standard fcc lattice, copper nanowires’ surfaces have different electron states due to a phase transition. Copper nanowires with a diameter of about 30 nm exhibit the optimum catalytic performance, and their Faradaic efficiency of multi‐carbon products is much higher than that of fcc copper nanocubes. Theoretical calculations have demonstrated that the presence of the strained bct phase induces significant upshifts of the d‐band center, which not only improves the overall electroactivity but also optimize the C‐C couplings, leading to improved Faradaic efficiency of multi‐carbon products during CO2RR. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/6475b5dd-2a86-4fbc-8ba6-06fdcb6e3c6b/adma73600-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Stabilization of Unconventional Body-Centered Tetragonal Phase in Copper Nanowires for Efficient Carbon Dioxide Electroreduction to Multi-Carbon Products"/> <p>The internal strain in five-fold twinned copper nanowires, induced by the need to fill a solid angle deficiency of 7.35°, triggers a phase transformation from face-centered cubic to body-centered tetragonal. This phase transformation alters the electronic state of copper nanowires, thereby enhancing their catalytic performance in carbon dioxide electroreduction to high-value multi-carbon products compared with common face-centered cubic copper nanostructures. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Copper nanomaterials with the common face-centered cubic (fcc) phase have been widely used in the electrocatalytic carbon dioxide (CO<sub>2</sub>) reduction reaction (CO<sub>2</sub>RR). However, copper with an unconventional phase is rarely reported as it is thermodynamically unfavorable. Here, through analyzing the strain within copper nanowires, we reveal the phase transition of copper from fcc to body-centered tetragonal (bct)/fcc heterophase. By systematically investigating copper nanowires with different diameters and copper nanocubes in CO<sub>2</sub>RR, we explain the relationship between their crystal phase and catalytic performance. Compared with the standard fcc lattice, copper nanowires’ surfaces have different electron states due to a phase transition. Copper nanowires with a diameter of about 30 nm exhibit the optimum catalytic performance, and their Faradaic efficiency of multi-carbon products is much higher than that of fcc copper nanocubes. Theoretical calculations have demonstrated that the presence of the strained bct phase induces significant upshifts of the <i>d</i>-band center, which not only improves the overall electroactivity but also optimize the C-C couplings, leading to improved Faradaic efficiency of multi-carbon products during CO<sub>2</sub>RR.</p> Guozhi Wang, Yangbo Ma, Mingzi Sun, Fengkun Hao, Qianhui Wei, Yunhao Wang, Juan Wang, Fu Liu, Xiang Meng, Liang Guo, Mingzheng Shao, Chaohui Wang, Shuheng Hao, Pengyi Lu, Yuecheng Xiong, Yanwei Lum, Shengqi Chu, Bolong Huang, Zhanxi Fan RESEARCH ARTICLE Stabilization of Unconventional Body‐Centered Tetragonal Phase in Copper Nanowires for Efficient Carbon Dioxide Electroreduction to Multi‐Carbon Products 10.1002/adma.73600 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73600 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73600?af=R RESEARCH ARTICLE https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73597?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 04:04:30 -0700 2026-06-02T04:04:30-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents 10.1002/adma.73597 Temperature Field‐Driven Directional Space‐Confined Crystallization of Perovskite Melt Toward High‐Performance Optoelectronic Devices Advanced Materials, EarlyView. A low‐cost, environmentally friendly, and high‐throughput perovskite fabrication method is pioneered via a temperature field‐driven directional space‐confined melt crystallization strategy, realizing the universal integration of high‐crystallinity perovskite single‐crystal films and high‐quality 2D‐3D perovskite heterojunctions, yielding high‐performance optical synaptic devices with linear polarization light response and high‐performance photodetectors with responsivity of 1.19 × 103 A W−1 and response speed of 160 µs. ABSTRACT The development of optoelectronic devices relies heavily on the advancement of semiconductor materials and microfabrication technologies. Notably, metal halide perovskites with superior optoelectronic properties show great prospects for optical sensing technology. However, the current processing techniques of perovskite materials are mainly solution methods and vapor‐phase methods, facing toxic solvents and material waste, which are harmful to the natural environment and human health. Herein, we originally developed the temperature field‐driven directional space‐confined melt crystallization strategy, which perfectly solved the problems of toxic solvent and material waste, yielding highly crystalline, purely oriented 2D perovskite single‐crystal films. Long‐range‐oriented single‐crystal films with fewer crystal domains and defects ensure that optical synaptic devices possess image encryption and high‐fidelity polarization imaging capabilities. Furthermore, the melt processing technique enables the fabrication of high‐quality 2D‐3D perovskite heterojunctions, which notably improve the photodetector performance of 2D perovskite. This pioneering work provides a new strategy and opportunity for the environmentally friendly, high‐throughput integration of perovskite optoelectronic devices. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/6fb53c3d-0926-448d-b47c-2969056da92c/adma73597-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Temperature Field-Driven Directional Space-Confined Crystallization of Perovskite Melt Toward High-Performance Optoelectronic Devices"/> <p>A low-cost, environmentally friendly, and high-throughput perovskite fabrication method is pioneered via a temperature field-driven directional space-confined melt crystallization strategy, realizing the universal integration of high-crystallinity perovskite single-crystal films and high-quality 2D-3D perovskite heterojunctions, yielding high-performance optical synaptic devices with linear polarization light response and high-performance photodetectors with responsivity of 1.19 × 10<sup>3</sup> A W<sup>−1</sup> and response speed of 160 µs. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>The development of optoelectronic devices relies heavily on the advancement of semiconductor materials and microfabrication technologies. Notably, metal halide perovskites with superior optoelectronic properties show great prospects for optical sensing technology. However, the current processing techniques of perovskite materials are mainly solution methods and vapor-phase methods, facing toxic solvents and material waste, which are harmful to the natural environment and human health. Herein, we originally developed the temperature field-driven directional space-confined melt crystallization strategy, which perfectly solved the problems of toxic solvent and material waste, yielding highly crystalline, purely oriented 2D perovskite single-crystal films. Long-range-oriented single-crystal films with fewer crystal domains and defects ensure that optical synaptic devices possess image encryption and high-fidelity polarization imaging capabilities. Furthermore, the melt processing technique enables the fabrication of high-quality 2D-3D perovskite heterojunctions, which notably improve the photodetector performance of 2D perovskite. This pioneering work provides a new strategy and opportunity for the environmentally friendly, high-throughput integration of perovskite optoelectronic devices.</p> Yi Hao, Kaixin Dong, Xinzhang Lin, Junjie Liu, Zhonghao Wei, Jinjin Zhao, Jingyuan Zhang, Xiangxiang Yang, Dan Li, Jingcheng Hu, Kaiqing Jiang, Mengru Zhang, Jiawang Zou, Zihao Zhu, Kebin Lin, Lei Jiang, Yuchen Wu, Yanlin Song, Yingjie Zhao RESEARCH ARTICLE Temperature Field‐Driven Directional Space‐Confined Crystallization of Perovskite Melt Toward High‐Performance Optoelectronic Devices 10.1002/adma.73597 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73597 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73597?af=R RESEARCH ARTICLE https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73561?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 03:06:59 -0700 2026-06-02T03:06:59-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents 10.1002/adma.73561 2D Perovskite Engineering Enables Robust Self‐Assembled Monolayers for High‐Performance Perovskite Solar Cells Advanced Materials, EarlyView. An in situ interfacial protection strategy stabilizes self‐assembled monolayers (SAMs) via transient 2D perovskite formation, suppressing interfacial degradation and enabling uniform crystallization. This approach delivers high‐efficiency, large‐area perovskite modules (30 cm × 30 cm) with improved operational stability, providing a scalable pathway toward reliable and commercially viable perovskite photovoltaics. ABSTRACT Self‐assembled monolayers (SAMs) are critical hole‐selective contacts in inverted perovskite solar cells (PSCs), yet their practical implementation is hindered by desorption and aggregation during solution‐based perovskite deposition. Here, we report a transient two‐dimensional (2D) perovskite protection strategy using volatile propylammonium chloride, which temporarily shields SAMs from solvent‐induced degradation and subsequently decouples during thermal annealing. This mechanism preserves the integrity of the SAM interface while enabling uniform crystallization of large‐area perovskite films and efficient charge extraction. Consequently, PSCs achieve a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 26.14% for 0.05 cm2 devices, 23.27% for a 5 × 5 cm2 mini‐module, and 22.34% for a 30 × 30 cm2 module (615.7 cm2 active area). Notably, the large‐area module retains 90% of its initial efficiency after 2000 h of continuous maximum power point operation, demonstrating both high performance and operational stability, highlighting a scalable route for reliable perovskite photovoltaics. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/d7c06d1e-3964-49b1-a81d-2a9b89d48076/adma73561-gra-0001-m.png" alt="2D Perovskite Engineering Enables Robust Self-Assembled Monolayers for High-Performance Perovskite Solar Cells"/> <p>An in situ interfacial protection strategy stabilizes self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) via transient 2D perovskite formation, suppressing interfacial degradation and enabling uniform crystallization. This approach delivers high-efficiency, large-area perovskite modules (30 cm × 30 cm) with improved operational stability, providing a scalable pathway toward reliable and commercially viable perovskite photovoltaics. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) are critical hole-selective contacts in inverted perovskite solar cells (PSCs), yet their practical implementation is hindered by desorption and aggregation during solution-based perovskite deposition. Here, we report a transient two-dimensional (2D) perovskite protection strategy using volatile propylammonium chloride, which temporarily shields SAMs from solvent-induced degradation and subsequently decouples during thermal annealing. This mechanism preserves the integrity of the SAM interface while enabling uniform crystallization of large-area perovskite films and efficient charge extraction. Consequently, PSCs achieve a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 26.14% for 0.05 cm<sup>2</sup> devices, 23.27% for a 5 × 5 cm<sup>2</sup> mini-module, and 22.34% for a 30 × 30 cm<sup>2</sup> module (615.7 cm<sup>2</sup> active area). Notably, the large-area module retains 90% of its initial efficiency after 2000 h of continuous maximum power point operation, demonstrating both high performance and operational stability, highlighting a scalable route for reliable perovskite photovoltaics.</p> Zihan Gu, Kaiyu Wang, Ruigang Yan, Guang Yang, Xingyu Gao, Qingxun Guo, Yingdong Xia, Zhelu Hu, Shuhong Xie, Lingfeng Chao, Yonghua Chen RESEARCH ARTICLE 2D Perovskite Engineering Enables Robust Self‐Assembled Monolayers for High‐Performance Perovskite Solar Cells 10.1002/adma.73561 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73561 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73561?af=R RESEARCH ARTICLE https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73461?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 01:20:54 -0700 2026-06-02T01:20:54-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents 10.1002/adma.73461 Zwitterionic Polymers: Synthesis, Architectures, Properties, and Biomedical Applications Advanced Materials, EarlyView. This review is organized around the hierarchical “synthesis–structure–application” relationship and outlines the progressive evolution from synthetic strategies to polymer structures, material architectures, and biomedical functions. Building on this framework, it further proposes application‐driven design principles, in which specific functional demands guide the rational selection of architectures, synthesis methods, and zwitterionic polymer systems. ABSTRACT Zwitterionic polymers are a class of polymeric materials characterized by repeating units bearing equal numbers of positively and negatively charged groups. Their unique chemical structure confers a strong hydration effect via ionic solvation, leading to distinctive properties such as resistance to protein adsorption and cell adhesion, effective interfacial lubrication, enhanced drug stability, and antifreezing capability. Consequently, zwitterionic polymers have garnered significant attention in biomedical applications in recent years. This review summarizes zwitterionic polymers through an application‐oriented “synthesis–architecture–application” framework. We emphasize that their biomedical performance is not determined solely by types of zwitterionic polymers, but by the hierarchical relationship among synthetic strategy, chain‐level topology, material architecture, and application‐specific biological interactions. Conversely, application requirements guide the selection of material architecture, polymer topology, synthetic strategy, and zwitterionic polymers. From this perspective, synthetic routes are discussed as tools for controlling polymer topology and architecture, while biomedical applications are analyzed in terms of the material forms and interfacial properties they require. We further discuss the current translational status of zwitterionic polymers, their major clinical challenges, and potential solutions. This review provides an application‐oriented design roadmap for linking zwitterionic chemistry, polymer synthesis, material architecture, and biomedical translation. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/365dffc7-5d68-4bd2-b923-0c849b15e061/adma73461-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Zwitterionic Polymers: Synthesis, Architectures, Properties, and Biomedical Applications"/> <p>This review is organized around the hierarchical “synthesis–structure–application” relationship and outlines the progressive evolution from synthetic strategies to polymer structures, material architectures, and biomedical functions. Building on this framework, it further proposes application-driven design principles, in which specific functional demands guide the rational selection of architectures, synthesis methods, and zwitterionic polymer systems. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Zwitterionic polymers are a class of polymeric materials characterized by repeating units bearing equal numbers of positively and negatively charged groups. Their unique chemical structure confers a strong hydration effect via ionic solvation, leading to distinctive properties such as resistance to protein adsorption and cell adhesion, effective interfacial lubrication, enhanced drug stability, and antifreezing capability. Consequently, zwitterionic polymers have garnered significant attention in biomedical applications in recent years. This review summarizes zwitterionic polymers through an application-oriented “synthesis–architecture–application” framework. We emphasize that their biomedical performance is not determined solely by types of zwitterionic polymers, but by the hierarchical relationship among synthetic strategy, chain-level topology, material architecture, and application-specific biological interactions. Conversely, application requirements guide the selection of material architecture, polymer topology, synthetic strategy, and zwitterionic polymers. From this perspective, synthetic routes are discussed as tools for controlling polymer topology and architecture, while biomedical applications are analyzed in terms of the material forms and interfacial properties they require. We further discuss the current translational status of zwitterionic polymers, their major clinical challenges, and potential solutions. This review provides an application-oriented design roadmap for linking zwitterionic chemistry, polymer synthesis, material architecture, and biomedical translation.</p> Hongying Wang, Kuan Cheng, Hanqi Zheng, Xiawei Zhao, Xiao Han, Danyang Chen, Xu Tian, Jianwei Sun, Yuqing Cheng, Liping Lang, Siyu Bao, Yongmao Li, Wenguang Liu, Jianhai Yang REVIEW Zwitterionic Polymers: Synthesis, Architectures, Properties, and Biomedical Applications 10.1002/adma.73461 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73461 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73461?af=R REVIEW https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202600020?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 01:18:47 -0700 2026-06-02T01:18:47-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents 10.1002/adma.202600020 Weak In‐Plane Ferromagnetism and Electronic Nematicity in the Distorted Triple‐Q Magnetic Phase of Co1/3TaS2 Advanced Materials, EarlyView. Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$ hosts a triple‐Q noncoplanar antiferromagnetic state with coexisting Z3${\rm Z}_3$ electronic nematicity. We report rotational hysteresis observed in both magnetoresistance and magnetic torque, revealing strongly pinned in‐plane weak ferromagnetic moments in the triple‐Q phase and the magnetism‐driven nature of the co‐existing nematicity. These results demonstrate that the weak in‐plane ferromagnetic moment offers an additional handle on the spin‐driven electronic nematicity. ABSTRACT An intercalated transition‐metal dichalcogenide Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$ hosts a triple‐Q (3Q) noncoplanar antiferromagnetic state that co‐exists with Z3$Z_{3}$ electronic nematicity, indicating broken threefold rotational symmetry. This nematicity exhibits versatile field‐ and strain‐tunability, making it promising for spintronics applications. However, its microscopic connection to magnetism has remained unclear. Here, we report rotational hysteresis observed in both magnetoresistance and magnetic torque, revealing strongly pinned in‐plane weak ferromagnetic moments in the triple‐Q phase and the magnetism‐driven nature of the co‐existing nematicity. In particular, when fields are rotated within a narrow angular range to restrict magnetization reversal, we observe additional hysteresis loops. This hysteresis can be well explained by domain repopulation, further supported by our theoretical simulations based on the spin Hamiltonian of Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$. These results demonstrate that the weak in‐plane ferromagnetic moment offers an additional handle on the spin‐driven electronic nematicity, providing a microscopic picture of the field‐tunable electronic responses in the 3Q state of Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/b3635f2b-4874-49b3-98d7-9006ca8682f0/adma73538-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Weak In-Plane Ferromagnetism and Electronic Nematicity in the Distorted Triple-Q Magnetic Phase of Co1/3TaS2"/> <p>Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$ hosts a triple-Q noncoplanar antiferromagnetic state with coexisting Z3${\rm Z}_3$ electronic nematicity. We report rotational hysteresis observed in both magnetoresistance and magnetic torque, revealing strongly pinned in-plane weak ferromagnetic moments in the triple-Q phase and the magnetism-driven nature of the co-existing nematicity. These results demonstrate that the weak in-plane ferromagnetic moment offers an additional handle on the spin-driven electronic nematicity. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>An intercalated transition-metal dichalcogenide Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$ hosts a triple-<b>Q</b> (3<b>Q</b>) noncoplanar antiferromagnetic state that co-exists with Z3$Z_{3}$ electronic nematicity, indicating broken threefold rotational symmetry. This nematicity exhibits versatile field- and strain-tunability, making it promising for spintronics applications. However, its microscopic connection to magnetism has remained unclear. Here, we report rotational hysteresis observed in both magnetoresistance and magnetic torque, revealing strongly pinned in-plane weak ferromagnetic moments in the triple-<b>Q</b> phase and the magnetism-driven nature of the co-existing nematicity. In particular, when fields are rotated within a narrow angular range to restrict magnetization reversal, we observe additional hysteresis loops. This hysteresis can be well explained by domain repopulation, further supported by our theoretical simulations based on the spin Hamiltonian of Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$. These results demonstrate that the weak in-plane ferromagnetic moment offers an additional handle on the spin-driven electronic nematicity, providing a microscopic picture of the field-tunable electronic responses in the 3<b>Q</b> state of Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$.</p> Joonyoung Choi, Woonghee Cho, Pyeongjae Park, Woun Kang, Je‐Geun Park, Younjung Jo RESEARCH ARTICLE Weak In‐Plane Ferromagnetism and Electronic Nematicity in the Distorted Triple‐Q Magnetic Phase of Co1/3TaS2 10.1002/adma.202600020 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.202600020 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202600020?af=R RESEARCH ARTICLE https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73585?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 01:18:44 -0700 2026-06-02T01:18:44-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents 10.1002/adma.73585 Stable and Low‐Cost Organic Photovoltaics Without Conjugated Donors Advanced Materials, EarlyView. The bulk heterojunction based on low‑cost inorganic p‑type semiconductor CuSCN and organic n‐type non‐fullerene acceptor L8‑BO delivers a champion power conversion efficiency of 7.21%, together with excellent thermal stability and operational durability, offering a practical materials approach for high‑performance and stable organic photovoltaics, thereby advancing their potential for industrial‐scale application. ABSTRACT Despite significant advances in power conversion efficiency (PCE) exceeding 20%, organic photovoltaics (OPVs) face industrialization challenges due to inherent stability issues associated with conventional organic donors and hole transport layers. Here, we employ an inorganic p‐type semiconductor copper(I) thiocyanate (CuSCN) as a multifunctional component concurrently serving as donor and hole transport layer. Combined with the non‐fullerene acceptor L8‐BO, the bulk heterojunction device achieves a champion PCE of 7.21%. More importantly, the CuSCN‐based devices exhibit exceptional thermal stability, maintaining 80% of their initial efficiency (T80) for nearly 400 h at 85°C, far exceeding the T80 of 3 to 33 h for OPVs based on conventional all‐organic active layers. Moreover, under ISOS‐L‐3 protocol (maximum power point tracking under 100 mW cm−2 illumination, 65°C, 50% relative humidity), the devices exhibit outstanding operational durability, sustaining approximately 50% of their initial efficiency after 900 h, in stark contrast to the rapid degradation observed in D18:L8‐BO and PM6:L8‐BO reference systems, which retain only 15% and 19%, respectively. This work underscores the potential of CuSCN in enabling efficient, durable, and industrially viable OPVs. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/1050b733-e415-4761-a7ec-f92915844fd2/adma73585-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Stable and Low-Cost Organic Photovoltaics Without Conjugated Donors"/> <p>The bulk heterojunction based on low‑cost inorganic p‑type semiconductor CuSCN and organic n-type non-fullerene acceptor L8‑BO delivers a champion power conversion efficiency of 7.21%, together with excellent thermal stability and operational durability, offering a practical materials approach for high‑performance and stable organic photovoltaics, thereby advancing their potential for industrial-scale application. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Despite significant advances in power conversion efficiency (PCE) exceeding 20%, organic photovoltaics (OPVs) face industrialization challenges due to inherent stability issues associated with conventional organic donors and hole transport layers. Here, we employ an inorganic p-type semiconductor copper(I) thiocyanate (CuSCN) as a multifunctional component concurrently serving as donor and hole transport layer. Combined with the non-fullerene acceptor L8-BO, the bulk heterojunction device achieves a champion PCE of 7.21%. More importantly, the CuSCN-based devices exhibit exceptional thermal stability, maintaining 80% of their initial efficiency (<i>T</i> <sub>80</sub>) for nearly 400 h at 85°C, far exceeding the <i>T</i> <sub>80</sub> of 3 to 33 h for OPVs based on conventional all-organic active layers. Moreover, under ISOS-L-3 protocol (maximum power point tracking under 100 mW cm<sup>−2</sup> illumination, 65°C, 50% relative humidity), the devices exhibit outstanding operational durability, sustaining approximately 50% of their initial efficiency after 900 h, in stark contrast to the rapid degradation observed in D18:L8-BO and PM6:L8-BO reference systems, which retain only 15% and 19%, respectively. This work underscores the potential of CuSCN in enabling efficient, durable, and industrially viable OPVs.</p> Siru Guo, Jiayu Wang, Xue Yan, Jie Xiong, Hongxiang Li, Bowei Li, Yakun He, Junyi Li, Weilin Zhou, Yufei Gong, Hua Tang, Cenqi Yan, Jiaqiang Qin, Min Deng, Xiaopeng Xu, Lei Meng, Frédéric Laquai, Qiang Peng, Yongfang Li, Pei Cheng RESEARCH ARTICLE Stable and Low‐Cost Organic Photovoltaics Without Conjugated Donors 10.1002/adma.73585 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73585 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73585?af=R RESEARCH ARTICLE https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73599?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 01:18:03 -0700 2026-06-02T01:18:03-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents 10.1002/adma.73599 Energetic All‐Polymer Fiber Batteries Enabled by Interface‐Interlocked Water‐In‐Network Electrolytes for Wearable Electronics Advanced Materials, EarlyView. A “water‐in‐network” strategy that simultaneously addresses narrow electrochemical stability windows and interfacial instability. By modulating crosslinking density to engineer water confinement and employing in situ polymerization to construct mechanically interlocked interfaces that suppress water‐induced delamination, this approach yields an all‐polymer sodium‐ion fiber battery featuring high energy density and exceptional deformability for wearable applications. ABSTRACT All‐polymer batteries configured in a fibrous form by integrating polymeric electrodes with aqueous quasi‐solid polymer electrolytes (AQPEs) represent a promising solution for wearable electronics with safety and sustainability. However, their practical development is constrained by the narrow electrochemical stability window (ESW) of AQPEs and the structural instability of electrode–electrolyte interfaces under deformation. Here, we present a “water‐in‐network” (WIN) electrolyte that precisely engineers water activity and dynamics by modulating the crosslinking density of the polymer network. We unanticipatedly discover a distinct “water confinement” effect wherein water activity exhibits a non‐monotonic dependence on crosslinking density, originating from the structural evolution of the network. Benefiting from this mechanism, the ESW significantly expands to 3.4 V, endowing an all‐polymer sodium‐ion fiber battery with an energy density of 92.4 Wh kg−1. Crucially, in situ interfacial polymerization following electrolyte pre‐infiltration forms a mechanically interlocked electrode–electrolyte interface, effectively suppressing water‐induced delamination and preserving 78% capacity after 12 000 bending cycles.When seamlessly woven into a shirt, the fiber batteries can power the fabric‐based chemical sensor, enabling real‐time, on‐body health monitoring across various activities. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/9260c6ae-ec43-4168-9c45-4a93e49359bc/adma73599-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Energetic All-Polymer Fiber Batteries Enabled by Interface-Interlocked Water-In-Network Electrolytes for Wearable Electronics"/> <p>A “water-in-network” strategy that simultaneously addresses narrow electrochemical stability windows and interfacial instability. By modulating crosslinking density to engineer water confinement and employing in situ polymerization to construct mechanically interlocked interfaces that suppress water-induced delamination, this approach yields an all-polymer sodium-ion fiber battery featuring high energy density and exceptional deformability for wearable applications. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>All-polymer batteries configured in a fibrous form by integrating polymeric electrodes with aqueous quasi-solid polymer electrolytes (AQPEs) represent a promising solution for wearable electronics with safety and sustainability. However, their practical development is constrained by the narrow electrochemical stability window (ESW) of AQPEs and the structural instability of electrode–electrolyte interfaces under deformation. Here, we present a “water-in-network” (WIN) electrolyte that precisely engineers water activity and dynamics by modulating the crosslinking density of the polymer network. We unanticipatedly discover a distinct “water confinement” effect wherein water activity exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on crosslinking density, originating from the structural evolution of the network. Benefiting from this mechanism, the ESW significantly expands to 3.4 V, endowing an all-polymer sodium-ion fiber battery with an energy density of 92.4 Wh kg<sup>−1</sup>. Crucially, in situ interfacial polymerization following electrolyte pre-infiltration forms a mechanically interlocked electrode–electrolyte interface, effectively suppressing water-induced delamination and preserving 78% capacity after 12 000 bending cycles.When seamlessly woven into a shirt, the fiber batteries can power the fabric-based chemical sensor, enabling real-time, on-body health monitoring across various activities.</p> Kangkang Jia, Qimin Liang, Yang Hong, Jiahao Zhu, Jinze Wang, Xin Wang, Weijing Zuo, Jiajun Tan, Min Wang, Xiaohua Zhong, Longbin Qiu, Yan Huang, Ruhong Li, Xiulin Fan, Sisi He RESEARCH ARTICLE Energetic All‐Polymer Fiber Batteries Enabled by Interface‐Interlocked Water‐In‐Network Electrolytes for Wearable Electronics 10.1002/adma.73599 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73599 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73599?af=R RESEARCH ARTICLE https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73430?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700 2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 10.1002/adma.73430 Issue Information Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. Issue Information Issue Information 10.1002/adma.73430 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73430 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73430?af=R Issue Information 38 31 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73192?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700 2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 10.1002/adma.73192 Erratum to “Successive Orthorhombic Distortions in Kagome Metals by Molecular Orbital Formation” Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. ERRATUM Erratum to “Successive Orthorhombic Distortions in Kagome Metals by Molecular Orbital Formation” 10.1002/adma.73192 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73192 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73192?af=R ERRATUM 38 31 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73425?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700 2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 10.1002/adma.73425 Hydrogelation via Supramolecular Copolymerization of Structural Water within Adaptive Metal–Organic Fibers (Adv. Mater. 31/2026) Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. Metal–Organic Fibers In their Research Article (DOI: 10.1002/adma.202519933), Merlin R. Stühler, Alex J. Plajer, and co‐workers report metal–organic fibres in which confined structural water acts as a co‐monomer to drive supramolecular polymerisation. These tubular assemblies form thermoresponsive hydrogels and enable selective chiral recognition via water‐mediated information transfer, establishing structural water as a functional design element in adaptive supramolecular materials. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/d0838232-a55f-446b-9213-241f5f735a16/adma73425-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Hydrogelation via Supramolecular Copolymerization of Structural Water within Adaptive Metal–Organic Fibers (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)"/> <p><b>Metal–Organic Fibers</b></p> <p>In their Research Article (DOI: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202519933">10.1002/adma.202519933</a>), Merlin R. Stühler, Alex J. Plajer, and co-workers report metal–organic fibres in which confined structural water acts as a co-monomer to drive supramolecular polymerisation. These tubular assemblies form thermoresponsive hydrogels and enable selective chiral recognition via water-mediated information transfer, establishing structural water as a functional design element in adaptive supramolecular materials. </p> <br/> Merlin R. Stühler, Hesam Makki, Tarek Hilal, Debsena Chakraboty, Mathias Dimde, Kai Ludwig, Rainer Haag, Sabine Rosenfeldt, Dorothee Silbernagl, Andreas Schäfer, Alex J. Plajer Cover Picture Hydrogelation via Supramolecular Copolymerization of Structural Water within Adaptive Metal–Organic Fibers (Adv. Mater. 31/2026) 10.1002/adma.73425 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73425 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73425?af=R Cover Picture 38 31 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73427?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700 2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 10.1002/adma.73427 Synaptic Functionality and Neuromorphic Information Processing in Membrane Ion Channel Junctions (Adv. Mater. 31/2026) Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. Membrane Ion Channel Synapses In their Research Article (DOI: 10.1002/adma.202519525), Aleksandr Noy and co‐workers report membrane ion channel synapses (MICS), artificial ionic synapses based on droplet interface junctions doped with biological ion channel Gramicidin A. MICS exhibits robust memristive behavior and synaptic plasticity and enables neuromorphic applications including associative learning and reservoir computing. In one application, researchers used MICS to play a board game of tic‐tac‐toe against an ideal opponent. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/67844c16-cbf5-4166-afdb-0fc2298dcda0/adma73427-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Synaptic Functionality and Neuromorphic Information Processing in Membrane Ion Channel Junctions (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)"/> <p><b>Membrane Ion Channel Synapses</b></p> <p>In their Research Article (DOI: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202519525">10.1002/adma.202519525</a>), Aleksandr Noy and co-workers report membrane ion channel synapses (MICS), artificial ionic synapses based on droplet interface junctions doped with biological ion channel Gramicidin A. MICS exhibits robust memristive behavior and synaptic plasticity and enables neuromorphic applications including associative learning and reservoir computing. In one application, researchers used MICS to play a board game of tic-tac-toe against an ideal opponent. </p> <br/> Zhongwu Li, Jiachen Feng, Jingyi Xiao, Anton Leuski, Aleksandr Noy Inside Back Cover Synaptic Functionality and Neuromorphic Information Processing in Membrane Ion Channel Junctions (Adv. Mater. 31/2026) 10.1002/adma.73427 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73427 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73427?af=R Inside Back Cover 38 31 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73428?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700 2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 10.1002/adma.73428 Flexible Multiband Photonic Synapses for Nociceptive Perception and Neuromorphic Computation via Fluorinated InP Quantum Dots (Adv. Mater. 31/2026) Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. Flexible InP Quantum Dot Photonic Synaptic Transistors Bio‐inspired visual perception is realized using flexible organic photonic synaptic transistors based on fluorinated InP quantum dots. The cover illustrates the coupling of biological vision and artificial synapses, enabling efficient damage perception, information processing, and neuromorphic computation. More details can be found in the Research Article by Yang Li and co‐workers (DOI: 10.1002/adma.202518057). <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/0b0b8f21-9518-42ec-8bdc-77605f7e513a/adma73428-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Flexible Multiband Photonic Synapses for Nociceptive Perception and Neuromorphic Computation via Fluorinated InP Quantum Dots (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)"/> <p><b>Flexible InP Quantum Dot Photonic Synaptic Transistors</b></p> <p>Bio-inspired visual perception is realized using flexible organic photonic synaptic transistors based on fluorinated InP quantum dots. The cover illustrates the coupling of biological vision and artificial synapses, enabling efficient damage perception, information processing, and neuromorphic computation. More details can be found in the Research Article by Yang Li and co-workers (DOI: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202518057">10.1002/adma.202518057</a>). </p> <br/> Wanlong Lu, Peixian Li, Bin Zeng, Jing Wang, Ning Dai, Yang Li Inside Front Cover Flexible Multiband Photonic Synapses for Nociceptive Perception and Neuromorphic Computation via Fluorinated InP Quantum Dots (Adv. Mater. 31/2026) 10.1002/adma.73428 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73428 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73428?af=R Inside Front Cover 38 31 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73432?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700 2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 10.1002/adma.73432 Beyond Earth: Resilience of Quasi‐2D Perovskite Solar Cells in Space (Adv. Mater. 31/2026) Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. Perovskite Solar Cells in Low Earth Orbit In the Research Article (DOI: 10.1002/adma.202520433), Christoph Putz, Martin Kaltenbrunner, and co‐workers demonstrate rigid quasi‐2D perovskite solar cells delivering stable power for over 100 days under real space conditions. Correlated in‐orbit data and extensive ground‐based thermal and proton‐irradiation studies reveal high intrinsic radiation tolerance, substrate‐driven losses, and key challenges toward lightweight perovskite space photovoltaics. The illustration depicts a perovskite‐structured satellite in low Earth orbit, symbolizing the deployment of quasi‐2D perovskite solar cells for space‐based power generation. Cover art by Florian Loimayr. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/93cd8c90-e03f-4907-9512-53d377085008/adma73432-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Beyond Earth: Resilience of Quasi-2D Perovskite Solar Cells in Space (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)"/> <p><b>Perovskite Solar Cells in Low Earth Orbit</b></p> <p>In the Research Article (DOI: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202520433">10.1002/adma.202520433</a>), Christoph Putz, Martin Kaltenbrunner, and co-workers demonstrate rigid quasi-2D perovskite solar cells delivering stable power for over 100 days under real space conditions. Correlated in-orbit data and extensive ground-based thermal and proton-irradiation studies reveal high intrinsic radiation tolerance, substrate-driven losses, and key challenges toward lightweight perovskite space photovoltaics. The illustration depicts a perovskite-structured satellite in low Earth orbit, symbolizing the deployment of quasi-2D perovskite solar cells for space-based power generation. Cover art by Florian Loimayr. </p> <br/> Christoph Putz, Lukas E. Lehner, Stepan Demchyshyn, Bekele Hailegnaw, Phillip Jahelka, Magdalena Breitwieser, Sercan Özen, Andrea Denker, Jürgen Bundesmann, Alina Hanna Dittwald, Dilara Karabulut, Philipp Tockhorn, Steve Albrecht, Felix Lang, Markus C. Scharber, Michael D. Kelzenberg, Harry A. Atwater, Martin Kaltenbrunner Back Cover Beyond Earth: Resilience of Quasi‐2D Perovskite Solar Cells in Space (Adv. Mater. 31/2026) 10.1002/adma.73432 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73432 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73432?af=R Back Cover 38 31 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73433?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700 2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 10.1002/adma.73433 3D‐Printable, Honeycomb‐Inspired Tissue‐Like Bioelectrodes for Patient‐Specific Neural Interface (Adv. Mater. 31/2026) Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. Patient‐Specific HiPGE This image illustrates a first‐of‐its‐kind soft neural interface inspired by the honeycomb architecture found in nature. The honeycomb‐inspired, patient‐specific design enables a neural interface that combines tissue‐like softness with mechanical robustness, advancing next‐generation brain‐computer interfaces and neuromodulation therapies. More details can be found in the Research Article by Tao Zhou, Nanyin Zhang, Sulin Zhang, and co‐workers (DOI: 10.1002/adma.202516291). <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/2a9e708a-6b8a-47a7-b17a-751243a0a607/adma73433-gra-0001-m.png" alt="3D-Printable, Honeycomb-Inspired Tissue-Like Bioelectrodes for Patient-Specific Neural Interface (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)"/> <p><b>Patient-Specific HiPGE</b></p> <p>This image illustrates a first-of-its-kind soft neural interface inspired by the honeycomb architecture found in nature. The honeycomb-inspired, patient-specific design enables a neural interface that combines tissue-like softness with mechanical robustness, advancing next-generation brain-computer interfaces and neuromodulation therapies. More details can be found in the Research Article by Tao Zhou, Nanyin Zhang, Sulin Zhang, and co-workers (DOI: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202516291">10.1002/adma.202516291</a>). </p> <br/> Marzia Momin, Luyi Feng, Xiaoai Chen, Salahuddin Ahmed, Basma AlMahmood, Li‐Pang Huang, Jiashu Ren, Xinyi Wang, Hyunjin Lee, Samuel R. Cramer, Nanyin Zhang, Sulin Zhang, Tao Zhou Frontispiece 3D‐Printable, Honeycomb‐Inspired Tissue‐Like Bioelectrodes for Patient‐Specific Neural Interface (Adv. Mater. 31/2026) 10.1002/adma.73433 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.73433 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73433?af=R Frontispiece 38 31 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202522834?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700 2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 10.1002/adma.202522834 Recent Advances in Electrospun Nanofibers for Triboelectric Nanogenerators: Performance Enhancement Strategies and Emerging Applications Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. This review highlights recent advances in electrospun nanofiber‐based triboelectric nanogenerators, emphasizing how material design, fiber architecture, and interface engineering collectively enhance output performance and mechanical durability. Key developments in porous, aligned, core‐shell, and hierarchical nanofibers are discussed alongside emerging applications in wearable electronics, biomedical systems, and self‐powered IoT. Current challenges and future opportunities for scalable, sustainable, and high‐performance energy harvesting are also outlined. ABSTRACT In the quest for next‐generation sustainable energy systems, triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) have emerged as a pivotal technology for converting mechanical energy into electrical power, especially at the micro and nanoscale. Among various structural platforms, fibrous materials, particularly those produced via electrospinning, have demonstrated exceptional potential for TENG development due to their tunable morphology, lightweight nature, mechanical flexibility, and large surface‐to‐volume ratio. These characteristics are critical for enhancing the interfacial charge generation and transfer that underpin triboelectric performance. This review offers a comprehensive examination of the latest advances in fibrous materials for TENGs, with a particular focus on material innovations, structural design strategies, and performance optimization techniques. Emphasis is placed on the role of hybrid nanocomposites, core–shell configurations, surface functionalization, and alignment control in maximizing electrical output and operational durability. Additionally, the paper surveys emerging application areas such as wearable electronics, self‐powered sensors, smart textiles, biomedical monitoring, and human‐machine interaction systems. By bridging fundamental material science with practical design paradigms, this review identifies critical bottlenecks and lays out future research directions toward scalable, efficient, and environmentally friendly fibrous TENG technologies. <img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/df6fe6eb-7d11-4edc-84fe-4b696a47e5e3/adma73144-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Recent Advances in Electrospun Nanofibers for Triboelectric Nanogenerators: Performance Enhancement Strategies and Emerging Applications"/> <p>This review highlights recent advances in electrospun nanofiber-based triboelectric nanogenerators, emphasizing how material design, fiber architecture, and interface engineering collectively enhance output performance and mechanical durability. Key developments in porous, aligned, core-shell, and hierarchical nanofibers are discussed alongside emerging applications in wearable electronics, biomedical systems, and self-powered IoT. Current challenges and future opportunities for scalable, sustainable, and high-performance energy harvesting are also outlined. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>In the quest for next-generation sustainable energy systems, triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) have emerged as a pivotal technology for converting mechanical energy into electrical power, especially at the micro and nanoscale. Among various structural platforms, fibrous materials, particularly those produced via electrospinning, have demonstrated exceptional potential for TENG development due to their tunable morphology, lightweight nature, mechanical flexibility, and large surface-to-volume ratio. These characteristics are critical for enhancing the interfacial charge generation and transfer that underpin triboelectric performance. This review offers a comprehensive examination of the latest advances in fibrous materials for TENGs, with a particular focus on material innovations, structural design strategies, and performance optimization techniques. Emphasis is placed on the role of hybrid nanocomposites, core–shell configurations, surface functionalization, and alignment control in maximizing electrical output and operational durability. Additionally, the paper surveys emerging application areas such as wearable electronics, self-powered sensors, smart textiles, biomedical monitoring, and human-machine interaction systems. By bridging fundamental material science with practical design paradigms, this review identifies critical bottlenecks and lays out future research directions toward scalable, efficient, and environmentally friendly fibrous TENG technologies.</p> MD Fajla Rabbi, Jee Hwan Ahn, Duy Linh Vu, Kyoung Kwan Ahn REVIEW Recent Advances in Electrospun Nanofibers for Triboelectric Nanogenerators: Performance Enhancement Strategies and Emerging Applications 10.1002/adma.202522834 Advanced Materials 10.1002/adma.202522834 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202522834?af=R REVIEW 38 31 https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202523422?af=R Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700 2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00 Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700 10.1002/adma.202523422 From Bio‐Interface Materials to Neural Integration: The Next‐Generation Brain–Machine Interfaces Powered by Hydrogels Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. This review summarizes recent advances in hydrogel‐based brain‐machine interfaces (BMIs), with a focus on key material attributes such as mechanical compliance, adhesion, conductivity, and biocompatibility, which address the limitations of rigid metal electrodes. We classify BMIs into non‐invasive and invasive systems based on their neural access modality and describe their fundamental operating principles. Additionally, we discuss emerging applications in neurological disorders and neurorehabilitation, and outline current challenges and future strategies aimed at achieving high‐fidelity, long‐term stability, and clinical translation of hydrogel‐based neural interfaces. ABSTRACT Brain–machine interfaces (BMIs), which serve as revolutionary tools for neural recording, modulation, and rehabilitation, are highly dependent on the biocompatibility and mechanical suitability of their electrode materials. Although traditional metal electrodes possess excellent conductivity, their inherent rigidity causes a substantial mechanical mismatch with soft neural tissue, leading to chronic inflammatory responses and poor long‐term stability. The emergence of hydrogel electrodes has provided a breakthrough solution to this fundamental limitation. Hydrogels, characterized by their softness, high ionic conductivity, and tissue‐like compliance, offer a viable solution to mitigate these issues. This review systematically explores the material properties of hydrogel‐integrated BMIs, providing an in‐depth investigation of key hydrogel characteristics, including toughness, adhesion, conductivity, and biocompatibility. Additionally, hydrogel‐based BMIs are categorized into non‐invasive and invasive systems, each defined by its characteristic operational principles and signal‐acquisition mechanisms. The study further reviews critical issues, including surgical implantation strategies, multimodal data fusion, integration of artificial intelligence, as well as system integration and clinical translation. From a therapeutic perspective, this work highlights the application of BMIs in treating neurological disorders such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, epilepsy, stroke, neuropathic pain, and depression.
