<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73624?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Thu, 04 Jun 2026 23:40:45 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-04T11:40:45-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73624</guid>
<title>Ratiometric Mycotoxin Detection in Living Plants With Dual‐Emissive Nanosensors</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> A minimally invasive microneedle patch integrates carbon dot‐embedded metal–organic frameworks as nanosensors to detect a key fungal toxin in living plants. The nanosensor produces a ratiometric fluorescence signal that enables early, non‐destructive diagnosis of fungal infection before visible symptoms, offering a new biomaterials‐based strategy for real‐time plant health monitoring and precision agriculture. ABSTRACT Early diagnosis of fungal infections in crops is essential for mitigating yield losses, yet most detection strategies rely on destructive assays that are incompatible with in situ monitoring. Among major fungal pathogens, Fusarium species pose a significant threat to global agriculture and produce the phytotoxin fusaric acid (FA), a key biomarker of early infection. Here, we develop a non‐destructive, ratiometric optical nanosensor that integrates aggregation‐induced emissive carbon dots within a zeolitic imidazolate framework (CD@ZIF‐8) for selective FA detection in living plants. By rationally tuning the balance between dispersed and aggregated CDs within the ZIF‐8 framework, we generated a dual‐emissive nanosensor with distinct blue and red fluorescent signals that enable self‐referencing readouts. Upon exposure to FA, interactions with CD@ZIF‐8 quench the blue emission while leaving the red aggregation‐induced emission stable, thereby affording highly selective ratiometric sensing. Integrating CD@ZIF‐8 into plant‐compatible microneedle patches enables minimally invasive sampling of plant interstitial fluid, allowing FA detection in Fusarium‐infected crops before visible symptoms and discrimination from abiotic and bacterial stresses. This work establishes an integrated, self‐referenced nanosensing platform for non‐destructive plant diagnostics, laying the foundation for early disease surveillance in precision and climate‐resilient agriculture. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/af4e5102-1385-4228-a7f0-3a10b3eaaa6b/adma73624-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Ratiometric Mycotoxin Detection in Living Plants With Dual-Emissive Nanosensors"/> <p>A minimally invasive microneedle patch integrates carbon dot-embedded metal–organic frameworks as nanosensors to detect a key fungal toxin in living plants. The nanosensor produces a ratiometric fluorescence signal that enables early, non-destructive diagnosis of fungal infection before visible symptoms, offering a new biomaterials-based strategy for real-time plant health monitoring and precision agriculture. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Early diagnosis of fungal infections in crops is essential for mitigating yield losses, yet most detection strategies rely on destructive assays that are incompatible with in situ monitoring. Among major fungal pathogens, <i>Fusarium</i> species pose a significant threat to global agriculture and produce the phytotoxin fusaric acid (FA), a key biomarker of early infection. Here, we develop a non-destructive, ratiometric optical nanosensor that integrates aggregation-induced emissive carbon dots within a zeolitic imidazolate framework (CD@ZIF-8) for selective FA detection in living plants. By rationally tuning the balance between dispersed and aggregated CDs within the ZIF-8 framework, we generated a dual-emissive nanosensor with distinct blue and red fluorescent signals that enable self-referencing readouts. Upon exposure to FA, interactions with CD@ZIF-8 quench the blue emission while leaving the red aggregation-induced emission stable, thereby affording highly selective ratiometric sensing. Integrating CD@ZIF-8 into plant-compatible microneedle patches enables minimally invasive sampling of plant interstitial fluid, allowing FA detection in <i>Fusarium</i>-infected crops before visible symptoms and discrimination from abiotic and bacterial stresses. This work establishes an integrated, self-referenced nanosensing platform for non-destructive plant diagnostics, laying the foundation for early disease surveillance in precision and climate-resilient agriculture.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Yuliang Li, Di Shen, Ziyang Huang, Suppanat Puangpathumanond, Calvin Thenarianto, Yue Zhao, Jolly Madathiparambil Saju, Rajani Sarojam, Dongmei Zhou, Tedrick Thomas Salim Lew </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Ratiometric Mycotoxin Detection in Living Plants With Dual‐Emissive Nanosensors</dc:title>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73559?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Thu, 04 Jun 2026 23:40:04 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-04T11:40:04-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73559</guid>
<title>Mercaptoimidazole‐Engineered Microenvironment Enables Durable CO2 Electroreduction in a Zero‐Gap PEM Electrolyzer</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Mercaptoimidazole ligands create a proton‐shielding alkaline microenvironment on a lead‐based catalyst for CO2 reduction in a zero‐gap PEM electrolyzer. This design resolves the trade‐off between salt precipitation and hydrogen evolution in PEM electrolysis, enabling stable and selective CO2‐to‐formate conversion. ABSTRACT CO2 conversion in proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolysis systems offers a sustainable pathway for chemical production by eliminating carbonate formation; however, it faces a trade‐off between suppressing the hydrogen evolution reaction and preventing salt precipitation. Here, we resolve this paradox through a molecular‐level engineering strategy by anchoring a mercaptoimidazole ligand on lead‐based catalyst. Operando spectroscopic analyses and theoretical studies reveal that this ligand shell creates a local alkaline microenvironment and establishes a proton‐shielding effect at the catalyst surface. When integrated into a zero‐gap PEM electrolyzer, the catalyst achieves a peak formate Faradaic efficiency of 95.8% and sustains over 90% selectivity at a current density of 600 mA cm−2. This performance persists under strongly acidic (pH 1.0) and cation‐starved (0.001 M) conditions. The PEM system delivers extended stability, with over 300 h of continuous operation at industrially relevant current densities. Our work establishes a design strategy that decouples the catalytic microenvironment from the bulk electrolyte and provides a route for durable and selective acidic CO2 electrolyzers. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/aa631673-5d73-4d7c-8911-3d9d096c4179/adma73559-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Mercaptoimidazole-Engineered Microenvironment Enables Durable CO2 Electroreduction in a Zero-Gap PEM Electrolyzer"/> <p>Mercaptoimidazole ligands create a proton-shielding alkaline microenvironment on a lead-based catalyst for CO<sub>2</sub> reduction in a zero-gap PEM electrolyzer. This design resolves the trade-off between salt precipitation and hydrogen evolution in PEM electrolysis, enabling stable and selective CO<sub>2</sub>-to-formate conversion. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>CO<sub>2</sub> conversion in proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolysis systems offers a sustainable pathway for chemical production by eliminating carbonate formation; however, it faces a trade-off between suppressing the hydrogen evolution reaction and preventing salt precipitation. Here, we resolve this paradox through a molecular-level engineering strategy by anchoring a mercaptoimidazole ligand on lead-based catalyst. <i>Operando</i> spectroscopic analyses and theoretical studies reveal that this ligand shell creates a local alkaline microenvironment and establishes a proton-shielding effect at the catalyst surface. When integrated into a zero-gap PEM electrolyzer, the catalyst achieves a peak formate Faradaic efficiency of 95.8% and sustains over 90% selectivity at a current density of 600 mA cm<sup>−2</sup>. This performance persists under strongly acidic (pH 1.0) and cation-starved (0.001 <span class="smallCaps">M</span>) conditions. The PEM system delivers extended stability, with over 300 h of continuous operation at industrially relevant current densities. Our work establishes a design strategy that decouples the catalytic microenvironment from the bulk electrolyte and provides a route for durable and selective acidic CO<sub>2</sub> electrolyzers.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Jia Chen Wu, Tingting Yu, Jianming Gu, Huai Qin Fu, Ziwei Ye, Hai Yang Yuan, Cheng Lian, Huiliang Cao, Hua Gui Yang, Peng Fei Liu </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Mercaptoimidazole‐Engineered Microenvironment Enables Durable CO2 Electroreduction in a Zero‐Gap PEM Electrolyzer</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73559</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73611?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Thu, 04 Jun 2026 06:27:03 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-04T06:27:03-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73611</guid>
<title>Aminotetrazole–Gold Nanocomplexes Shunt Electrons to Eradicate Bacteria Without ROS‐Induced Resistance</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> ATZ‐Au are electron‐shunting gold nanocomplexes that shunt electrons from NADH/FADH2 at upstream bacterial ETC nodes, inhibit ROS production, disrupt PMF, and deplete cellular reducing power, ultimately causing bacterial death via acidosis and energy exhaustion. This ROS‐independent mechanism circumvents ROS‐mediated resistance evolution with broad implications for clinical anti‐infective therapy. ABSTRACT Most bactericides directly or indirectly disrupt the electron transport chain (ETC)‐mediated energy metabolism to exert antibacterial effects. However, the consequent perturbation of electron transfer states (e.g., flux overload or blockade) leads to electron accumulation, which enhances reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. High ROS concentrations kill bacteria, while sublethal levels induce DNA damage, thereby elevating antibiotic resistance risk. In this study, we found that shunting electrons at critical ETC nodes is able to disrupt bacterial energy metabolism, damaging bacteria without ROS generation. To leverage the ROS‐free mechanism for infection control, we synthesized 5‐aminotetrazole‐functionalized gold nanocomplexes (ATZ‐Au) that intercept electron transfer from electron donors to downstream carriers. This rapidly depletes reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH), disrupts the proton motive force (PMF), impairs ATP synthesis, and blocks bacterial compensatory metabolic pathways, ultimately leading to lethal intracellular acidosis and metabolic collapse. This effect blocks the capture of electrons by oxygen, thereby preventing the generation of ROS. The ATZ‐Au efficiently eliminates pathogenic bacteria in complex infections, providing a promising strategy to address antibiotic resistance. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/63afe453-9195-41c2-a491-06ca13ef974c/adma73611-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Aminotetrazole–Gold Nanocomplexes Shunt Electrons to Eradicate Bacteria Without ROS-Induced Resistance"/> <p>ATZ-Au are electron-shunting gold nanocomplexes that shunt electrons from NADH/FADH<sub>2</sub> at upstream bacterial ETC nodes, inhibit ROS production, disrupt PMF, and deplete cellular reducing power, ultimately causing bacterial death via acidosis and energy exhaustion. This ROS-independent mechanism circumvents ROS-mediated resistance evolution with broad implications for clinical anti-infective therapy. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Most bactericides directly or indirectly disrupt the electron transport chain (ETC)-mediated energy metabolism to exert antibacterial effects. However, the consequent perturbation of electron transfer states (e.g., flux overload or blockade) leads to electron accumulation, which enhances reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. High ROS concentrations kill bacteria, while sublethal levels induce DNA damage, thereby elevating antibiotic resistance risk. In this study, we found that shunting electrons at critical ETC nodes is able to disrupt bacterial energy metabolism, damaging bacteria without ROS generation. To leverage the ROS-free mechanism for infection control, we synthesized 5-aminotetrazole-functionalized gold nanocomplexes (ATZ-Au) that intercept electron transfer from electron donors to downstream carriers. This rapidly depletes reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH), disrupts the proton motive force (PMF), impairs ATP synthesis, and blocks bacterial compensatory metabolic pathways, ultimately leading to lethal intracellular acidosis and metabolic collapse. This effect blocks the capture of electrons by oxygen, thereby preventing the generation of ROS. The ATZ-Au efficiently eliminates pathogenic bacteria in complex infections, providing a promising strategy to address antibiotic resistance.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Mingzhi Huang, Qingqing Li, Zhuangzhuang Sun, Jiayi Liu, Zhiming Hou, Rongbing Tang </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Aminotetrazole–Gold Nanocomplexes Shunt Electrons to Eradicate Bacteria Without ROS‐Induced Resistance</dc:title>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73609?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Thu, 04 Jun 2026 03:32:26 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-04T03:32:26-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73609</guid>
<title>Shape‐Recognition in an Al‐MOF Enables High C3F6 Adsorption and Size‐Sieving Separation of C3F6/C3F8</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> An Al‐MOF (HIAM‐344) with narrow windows and large cavities achieves high‐capacity size‐sieving separation of C3F8/C3F6 and directly produces C3F8 (> 99.999%). ABSTRACT The purification of perfluoropropane (C3F8) relies on the selective removal of trace perfluoropropylene (C3F6), which remains challenging due to their similar physicochemical properties. Herein, we report a rigid Al‐based MOF, HIAM‐344, featuring nitrate‐regulated narrow pore windows and large cavities that enable simultaneous molecular sieving of C3F6/C3F8 and high C3F6 adsorption at low pressure. HIAM‐344 exhibits a record‐high C3F6 uptake of 78.8 cm3 g−1 at 298 K and 1 bar, and maintains a remarkably high capacity of 59.2 cm3 g−1 at a low pressure of 0.01 bar. Dynamic breakthrough experiments demonstrate complete splitting of C3F6 and C3F8, and direct production of electronic‐grade C3F8 (>99.999%). Computational studies reveal that the outstanding separation performance originates from the synergistic size and shape recognition, as well as the favorable adsorbate‐adsorbent interactions. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/a08b8ef0-4496-4e1f-bbc4-9220bb0e192c/adma73609-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Shape-Recognition in an Al-MOF Enables High C3F6 Adsorption and Size-Sieving Separation of C3F6/C3F8"/> <p>An Al-MOF (HIAM-344) with narrow windows and large cavities achieves high-capacity size-sieving separation of C<sub>3</sub>F<sub>8</sub>/C<sub>3</sub>F<sub>6</sub> and directly produces C<sub>3</sub>F<sub>8</sub> (&gt; 99.999%). </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>The purification of perfluoropropane (C<sub>3</sub>F<sub>8</sub>) relies on the selective removal of trace perfluoropropylene (C<sub>3</sub>F<sub>6</sub>), which remains challenging due to their similar physicochemical properties. Herein, we report a rigid Al-based MOF, HIAM-344, featuring nitrate-regulated narrow pore windows and large cavities that enable simultaneous molecular sieving of C<sub>3</sub>F<sub>6</sub>/C<sub>3</sub>F<sub>8</sub> and high C<sub>3</sub>F<sub>6</sub> adsorption at low pressure. HIAM-344 exhibits a record-high C<sub>3</sub>F<sub>6</sub> uptake of 78.8 cm<sup>3</sup> g<sup>−1</sup> at 298 K and 1 bar, and maintains a remarkably high capacity of 59.2 cm<sup>3</sup> g<sup>−1</sup> at a low pressure of 0.01 bar. Dynamic breakthrough experiments demonstrate complete splitting of C<sub>3</sub>F<sub>6</sub> and C<sub>3</sub>F<sub>8</sub>, and direct production of electronic-grade C<sub>3</sub>F<sub>8</sub> (&gt;99.999%). Computational studies reveal that the outstanding separation performance originates from the synergistic size and shape recognition, as well as the favorable adsorbate-adsorbent interactions.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Xin Zhou, Xuxuan Su, Shenfang Li, Muyang Zhou, Jinze Yao, Yilu Wu, Liang Yu, Kecai Xiong, Qibin Xia, Hao Wang </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Shape‐Recognition in an Al‐MOF Enables High C3F6 Adsorption and Size‐Sieving Separation of C3F6/C3F8</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73609</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73609</prism:doi>

<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73572?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Thu, 04 Jun 2026 00:46:37 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-04T12:46:37-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73572</guid>
<title>Ultrasound‐Assisted Zwitterion Grafting on NiOx for Suppressing Self‐Assembled Monolayer Migration in Perovskite Solar Cells</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> A novel ultrasonic strategy functionalizes NiOx with PIPES, improving conductivity and SAM assembly. The integrated NiOx+PIPES/4PABCz layer enhances charge transport, achieving 27.03% efficiency (26.47% certified). The devices also exhibit exceptional operational and thermal stability under harsh aging conditions. ABSTRACT Self‐assembled monolayers (SAMs) are widely used as hole‐selective materials in inverted perovskite solar cells (PSCs), yet their performance and stability are often limited by poor molecular ordering and interfacial incompatibility. Here, we present a novel ultrasonic chemical strategy to functionalize NiOx nanoparticles via anchoring the piperazine‐1,4‐bisethanesulfonic acid (PIPES) zwitterionic molecule. Ultrasonic cavitation generates hydroxyl radicals that oxidize Ni2+ to Ni3+, enhancing the electrical conductivity and hole mobility of NiOx. The exposed sulfonate groups of PIPES further promote the uniform assembly and hydrogen bonding anchoring of the subsequent SAM layer [(4‐(9'‐phenyl‐9H,9'H‐[3,3'‐bicarbazole]‐9‐yl)butyl)phosphonic acid, 4PABCz]. This integrated NiOx+PIPES/4PABCz hole transport layer fosters stronger dipole formation and interfacial polarization, facilitating charge separation and transport. Consequently, the optimized devices achieve a champion power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 27.03% (with a certified steady‐state efficiency of 26.47%). Remarkably, the devices exhibit exceptional operational and thermal stability, retaining 88.2% of their initial PCE after 1000 h of continuous illumination at 85°C and 91.0% after 1200 h of thermal aging at 85°C. This work introduces a robust and effective NiOx modification strategy, providing profound insights into interfacial design for high‐performance, stable inverted PSCs. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/da802bde-9a4d-48fb-ade8-5f57a828c67b/adma73572-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Ultrasound-Assisted Zwitterion Grafting on NiOx for Suppressing Self-Assembled Monolayer Migration in Perovskite Solar Cells"/> <p>A novel ultrasonic strategy functionalizes NiO<sub>x</sub> with PIPES, improving conductivity and SAM assembly. The integrated NiO<sub>x</sub>+PIPES/4PABCz layer enhances charge transport, achieving 27.03% efficiency (26.47% certified). The devices also exhibit exceptional operational and thermal stability under harsh aging conditions. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) are widely used as hole-selective materials in inverted perovskite solar cells (PSCs), yet their performance and stability are often limited by poor molecular ordering and interfacial incompatibility. Here, we present a novel ultrasonic chemical strategy to functionalize NiO<sub>x</sub> nanoparticles via anchoring the piperazine-1,4-bisethanesulfonic acid (PIPES) zwitterionic molecule. Ultrasonic cavitation generates hydroxyl radicals that oxidize Ni<sup>2</sup> <sup>+</sup> to Ni<sup>3</sup> <sup>+</sup>, enhancing the electrical conductivity and hole mobility of NiO<sub>x</sub>. The exposed sulfonate groups of PIPES further promote the uniform assembly and hydrogen bonding anchoring of the subsequent SAM layer [(4-(9'-phenyl-9H,9'H-[3,3'-bicarbazole]-9-yl)butyl)phosphonic acid, 4PABCz]. This integrated NiO<sub>x</sub>+PIPES/4PABCz hole transport layer fosters stronger dipole formation and interfacial polarization, facilitating charge separation and transport. Consequently, the optimized devices achieve a champion power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 27.03% (with a certified steady-state efficiency of 26.47%). Remarkably, the devices exhibit exceptional operational and thermal stability, retaining 88.2% of their initial PCE after 1000 h of continuous illumination at 85°C and 91.0% after 1200 h of thermal aging at 85°C. This work introduces a robust and effective NiO<sub>x</sub> modification strategy, providing profound insights into interfacial design for high-performance, stable inverted PSCs.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Qi Cao, Jianjun Mei, Zhi Wan, Yan Wang, Jiajun Song, Tao Du, Zhihao Li, Cong Chen, Xuanhua Li, Feng Yan </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Ultrasound‐Assisted Zwitterion Grafting on NiOx for Suppressing Self‐Assembled Monolayer Migration in Perovskite Solar Cells</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73572</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>

<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73493?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Thu, 04 Jun 2026 00:45:48 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-04T12:45:48-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73493</guid>
<title>Advances in Magnesium‐Based Thermoelectrics: A Critical Review</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Magnesium‐based thermoelectric materials have emerged as promising candidates for low‐to‐mid‐temperature energy conversion due to their abundance, low cost, and competitive performance. This review summarizes recent advances in Mg3X2, MgAgSb, and Mg2X systems, covering transport mechanisms, fabrication strategies, stability challenges, and device integration, and emphasizes a shift from material optimization toward integrated material‐device‐system co‐design. ABSTRACT In recent years, magnesium‐based thermoelectric materials have emerged as a key research direction toward large‐scale applications, owing to their elemental abundance, low material cost, good environmental compatibility, and competitive thermoelectric performance in the low‐to‐mid temperature range. Given the rapid progress achieved in this field, this review systematically surveys the latest advances in three representative classes of magnesium‐based thermoelectric materials: Mg3X2 (X = Sb, Bi), MgAgSb, and Mg2X (X = Si, Ge, Sn). Emphasis is placed on their crystal structures and electronic band features, phonon transport behavior, and carrier scattering mechanisms, together with a discussion of bulk and thin‐film fabrication strategies and their impacts on thermoelectric performance. Commonalities and distinct characteristics of performance optimization through carrier concentration tuning, band engineering, microstructural design, and interface engineering are summarized. Furthermore, key challenges associated with chemical and thermal stability as well as device integration are critically assessed, and device‐level design principles such as electrode contacting, diffusion barrier layers, and substrate selection are systematically reviewed. Finally, considering the current physical and engineering bottlenecks, several critical directions for the future development of magnesium‐based thermoelectric materials are proposed, highlighting the importance of a paradigm shift from “material‐level performance optimization” toward “material‐device‐system co‐design” to accelerate their practical deployment. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/4314f80c-6195-414f-9e29-695183ffa595/adma73493-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Advances in Magnesium-Based Thermoelectrics: A Critical Review"/> <p>Magnesium-based thermoelectric materials have emerged as promising candidates for low-to-mid-temperature energy conversion due to their abundance, low cost, and competitive performance. This review summarizes recent advances in Mg<sub>3</sub>X<sub>2</sub>, MgAgSb, and Mg<sub>2</sub>X systems, covering transport mechanisms, fabrication strategies, stability challenges, and device integration, and emphasizes a shift from material optimization toward integrated material-device-system co-design. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>In recent years, magnesium-based thermoelectric materials have emerged as a key research direction toward large-scale applications, owing to their elemental abundance, low material cost, good environmental compatibility, and competitive thermoelectric performance in the low-to-mid temperature range. Given the rapid progress achieved in this field, this review systematically surveys the latest advances in three representative classes of magnesium-based thermoelectric materials: Mg<sub>3</sub>X<sub>2</sub> (X = Sb, Bi), MgAgSb, and Mg<sub>2</sub>X (X = Si, Ge, Sn). Emphasis is placed on their crystal structures and electronic band features, phonon transport behavior, and carrier scattering mechanisms, together with a discussion of bulk and thin-film fabrication strategies and their impacts on thermoelectric performance. Commonalities and distinct characteristics of performance optimization through carrier concentration tuning, band engineering, microstructural design, and interface engineering are summarized. Furthermore, key challenges associated with chemical and thermal stability as well as device integration are critically assessed, and device-level design principles such as electrode contacting, diffusion barrier layers, and substrate selection are systematically reviewed. Finally, considering the current physical and engineering bottlenecks, several critical directions for the future development of magnesium-based thermoelectric materials are proposed, highlighting the importance of a paradigm shift from “material-level performance optimization” toward “material-device-system co-design” to accelerate their practical deployment.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Li‐Min Zhang, Li Zhang, Nan‐Hai Li, Yan‐Ling Yang, Xiao‐Lei Shi, Zhi‐Gang Chen </dc:creator>
<category>REVIEW</category>
<dc:title>Advances in Magnesium‐Based Thermoelectrics: A Critical Review</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73493</dc:identifier>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73596?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Thu, 04 Jun 2026 00:45:45 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-04T12:45:45-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73596</guid>
<title>Asymmetric Oxygen Bridges: A Unified Design Framework for Enhanced Catalysis</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Asymmetric oxygen bridges (AOBs), arise from asymmetry of M1‐O‐M2, which is induced by the differences in valence states, ionic radii, and electronegativities of oxygen‐bridged metal sites. It summarized the enhancement mechanisms of heteronuclear, homonuclear hetero‐valent, heteroatom modified, and dynamically tunable AOBs for heterogeneous catalysis. Finally, it discusses that mechanistic understanding, catalyst design, and advanced energy‐conversion technologies are in AOBs based catalysts. ABSTRACT The asymmetric oxygen bridges (AOBs), composed of asymmetric Metal1‐Oxygen‐Metal2 (M1‐O‐M2) with intrinsic electronic and geometric asymmetry, have gained significant attention in heterogeneous catalysis. The intrinsic asymmetric coordination induces unique and controllable interfacial charge polarization, which have recently inspired novel design concepts to achieve improved structure‐activity relationships. However, fully exploiting their potential requires a systematic framework to unify design principles. This review systematically presents the unique characteristics of AOBs originated by the different valence states, ionic radius and electronegativities of oxygen bridged metal sites. By analyzing the structure‐activity relationship between atomic‐scale charge polarization and lattice strain with macroscopic functionality, the enhancing mechanisms of AOBs from categories including heteronuclear AOBs, homonuclear hetero‐valent AOBs, heteroatoms modified AOBs and dynamically tunable AOBs is illustrated. These insights will provide new opportunities for understanding of catalytic mechanisms, the design of highly efficient catalysts and the development of more efficient energy conversion technologies. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/deb9f551-852d-49d0-a9b3-68cdd7dfd4be/adma73596-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Asymmetric Oxygen Bridges: A Unified Design Framework for Enhanced Catalysis"/> <p>Asymmetric oxygen bridges (AOBs), arise from asymmetry of M<sub>1</sub>-O-M<sub>2</sub>, which is induced by the differences in valence states, ionic radii, and electronegativities of oxygen-bridged metal sites. It summarized the enhancement mechanisms of heteronuclear, homonuclear hetero-valent, heteroatom modified, and dynamically tunable AOBs for heterogeneous catalysis. Finally, it discusses that mechanistic understanding, catalyst design, and advanced energy-conversion technologies are in AOBs based catalysts. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>The asymmetric oxygen bridges (AOBs), composed of asymmetric Metal<sub>1</sub>-Oxygen-Metal<sub>2</sub> (M<sub>1</sub>-O-M<sub>2</sub>) with intrinsic electronic and geometric asymmetry, have gained significant attention in heterogeneous catalysis. The intrinsic asymmetric coordination induces unique and controllable interfacial charge polarization, which have recently inspired novel design concepts to achieve improved structure-activity relationships. However, fully exploiting their potential requires a systematic framework to unify design principles. This review systematically presents the unique characteristics of AOBs originated by the different valence states, ionic radius and electronegativities of oxygen bridged metal sites. By analyzing the structure-activity relationship between atomic-scale charge polarization and lattice strain with macroscopic functionality, the enhancing mechanisms of AOBs from categories including heteronuclear AOBs, homonuclear hetero-valent AOBs, heteroatoms modified AOBs and dynamically tunable AOBs is illustrated. These insights will provide new opportunities for understanding of catalytic mechanisms, the design of highly efficient catalysts and the development of more efficient energy conversion technologies.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Xurui Zhang, Tailei Hou, Bohan Wu, Xiaoting He, Xinyuan Li, Dingsheng Wang, Jiatao Zhang </dc:creator>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73632?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Thu, 04 Jun 2026 00:44:09 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-04T12:44:09-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73632</guid>
<title>Non‐Fullerene Acceptor Photo‐Charge Enabled Stretchable Photovoltaic Robustness Under 90% Tensile Strain and 30% Strain Cycles</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> This study reports two record values in stretchable organic solar cells, which are >80% retention of initial power conversion efficiency at 90% tensile strain and at 30% strain after 1000 stretch‐release cycles. These successes originate from the utilization of a bulk photo‐charge generation pathway, where it takes place in non‐fullerene acceptor rather than at the elastomer embedded donor‐acceptor interface. ABSTRACT Stretchable organic solar cells (s‐OSCs) are promising for wearable electronics but suffer from performance degradation under deformation. We improve this situation by leveraging the property of bulk photo‐charge generation in non‐fullerene acceptors (NFAs). Due to its weak dependence on blend morphology, the bulk photo‐charge generation pathway helps alleviate the exciton utilization loss in elastomer‐diluted photoactive layer. Screened by the molecular descriptor of π‐π quadrupole moment (QZZ) and identified by the subsequent photovoltaic performance measurements on single‐component device, AQx‐2F is selected from 130 NFAs with superior bulk photo‐charge generation capability. Incorporating SEEPS via sequential deposition (SD) confers a high fracture strain (εf) of 185% in the photoactive layer. Taking advantage of it, the s‐OSC exhibits a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 9.8% with record photovoltaic robustness. It retains over 80% of its initial PCE both under a high tensile strain of 90% and after 1000 stretching‐releasing cycles at 30% strain. This photoactive layer also enables a flexible semi‐transparent OSC (FST‐OSC) that achieves the light utilization efficiency (LUE) comparable to rigid devices while maintaining mechanical stability. Rooted in emerging photo‐physics of organic photovoltaic materials, this work establishes a new strategy for photovoltaic robust s‐OSCs. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/3e435e04-c887-4e12-9857-6061ba9cfcdd/adma73632-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Non-Fullerene Acceptor Photo-Charge Enabled Stretchable Photovoltaic Robustness Under 90% Tensile Strain and 30% Strain Cycles"/> <p>This study reports two record values in stretchable organic solar cells, which are &gt;80% retention of initial power conversion efficiency at 90% tensile strain and at 30% strain after 1000 stretch-release cycles. These successes originate from the utilization of a bulk photo-charge generation pathway, where it takes place in non-fullerene acceptor rather than at the elastomer embedded donor-acceptor interface. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Stretchable organic solar cells (s-OSCs) are promising for wearable electronics but suffer from performance degradation under deformation. We improve this situation by leveraging the property of bulk photo-charge generation in non-fullerene acceptors (NFAs). Due to its weak dependence on blend morphology, the bulk photo-charge generation pathway helps alleviate the exciton utilization loss in elastomer-diluted photoactive layer. Screened by the molecular descriptor of π-π quadrupole moment (Q<sub>ZZ</sub>) and identified by the subsequent photovoltaic performance measurements on single-component device, AQx-2F is selected from 130 NFAs with superior bulk photo-charge generation capability. Incorporating SEEPS via sequential deposition (SD) confers a high fracture strain (ε<sub>f</sub>) of 185% in the photoactive layer. Taking advantage of it, the s-OSC exhibits a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 9.8% with record photovoltaic robustness. It retains over 80% of its initial PCE both under a high tensile strain of 90% and after 1000 stretching-releasing cycles at 30% strain. This photoactive layer also enables a flexible semi-transparent OSC (FST-OSC) that achieves the light utilization efficiency (LUE) comparable to rigid devices while maintaining mechanical stability. Rooted in emerging photo-physics of organic photovoltaic materials, this work establishes a new strategy for photovoltaic robust s-OSCs.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Yu Cui, Yerun Gao, Zhi Wang, Ming Shao, Bonan Shi, Zeyu Wang, Bohao Song, Guanghao Lu, Min Li, Xiaoyan Du, Bo Liu, Tao Li, Wei Ma, Han Yan </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Non‐Fullerene Acceptor Photo‐Charge Enabled Stretchable Photovoltaic Robustness Under 90% Tensile Strain and 30% Strain Cycles</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73632</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73632</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73632?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73535?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Thu, 04 Jun 2026 00:34:32 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-04T12:34:32-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73535</guid>
<title>Decoupling Bulk Homogenization and Interfacial Reconstruction via a Triple‐Alkali‐Cation Interlayer for High‐Performance Perovskite Solar Cells</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> High‐performance perovskite solar cells are achieved via a spatially partitioned triple‐alkali‐cation interlayer. This bottom‐up strategy enables interfacial reconstruction by Li+/K+ retention and promotes in situ Cs+ redistribution for bulk homogenization. The resulting stress‐free films deliver efficiencies exceeding 26% with enhanced long‐term stability. ABSTRACT Precise control over cation distribution is critical for high‐performance perovskite solar cells (PSCs). Conventional bulk doping often leads to vertical segregation and lattice strain, while surface passivation dose not ensure bulk homogeneity. We introduce a triple‐alkali interlayer (LiOH/KCl/CsI) deposited on the electron transport layer prior to crystallization of the perovskite film. This design spatially decouples crystallization regulation from compositional modulation, i.e., localized Li+ and K+ ions reconstruct the buried contact and passivate defects and interfacial Cs+ acts as a dynamic source for in situ upward diffusion. This bottom‐up mechanism facilitates stress‐free crystallization, resulting in a dense, preferentially oriented perovskite film with a void‐free buried interface and superior compositional homogeneity. Consequently, the resulting champion n‐i‐p PSC achieves a remarkable power conversion efficiency of 26.13%, with a high open‐circuit voltage of 1.184 V and a fill factor of 83.81%. Furthermore, the devices demonstrate robust durability maintaining 93.7% after 1440 h of continuous 1‐sun irradiation at 65°C. This work provides a promising pathway for managing cation dynamics to realize efficient and stable perovskite photovoltaics. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/6708e0fe-e9b0-453a-965c-630cd2d938d4/adma73535-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Decoupling Bulk Homogenization and Interfacial Reconstruction via a Triple-Alkali-Cation Interlayer for High-Performance Perovskite Solar Cells"/> <p>High-performance perovskite solar cells are achieved via a spatially partitioned triple-alkali-cation interlayer. This bottom-up strategy enables interfacial reconstruction by Li<sup>+</sup>/K<sup>+</sup> retention and promotes in situ Cs<sup>+</sup> redistribution for bulk homogenization. The resulting stress-free films deliver efficiencies exceeding 26% with enhanced long-term stability. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Precise control over cation distribution is critical for high-performance perovskite solar cells (PSCs). Conventional bulk doping often leads to vertical segregation and lattice strain, while surface passivation dose not ensure bulk homogeneity. We introduce a triple-alkali interlayer (LiOH/KCl/CsI) deposited on the electron transport layer prior to crystallization of the perovskite film. This design spatially decouples crystallization regulation from compositional modulation, i.e., localized Li<sup>+</sup> and K<sup>+</sup> ions reconstruct the buried contact and passivate defects and interfacial Cs<sup>+</sup> acts as a dynamic source for in situ upward diffusion. This bottom-up mechanism facilitates stress-free crystallization, resulting in a dense, preferentially oriented perovskite film with a void-free buried interface and superior compositional homogeneity. Consequently, the resulting champion n-i-p PSC achieves a remarkable power conversion efficiency of 26.13%, with a high open-circuit voltage of 1.184 V and a fill factor of 83.81%. Furthermore, the devices demonstrate robust durability maintaining 93.7% after 1440 h of continuous 1-sun irradiation at 65°C. This work provides a promising pathway for managing cation dynamics to realize efficient and stable perovskite photovoltaics.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Yuanhang Zhang, Xiao‐Xin Gao, Hewei Wang, Yanyan Fang, Pengfei Zhang, Enjia Jiang, Xiangrong Li, Jiayi Niu, Hong‐Bo Cheng, Dongmei Xie, Yao Zhao, Paul J. Dyson, Mohammad Khaja Nazeeruddin, Yuan Lin </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Decoupling Bulk Homogenization and Interfacial Reconstruction via a Triple‐Alkali‐Cation Interlayer for High‐Performance Perovskite Solar Cells</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73535</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>

<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73604?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Thu, 04 Jun 2026 00:33:13 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-04T12:33:13-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73604</guid>
<title>Photoinduced Structural Relaxation in Chiral Copper(l) Iodide Cluster Scintillators for Circularly Polarized Radioluminescence Imaging</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> This work reports chiral copper‐iodide cluster scintillators with circularly polarized luminescence. Chiral ligands create a non‐centrosymmetric crystal field and photoinduced Cu–Cu contraction, enabling efficient CPL. A flexible polarization‐gated film achieves 21.2 lp/mm x‐ray resolution by suppressing optical crosstalk, with a high light yield of 58 000 photons/MeV and excellent radiation stability. ABSTRACT Circularly polarized luminescence offers untapped potential for enhancing x‐ray imaging resolution by suppressing optical crosstalk, yet its integration into high‐performance scintillators remains unexplored. Here we report a new class of chiral copper(I) halide scintillators, including enantiopure R‑/S‑3‑MLCuI (3‐ML represents 3‐methylmorpholine) circularly polarized scintillators and racemic Rac‑3‑MLCuI scintillators. Strategic enantiomer selection enables precise control over crystal symmetry—chiral P42212 vs. centrosymmetric C2/c—dictating chiroptical activity. Combining TD‐DFT, in situ XAS, and ultrafast spectroscopy, we elucidate that emission originates from cluster‐centered self‐trapped excitons confined within discrete [Cu4I4] cores, where pronounced excited‐state Cu─Cu contraction and Cu─I elongation induce strong exciton‐phonon coupling. Homochiral ligand environments impart persistent chiral imprinting, yielding |glum| values up to 1.0 × 10−2. The materials exhibit record light yields (58 000 photons•MeV−1), exceptional x‐ray stability (>99% retention after 115 Gy), and ultra‐low detection limits (78.9 n Gyair•s−1). A flexible S‐3‐MLCuI@TPU film incorporating polarization‐gated imaging achieves 21.2 lp/mm resolution—significantly surpassing conventional scintillators by suppressing optical crosstalk. High‑fidelity x‑ray imaging of complex biological and electronic structures is demonstrated. This work provides a structure–property relationship that enables circularly polarized scintillation in chiral copper–iodide clusters, offering insights for further development of related systems. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/1724bc9e-40ea-4c2d-b5fb-b4893848a85f/adma73604-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Photoinduced Structural Relaxation in Chiral Copper(l) Iodide Cluster Scintillators for Circularly Polarized Radioluminescence Imaging"/> <p>This work reports chiral copper-iodide cluster scintillators with circularly polarized luminescence. Chiral ligands create a non-centrosymmetric crystal field and photoinduced Cu–Cu contraction, enabling efficient CPL. A flexible polarization-gated film achieves 21.2 lp/mm x-ray resolution by suppressing optical crosstalk, with a high light yield of 58 000 photons/MeV and excellent radiation stability. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Circularly polarized luminescence offers untapped potential for enhancing x-ray imaging resolution by suppressing optical crosstalk, yet its integration into high-performance scintillators remains unexplored. Here we report a new class of chiral copper(I) halide scintillators, including enantiopure R‑/S‑3‑MLCuI (3-ML represents 3-methylmorpholine) circularly polarized scintillators and racemic Rac‑3‑MLCuI scintillators. Strategic enantiomer selection enables precise control over crystal symmetry—chiral P4<sub>2</sub>2<sub>1</sub>2 vs. centrosymmetric C2/c—dictating chiroptical activity. Combining TD-DFT, in situ XAS, and ultrafast spectroscopy, we elucidate that emission originates from cluster-centered self-trapped excitons confined within discrete [Cu<sub>4</sub>I<sub>4</sub>] cores, where pronounced excited-state Cu─Cu contraction and Cu─I elongation induce strong exciton-phonon coupling. Homochiral ligand environments impart persistent chiral imprinting, yielding |g<sub>lum</sub>| values up to 1.0 × 10<sup>−2</sup>. The materials exhibit record light yields (58 000 photons•MeV<sup>−1</sup>), exceptional x-ray stability (&gt;99% retention after 115 Gy), and ultra-low detection limits (78.9 n Gy<sub>air</sub>•s<sup>−1</sup>). A flexible S-3-MLCuI@TPU film incorporating polarization-gated imaging achieves 21.2 lp/mm resolution—significantly surpassing conventional scintillators by suppressing optical crosstalk. High‑fidelity x‑ray imaging of complex biological and electronic structures is demonstrated. This work provides a structure–property relationship that enables circularly polarized scintillation in chiral copper–iodide clusters, offering insights for further development of related systems.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Pengyu Zhang, Chen Li, Yiping Du, Qingsong Hu, Zhuoer Cai, Sen Zhang, Ling Guan, Jiawen Xiao, Bing Tang, Li Wang, Feng Luo, Zhengguang Yan </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Photoinduced Structural Relaxation in Chiral Copper(l) Iodide Cluster Scintillators for Circularly Polarized Radioluminescence Imaging</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73604</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73604</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73604?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>
...
</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73625?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Thu, 04 Jun 2026 00:30:47 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-04T12:30:47-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73625</guid>
<title>Local Charge Engineering Through Anion–Cation Co‐modulation for Multifunctional Programmable Electromagnetic Wave Attenuation</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Based on a dual‐ion synergistic regulation mechanism leveraging S, N co‐doping strategy, the electronic structure was optimized, accompanied by synchronous modulation of the MxSy's work function, which allowed precise control over the BIEF intensity, ultimately achieving programmable bandwidth EMWA capability. The potential for wireless communication was validated through microstrip antenna and power divider designs. Additionally, these composites achieved radar–infrared‐compatible stealth. ABSTRACT Electromagnetic wave absorption (EMWA) materials with tunable responses are critically important for operation in complex electromagnetic environments. In this study, a novel dual‐ion co‐modulation strategy is introduced to overcome the limited controllability of conventional EMWA materials. By employing a coordination‐mediated gelation phase transformation approach, a series of transition metal sulfide/sulfur–nitrogen co‐doped carbon (MxSy/SNC, M = Fe, Co, Ni, or Cu) aerogels are successfully fabricated. First‐principles calculations demonstrate that N,S co‐doping tunes the electronic structure of the carbon matrix, enhancing local charge imbalance and promoting dipole polarization, which significantly broadens the EMWA band. At 1.65 mm, the effective absorption bandwidth almost covers the entire Ku band. Furthermore, cation‐induced modulation of the electronic configuration enables precise tuning of the built‐in electric field and dielectric response, resulting in customizable absorption peaks and bandwidths. All samples achieve a minimum reflection loss (RLmin) below −60 dB, with the RLmin peak frequency shifting from 17.44 GHz to 11.6, 9.84, and 5.12 GHz depending on the metal ion. Finally, a low‐frequency antenna and a one‐to‐two power divider are constructed, demonstrating strong application potential in the communications field. This study provides a new pathway for designing high‐performance, programmable EMWA systems and multifunctional materials. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/fa08b3fb-92fc-4355-bf71-7b78dd70fd0c/adma73625-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Local Charge Engineering Through Anion–Cation Co-modulation for Multifunctional Programmable Electromagnetic Wave Attenuation"/> <p>Based on a dual-ion synergistic regulation mechanism leveraging S, N co-doping strategy, the electronic structure was optimized, accompanied by synchronous modulation of the M<sub>x</sub>S<sub>y</sub>'s work function, which allowed precise control over the BIEF intensity, ultimately achieving programmable bandwidth EMWA capability. The potential for wireless communication was validated through microstrip antenna and power divider designs. Additionally, these composites achieved radar–infrared-compatible stealth. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Electromagnetic wave absorption (EMWA) materials with tunable responses are critically important for operation in complex electromagnetic environments. In this study, a novel dual-ion co-modulation strategy is introduced to overcome the limited controllability of conventional EMWA materials. By employing a coordination-mediated gelation phase transformation approach, a series of transition metal sulfide/sulfur–nitrogen co-doped carbon (M<sub>x</sub>S<sub>y</sub>/SNC, M = Fe, Co, Ni, or Cu) aerogels are successfully fabricated. First-principles calculations demonstrate that N,S co-doping tunes the electronic structure of the carbon matrix, enhancing local charge imbalance and promoting dipole polarization, which significantly broadens the EMWA band. At 1.65 mm, the effective absorption bandwidth almost covers the entire Ku band. Furthermore, cation-induced modulation of the electronic configuration enables precise tuning of the built-in electric field and dielectric response, resulting in customizable absorption peaks and bandwidths. All samples achieve a minimum reflection loss (<i>RL<sub>min</sub> </i>) below −60 dB, with the <i>RL<sub>min</sub> </i> peak frequency shifting from 17.44 GHz to 11.6, 9.84, and 5.12 GHz depending on the metal ion. Finally, a low-frequency antenna and a one-to-two power divider are constructed, demonstrating strong application potential in the communications field. This study provides a new pathway for designing high-performance, programmable EMWA systems and multifunctional materials.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Dan Wang, Chongbo Liu, Hualong Peng, Ruizhe Hu, Qi Zheng, Haoran Huang, Fang Zhao, Yuhui Peng, Maosheng Cao </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Local Charge Engineering Through Anion–Cation Co‐modulation for Multifunctional Programmable Electromagnetic Wave Attenuation</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73625</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73625</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73625?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73594?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Thu, 04 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-04T12:00:00-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73594</guid>
<title>Recent Advances of Organic Room Temperature Phosphorescence for Biological Applications</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> This review provides a comprehensive overview of prepared methods for phosphorescent nanoparticles (NPs) and elucidates molecular design strategies tailored to optimize RTP performance for biomedical applications, including advanced bioimaging, photodynamic cancer therapy. ABSTRACT Organic room‐temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have attracted significant attention in the fields of organic light‐emitting diodes (OLEDs), secure data encryption, chemical sensing, bioimaging, and scintillation, driven by their superior triplet exciton utilization, large Stokes shifts, and long‐lived emission. However, traditional RTP systems are predominantly synthesized in the bulk state, resulting in inherent mechanical brittleness and poor aqueous dispersibility that impede their integration into complex biological environments. Developing high‐efficiency nanoscale organic phosphors that maintain intense triplet emission remains a formidable challenge. This review provides a comprehensive overview of prepared methods for phosphorescent nanoparticles (NPs) and elucidates molecular design strategies tailored to optimize RTP performance for biomedical frontiers. Specifically, we highlight their transformative potential in advanced bioimaging and photodynamic cancer therapy. Finally, we discuss current bottlenecks and offer a prospective outlook on future advancements, aiming to bridge the gap between fundamental molecular design and clinical translatability. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/e4e202c4-97b1-4ae5-ac37-6ed4906221cf/adma73594-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Recent Advances of Organic Room Temperature Phosphorescence for Biological Applications"/> <p>This review provides a comprehensive overview of prepared methods for phosphorescent nanoparticles (NPs) and elucidates molecular design strategies tailored to optimize RTP performance for biomedical applications, including advanced bioimaging, photodynamic cancer therapy. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Organic room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have attracted significant attention in the fields of organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs), secure data encryption, chemical sensing, bioimaging, and scintillation, driven by their superior triplet exciton utilization, large Stokes shifts, and long-lived emission. However, traditional RTP systems are predominantly synthesized in the bulk state, resulting in inherent mechanical brittleness and poor aqueous dispersibility that impede their integration into complex biological environments. Developing high-efficiency nanoscale organic phosphors that maintain intense triplet emission remains a formidable challenge. This review provides a comprehensive overview of prepared methods for phosphorescent nanoparticles (NPs) and elucidates molecular design strategies tailored to optimize RTP performance for biomedical frontiers. Specifically, we highlight their transformative potential in advanced bioimaging and photodynamic cancer therapy. Finally, we discuss current bottlenecks and offer a prospective outlook on future advancements, aiming to bridge the gap between fundamental molecular design and clinical translatability.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Yue Feng, Yue Shen, Beishen Wu, Xiaobing Liu, Zhongfu An, Huifang Shi, Wei Huang </dc:creator>

<dc:title>Recent Advances of Organic Room Temperature Phosphorescence for Biological Applications</dc:title>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202523099?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Wed, 03 Jun 2026 21:20:19 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-03T09:20:19-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202523099</guid>
<title>Dexime: A Selectively Enzyme‐Degradable Hydrogel for Protein Therapeutic Release</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> A dextrin‐oxime hydrogel (dexime) is produced using ketone or aldehyde modified dextrin and tetra‐oxyamine modified poly(ethylene glycol). The rheological and mechanical properties of dexime are tunable. Dexime is injectable, cytocompatible, hydrolytically stable, and selectively degradable by α‐amylase. Dexime delivers bioactive chondroitinase ABC to the rat spinal cord using a minimally invasive procedure. ABSTRACT Hydrogels are of great interest for the delivery of protein therapeutics as they obviate the denaturing shear forces and/or organic solvents common to other delivery systems. Dextrin, a polysaccharide derived from starch, has been largely ignored in the design of hydrogels, yet is biodegradable and can be modified to allow in situ gelation after injection by click‐crosslinking. Herein, dextrin is modified with either ketone or aldehyde moieties and then click‐crosslinked with poly(ethylene glycol)‐tetraoxyamine to form dextrin‐oxime (dexime) hydrogels. The rheological and mechanical properties are tunable by changing formulation parameters such as total dextrin weight percentage (wt.%) and pH. The hydrogels are injectable through a 30G needle, selectively degradable via α‐amylase, yet stable and minimally swelling in the presence of other enzymes, including hyaluronidase and chondroitinase ABC (ChASE). The presence of low amounts of α‐amylase in many fluids and tissues of the body allow for the slow degradation of dexime with minimal swelling. Dexime is particularly useful for local delivery of ChASE: an enzyme that degrades the aggregate scar formed after injury in the central nervous system. Dexime is cytocompatible and releases active ChASE both in vitro and in vivo as evidenced by chondroitin sulphate proteoglycan degradation in the spinal cord. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/34380f2d-7eb6-4a9c-93f2-e6e86b04d457/adma73262-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Dexime: A Selectively Enzyme-Degradable Hydrogel for Protein Therapeutic Release"/> <p>A dextrin-oxime hydrogel (dexime) is produced using ketone or aldehyde modified dextrin and tetra-oxyamine modified poly(ethylene glycol). The rheological and mechanical properties of dexime are tunable. Dexime is injectable, cytocompatible, hydrolytically stable, and selectively degradable by α-amylase. Dexime delivers bioactive chondroitinase ABC to the rat spinal cord using a minimally invasive procedure. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Hydrogels are of great interest for the delivery of protein therapeutics as they obviate the denaturing shear forces and/or organic solvents common to other delivery systems. Dextrin, a polysaccharide derived from starch, has been largely ignored in the design of hydrogels, yet is biodegradable and can be modified to allow in situ gelation after injection by click-crosslinking. Herein, dextrin is modified with either ketone or aldehyde moieties and then click-crosslinked with poly(ethylene glycol)-tetraoxyamine to form dextrin-oxime (dexime) hydrogels. The rheological and mechanical properties are tunable by changing formulation parameters such as total dextrin weight percentage (wt.%) and pH. The hydrogels are injectable through a 30G needle, selectively degradable via α-amylase, yet stable and minimally swelling in the presence of other enzymes, including hyaluronidase and chondroitinase ABC (ChASE). The presence of low amounts of α-amylase in many fluids and tissues of the body allow for the slow degradation of dexime with minimal swelling. Dexime is particularly useful for local delivery of ChASE: an enzyme that degrades the aggregate scar formed after injury in the central nervous system. Dexime is cytocompatible and releases active ChASE both in vitro and in vivo as evidenced by chondroitin sulphate proteoglycan degradation in the spinal cord.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Quinton E. A. Sirianni, Nitzan Letko Khait, Adam Forman, Edward A. Armstrong, Karim Fouad, Molly S. Shoichet </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Dexime: A Selectively Enzyme‐Degradable Hydrogel for Protein Therapeutic Release</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.202523099</dc:identifier>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73566?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Wed, 03 Jun 2026 04:36:45 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-03T04:36:45-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73566</guid>
<title>Nanomachines Based on Inner Ultrasonic Multiple Scattering for Ameliorating Renal Function</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Rui Gao et al. synthesized an Au@PASP‐PArg/BaTiO3 (ASPB) nanomachine that could repair renal damnification via the wireless automatic positioning and tracking under current‐ultrasound alternation. These distinguished mobile nanomachines showed dramatic kidney protection based on modulating MYDGF (myeloid‐derived growth factor) expression, KCNQ1 (potassium voltage‐gated channel subfamily KQT member 1) functions, and TMAO (trimethylamine N‐oxide) targeted metabolomics. ABSTRACT Protecting cell/tissue disservice during acute kidney injury (AKI) was an important but still challenging goal. Here, we proposed a current‐ultrasound convertion‐propelled wireless positioning and tracking nanomachine in which Au covered one side of a PASP‐PArg/BaTiO3 footplate (Au@PASP‐PArg/BaTiO3, ASPB). These navigational nanomaches were used in the treatment of AKI to confirm a new technology and method for actively inhibiting kidney‐trauma formation to enable highly efficient kidney‐protection. ASPB possessed the high dielectric constant and piezoelectric properties, which acquired endogenous ultrasound‐current and guided navigation under exogenous current excitement. Under the triggering of endogenous ultrasound, ASPB further gave rise to the unbalanced charges on its surface and underwent oxidation‐reduction reactions with surrounding water, obtaining H2O2 and 1O2; meanwhile, ASPB could also decompose water to produce O2 under the inducement of endogenous ultrasound. Additionally, the attained H2O2 and 1O2 catalyzed polyarginine to discharge NO. Importantly, under accurate navigation, endogenous ultrasound‐triggered gas sensor discharged NO/O2, and endogenous current could protect renal tissues from damage based on modulating MYDGF expressions, KCNQ1 functions, and TMAO targeted metabolomics. These current‐ultrasound convertion‐pushed navigational nanomaches clearly unveiled illustrious potential for promoting highly efficient kidney‐function protection. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/1c13dc0b-4159-44d1-b736-cc86a5d18b9e/adma73566-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Nanomachines Based on Inner Ultrasonic Multiple Scattering for Ameliorating Renal Function"/> <p>Rui Gao et al. synthesized an Au@PASP-PArg/BaTiO<sub>3</sub> (ASPB) nanomachine that could repair renal damnification via the wireless automatic positioning and tracking under current-ultrasound alternation. These distinguished mobile nanomachines showed dramatic kidney protection based on modulating MYDGF (myeloid-derived growth factor) expression, KCNQ1 (potassium voltage-gated channel subfamily KQT member 1) functions, and TMAO (trimethylamine N-oxide) targeted metabolomics. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Protecting cell/tissue disservice during acute kidney injury (AKI) was an important but still challenging goal. Here, we proposed a current-ultrasound convertion-propelled wireless positioning and tracking nanomachine in which Au covered one side of a PASP-PArg/BaTiO<sub>3</sub> footplate (Au@PASP-PArg/BaTiO<sub>3</sub>, ASPB). These navigational nanomaches were used in the treatment of AKI to confirm a new technology and method for actively inhibiting kidney-trauma formation to enable highly efficient kidney-protection. ASPB possessed the high dielectric constant and piezoelectric properties, which acquired endogenous ultrasound-current and guided navigation under exogenous current excitement. Under the triggering of endogenous ultrasound, ASPB further gave rise to the unbalanced charges on its surface and underwent oxidation-reduction reactions with surrounding water, obtaining H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> and <sup>1</sup>O<sub>2</sub>; meanwhile, ASPB could also decompose water to produce O<sub>2</sub> under the inducement of endogenous ultrasound. Additionally, the attained H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> and <sup>1</sup>O<sub>2</sub> catalyzed polyarginine to discharge NO. Importantly, under accurate navigation, endogenous ultrasound-triggered gas sensor discharged NO/O<sub>2,</sub> and endogenous current could protect renal tissues from damage based on modulating MYDGF expressions, KCNQ1 functions, and TMAO targeted metabolomics. These current-ultrasound convertion-pushed navigational nanomaches clearly unveiled illustrious potential for promoting highly efficient kidney-function protection.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Rui Gao, Yongsheng Du, Yu Dong, Lin Zhang, Yaning Wang, Fei Tong </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73583?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Wed, 03 Jun 2026 04:34:22 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-03T04:34:22-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73583</guid>
<title>Programmable Stepwise Heteroepitaxial Growth of Colloidal Crystals With Different Phases</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Stepwise heteroepitaxial growth is adapted to colloidal crystal engineering with DNA, enabling face‐centered cubic (fcc) facets to grow on body‐centered cubic (bcc) crystals with 110 facets. This approach tolerates large lattice mismatches, extending heteroepitaxy beyond the limits of conventional atomic systems. ABSTRACT Heteroepitaxial growth is a powerful strategy for constructing hierarchical systems by integrating materials with different structures across the angstrom to nanometer length scale. However, lattice mismatches between different phases often impact the resulting crystal stability. This is especially true for colloidal crystal systems. Here, colloidal crystal engineering with DNA is used to assemble multi‐phase colloidal crystals, with extreme tolerance for lattice strain. Most notably, the structural flexibility of DNA can accommodate lattice mismatch up to 18%, allowing one to grow, for the first time, face‐centered cubic (fcc) lattices with (111) facets on body‐centered cubic (bcc) crystals with (110) facets (a 13% bcc‐fcc phase misfit for the particles studied; 2%–4% in an atomic system). By adjusting particle size, more or less strain can be induced, allowing one to determine the upper limit for bcc‐fcc phase misfit (34%). Finite‐difference time‐domain (FDTD) optical simulations reveal that these multi‐phase heteroepitaxial structures can function as waveguides, making them attractive targets for those interested in optics. The lattice mismatches accommodated through DNA bonding exceed those typical in atomic heteroepitaxy (a few percent without a buffer layer), highlighting the versatility of this technique for designing and preparing hierarchical materials with tailored structure‐function relationships. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/70624a4d-040d-494b-af1c-af162be3afc7/adma73583-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Programmable Stepwise Heteroepitaxial Growth of Colloidal Crystals With Different Phases"/> <p>Stepwise heteroepitaxial growth is adapted to colloidal crystal engineering with DNA, enabling face-centered cubic (fcc) facets to grow on body-centered cubic (bcc) crystals with 110 facets. This approach tolerates large lattice mismatches, extending heteroepitaxy beyond the limits of conventional atomic systems. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Heteroepitaxial growth is a powerful strategy for constructing hierarchical systems by integrating materials with different structures across the angstrom to nanometer length scale. However, lattice mismatches between different phases often impact the resulting crystal stability. This is especially true for colloidal crystal systems. Here, colloidal crystal engineering with DNA is used to assemble multi-phase colloidal crystals, with extreme tolerance for lattice strain. Most notably, the structural flexibility of DNA can accommodate lattice mismatch up to 18%, allowing one to grow, for the first time, face-centered cubic (fcc) lattices with (111) facets on body-centered cubic (bcc) crystals with (110) facets (a 13% bcc-fcc phase misfit for the particles studied; 2%–4% in an atomic system). By adjusting particle size, more or less strain can be induced, allowing one to determine the upper limit for bcc-fcc phase misfit (34%). Finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) optical simulations reveal that these multi-phase heteroepitaxial structures can function as waveguides, making them attractive targets for those interested in optics. The lattice mismatches accommodated through DNA bonding exceed those typical in atomic heteroepitaxy (a few percent without a buffer layer), highlighting the versatility of this technique for designing and preparing hierarchical materials with tailored structure-function relationships.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Xiaowei Liu, Yuanwei Li, Ramin Yazdaanpanah, Ye Zhang, Rachel R. Chan, Xiaobing Hu, Yiming Yang, Vinayak P. Dravid, Koray Aydin, Chad A. Mirkin </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Programmable Stepwise Heteroepitaxial Growth of Colloidal Crystals With Different Phases</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73583</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73583</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73583?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>
...
</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73569?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Wed, 03 Jun 2026 00:25:51 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-03T12:25:51-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73569</guid>
<title>Chiral Memory‐Driven Helical Supramolecular Photodetector for Deciphering Circularly Polarized Light</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> A template‐free strategy using chiral memory effect constructed helical supramolecules in chiral small‐molecule semiconductors. By modifying perylene diimide with expanded bay‐region conjugation and (R/S)‐1‐phenylethyl pendants, chiral dinaphthocoronene tetraimides were synthesized. Annealing removed chiral pendants, yet helical structures formed via chiral memory, affording (R)‐PDI‐(R)‐NI films with a |gabs| of 0.08. Large‐area devices based on these films achieve efficient CPL detection and imaging. ABSTRACT Chiral π‐conjugated organic small molecules, due to their intrinsic chirality, well‐defined molecular structures and ease of modification, are promising for circularly polarized light (CPL) detection. However, their practical application is hindered by low absorption dissymmetry factor (gabs), arising from imbalanced electric and magnetic transition dipole moments (µ and m). Here, a template‐free method using chiral memory effect was reported to fabricate helical supramolecules with high gabs. Two chiral dinaphthocoronene tetraimides enantiomer pairs were synthesized: (R)‐PDI‐(R)‐NI‐Ph/(S)‐PDI‐(S)‐NI‐Ph and (S)‐PDI‐(R)‐NI‐Ph/(R)‐PDI‐(S)‐NI‐Ph. After annealing films at 350°C to remove chiral pendants, (R)‐PDI‐(R)‐NI/(S)‐PDI‐(S)‐NI films showed much higher |gabs| (0.08) than the other set (0.01), with helical assemblies formed via chiral memory effect. Notably, large‐area arrays of (R)‐PDI‐(R)‐NI films enabled CPL imaging, and a bionic artificial visual system was constructed with this film as “retina” and an artificial neural network as “neural center” for reliable CPL discrimination, offering a scalable strategy for high‐performance chiral optoelectronics. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/3e49a2f9-fb99-4153-a65b-978d43df7d24/adma73569-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Chiral Memory-Driven Helical Supramolecular Photodetector for Deciphering Circularly Polarized Light"/> <p>A template-free strategy using chiral memory effect constructed helical supramolecules in chiral small-molecule semiconductors. By modifying perylene diimide with expanded bay-region conjugation and (<i>R</i>/<i>S</i>)-1-phenylethyl pendants, chiral dinaphthocoronene tetraimides were synthesized. Annealing removed chiral pendants, yet helical structures formed via chiral memory, affording (<i>R</i>)-PDI-(<i>R</i>)-NI films with a |<i>g</i> <sub>abs</sub>| of 0.08. Large-area devices based on these films achieve efficient CPL detection and imaging. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Chiral π-conjugated organic small molecules, due to their intrinsic chirality, well-defined molecular structures and ease of modification, are promising for circularly polarized light (CPL) detection. However, their practical application is hindered by low absorption dissymmetry factor (<i>g</i> <sub>abs</sub>), arising from imbalanced electric and magnetic transition dipole moments (<b> <i>µ</i> </b> and <b> <i>m</i> </b>). Here, a template-free method using chiral memory effect was reported to fabricate helical supramolecules with high <i>g</i> <sub>abs</sub>. Two chiral dinaphthocoronene tetraimides enantiomer pairs were synthesized: (<i>R</i>)-PDI-(<i>R</i>)-NI-Ph/(<i>S</i>)-PDI-(<i>S</i>)-NI-Ph and (<i>S</i>)-PDI-(<i>R</i>)-NI-Ph/(<i>R</i>)-PDI-(<i>S</i>)-NI-Ph. After annealing films at 350°C to remove chiral pendants, (<i>R</i>)-PDI-(<i>R</i>)-NI/(<i>S</i>)-PDI-(<i>S</i>)-NI films showed much higher |<i>g</i> <sub>abs</sub>| (0.08) than the other set (0.01), with helical assemblies formed via chiral memory effect. Notably, large-area arrays of (<i>R</i>)-PDI-(<i>R</i>)-NI films enabled CPL imaging, and a bionic artificial visual system was constructed with this film as “retina” and an artificial neural network as “neural center” for reliable CPL discrimination, offering a scalable strategy for high-performance chiral optoelectronics.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Ke Gao, Ye In Cho, Wenkai Zhao, Shijie Wang, Sang Hyuk Lee, Jaeyong Ahn, Wenda Zhao, Zhaoyu Wang, Zhiwei Wang, Zhenping Li, Xingshuo Chu, Pei Zhou, Wei Ma, Guankui Long, Joon Hak Oh, Xiaobo Shang </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Chiral Memory‐Driven Helical Supramolecular Photodetector for Deciphering Circularly Polarized Light</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73569</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73569</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73569?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>
