</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202519723?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Wed, 03 Jun 2026 00:21:51 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-03T12:21:51-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202519723</guid>
<title>Immunoengineering External Field Responsive Biomaterials for Tissue Repair and Regeneration</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> This review presents the design and application of external field‐responsive biomaterials (EFRBs) for tissue repair and regeneration. By leveraging external stimuli such as light, heat, ultrasound, and electrical fields, EFRBs dynamically regulate immune responses, enhance tissue regeneration, and promote functional recovery. The study highlights key materials, mechanisms, and future directions in immunoengineering. ABSTRACT Tissue defect repair is a dynamic process involving inflammation regulation, angiogenesis, organized extracellular matrix assembly, and immune microenvironment homeostasis. Its success critically depends on repair strategies with precise spatiotemporal control. External field‐responsive biomaterials (EFRBs), which respond to optical, magnetic, electrical, ultrasonic, and thermal stimuli, have become a research focus in regenerative medicine due to their on‐demand activation, unique (bio)physicochemical properties, and ability to modulate immune‐related pathways. This review first outlines the dynamic biological requirements of tissue repair, including the immune microenvironment's role, and the core advantages of EFRBs. It then details the structural features and external field response mechanisms of various EFRBs—such as nanoparticles, scaffolds, hydrogels, and microneedles—and their regulatory effects on immune‐related repair processes. Finally, it systematically summarizes recent advances in immunoengineering EFRBs for enhancing tissue repair and regeneration. This review thus aims to provide valuable insights for optimizing EFRB‐based therapies and to inspire further breakthroughs in this evolving field. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/538edeee-f6ca-4d90-a64c-bc7540828f95/adma73591-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Immunoengineering External Field Responsive Biomaterials for Tissue Repair and Regeneration"/> <p>This review presents the design and application of external field-responsive biomaterials (EFRBs) for tissue repair and regeneration. By leveraging external stimuli such as light, heat, ultrasound, and electrical fields, EFRBs dynamically regulate immune responses, enhance tissue regeneration, and promote functional recovery. The study highlights key materials, mechanisms, and future directions in immunoengineering. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Tissue defect repair is a dynamic process involving inflammation regulation, angiogenesis, organized extracellular matrix assembly, and immune microenvironment homeostasis. Its success critically depends on repair strategies with precise spatiotemporal control. External field-responsive biomaterials (EFRBs), which respond to optical, magnetic, electrical, ultrasonic, and thermal stimuli, have become a research focus in regenerative medicine due to their on-demand activation, unique (bio)physicochemical properties, and ability to modulate immune-related pathways. This review first outlines the dynamic biological requirements of tissue repair, including the immune microenvironment's role, and the core advantages of EFRBs. It then details the structural features and external field response mechanisms of various EFRBs—such as nanoparticles, scaffolds, hydrogels, and microneedles—and their regulatory effects on immune-related repair processes. Finally, it systematically summarizes recent advances in immunoengineering EFRBs for enhancing tissue repair and regeneration. This review thus aims to provide valuable insights for optimizing EFRB-based therapies and to inspire further breakthroughs in this evolving field.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Wanli Song, Jia Song, Jinchu Liang, Yang Liu, Boon Chin Heng, Xuliang Deng, Xuehui Zhang </dc:creator>
<category>REVIEW</category>
<dc:title>Immunoengineering External Field Responsive Biomaterials for Tissue Repair and Regeneration</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.202519723</dc:identifier>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73595?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Wed, 03 Jun 2026 00:16:57 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-03T12:16:57-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73595</guid>
<title>Designable van der Waals Crystal for Artificial Neuronal Cell Mimicking</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Designable van der Waals crystal has been demonstrated for device‐scale neuronal cell mimicking. The structural similarity between ion‐channel in biological membranes and layered vdW lattices is realized with nano‐crystallization via Ar + H2S plasma sulfurization. As the sequential transition from intralayer migration to coupled intra‐/interlayer migration reveals the dynamic control of synaptic weight updates. Optoelectronic synaptic plasticity in designable vdW were conclusively observed and correlates with photo‐induced carrier trapping and ionic migration. Furthermore, learning‐forgetting‐relearning cycles achieves 34.7 % increased retention efficiency compared to bulk ReSe2. Functional demonstrations in edge detection and CIFAR‐10 image recognition confirm the synaptic plasticity into system‐level neuromorphic computation, with a recognition accuracy of 96.24%. In conclusion, our designable vdW crystal will offer the generalizable platform for next‐generation 3D integrated neuromorphic device and edge‐computing AI hardware. ABSTRACT Optogenetics employs light to regulate neuronal activity with exceptional spatiotemporal precision, thereby enabling the direct modulation of learning and memory processes in the human brain. This capability to externally control neuronal signaling via optical stimuli has provided profound insights into brain function and established a versatile strategy for engineering bio‐inspired information processing. Herein, a designable van der Waals (vdW) crystal has been demonstrated for device‐scale neuronal cell mimicking. The structural similarity between ion‐channel in biological membranes and layered vdW lattices is realized with nano‐crystallization. As the sequential transition from carrier transport dominance to ion transport dominance reveals the dynamic control over synaptic weight updates. Optoelectric synaptic plasticity in designable vdW crystal (long‐term potentiation and depression, paired‐pulse facilitation, and a tunable short‐term to long‐term memory transition) were conclusively observed and correlates with photo‐induced carrier trapping and ionic migration. Furthermore, learning‐forgetting‐relearning cycles achieve 34.7% increased retention efficiency compared to bulk ReSe2. Functional demonstrations in edge detection and CIFAR‐10 image recognition confirm the synaptic plasticity into system‐level neuromorphic computation, with a recognition accuracy of 96.24%. In conclusion, we envision that designable vdW artificial crystal will provide the versatile advances for 3D stackable neuromorphic vision architectures. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/bb532905-5e02-49d8-8cea-136015c1b11a/adma73595-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Designable van der Waals Crystal for Artificial Neuronal Cell Mimicking"/> <p><b>Designable van der Waals crystal</b> has been demonstrated for <b>device-scale neuronal cell mimicking</b>. The structural similarity between ion-channel in biological membranes and layered vdW lattices is realized with <b>nano-crystallization via Ar + H<sub>2</sub>S plasma sulfurization</b>. As the sequential transition from <b>intralayer migration to coupled intra-/interlayer migration</b> reveals the <b>dynamic control of synaptic weight updates</b>. Optoelectronic synaptic plasticity in designable vdW were conclusively observed and correlates with photo-induced carrier trapping and ionic migration. Furthermore, <b>learning-forgetting-relearning cycles achieves 34.7 % increased retention efficiency</b> compared to bulk ReSe<sub>2</sub>. Functional demonstrations in edge detection and CIFAR-10 image recognition confirm the synaptic plasticity into system-level neuromorphic computation, <b>with a recognition accuracy of 96.24%</b>. In conclusion, our designable vdW crystal will offer the <b>generalizable platform for next-generation 3D integrated neuromorphic device</b> and edge-computing AI hardware. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Optogenetics employs light to regulate neuronal activity with exceptional spatiotemporal precision, thereby enabling the direct modulation of learning and memory processes in the human brain. This capability to externally control neuronal signaling via optical stimuli has provided profound insights into brain function and established a versatile strategy for engineering bio-inspired information processing. Herein, a designable van der Waals (vdW) crystal has been demonstrated for device-scale neuronal cell mimicking. The structural similarity between ion-channel in biological membranes and layered vdW lattices is realized with nano-crystallization. As the sequential transition from carrier transport dominance to ion transport dominance reveals the dynamic control over synaptic weight updates. Optoelectric synaptic plasticity in designable vdW crystal (long-term potentiation and depression, paired-pulse facilitation, and a tunable short-term to long-term memory transition) were conclusively observed and correlates with photo-induced carrier trapping and ionic migration. Furthermore, learning-forgetting-relearning cycles achieve 34.7% increased retention efficiency compared to bulk ReSe<sub>2</sub>. Functional demonstrations in edge detection and CIFAR-10 image recognition confirm the synaptic plasticity into system-level neuromorphic computation, with a recognition accuracy of 96.24%. In conclusion, we envision that designable vdW artificial crystal will provide the versatile advances for 3D stackable neuromorphic vision architectures.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Jinhyoung Lee, Gunhyoung Kim, Dongho Lee, Seowoo Son, Hyunho Seok, Sihoon Son, Hyunbin Choi, Geonwook Kim, Geumji Back, Hyunkyu Kim, Chaerin Park, Junmin Ahn, Seongyun Je, Chaeyoung Im, Junil Cho, Magdalena Grzeszczyk, Seongho Kim, Eunseo Go, Hyunwoo Shim, Donghwan Choi, Muyoung Kim, Hyoeng‐U. Kim, Won‐Jun Jang, Taesung Kim </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Designable van der Waals Crystal for Artificial Neuronal Cell Mimicking</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73595</dc:identifier>

<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73595</prism:doi>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73370?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Wed, 03 Jun 2026 00:14:31 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-03T12:14:31-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73370</guid>
<title>Mixed‐Isomers Strategy for Thermally Stable and High‐Performance Thick‐Film All‐Small‐Molecule Organic Solar Cells</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> A mixed‐isomers guest strategy is proposed to improve the efficiency of all‐small molecule organic solar cells. Introducing the mixed‐isomers (BTP‐Br‐γ and BTP‐Br‐δ) into the MPhS‐C2:BTP‐eC9 binary system collaboratively optimize charge management and energy loss. The all‐small molecule quaternary system delivers a device efficiency of 19.06% and exhibits film‐thickness insensitivity to device efficiency and thermal stability. ABSTRACT All‐small‐molecule organic solar cells (all‐SMOSCs) have emerged as promising candidates for next‐generation photovoltaic technologies due to their advantages in chemical tunability, purification ease, and batch‐to‐batch consistency. Despite significant progress, their power conversion efficiencies (PCEs) still trail those of polymer‐based solar cells (PSCs), primarily due to suboptimal charge management and morphology control. By introducing two structurally similar yet functionally complementary isomers, BTP‐Br‐γ and BTP‐Br‐δ, into a high‐performing host system (MPhS‐C2:BTP‐eC9), herein we construct quaternary blends that exhibit superior morphological robustness, efficient charge transport, and suppressed energy loss. The optimized quaternary device delivers a record PCE of 19.06% (certified at 18.7%), representing the highest value reported for all‐SMOSCs to date. Further investigations reveal that the quaternary devices also exhibit high thickness tolerance to device efficiency and thermal stability, owing to fast exciton dissociation, long carrier diffusion length, low recombination loss, and rapid charge extraction. The work offers a promising strategy to bridge the performance gap between all‐SMOSCs and PSCs, paving the way for their practical deployment in high‐efficiency organic photovoltaics. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/8695f7ff-5c6a-4eb7-adc4-5348a53ee2f6/adma73370-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Mixed-Isomers Strategy for Thermally Stable and High-Performance Thick-Film All-Small-Molecule Organic Solar Cells"/> <p>A mixed-isomers guest strategy is proposed to improve the efficiency of all-small molecule organic solar cells. Introducing the mixed-isomers (BTP-Br-γ and BTP-Br-δ) into the MPhS-C2:BTP-eC9 binary system collaboratively optimize charge management and energy loss. The all-small molecule quaternary system delivers a device efficiency of 19.06% and exhibits film-thickness insensitivity to device efficiency and thermal stability. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>All-small-molecule organic solar cells (all-SMOSCs) have emerged as promising candidates for next-generation photovoltaic technologies due to their advantages in chemical tunability, purification ease, and batch-to-batch consistency. Despite significant progress, their power conversion efficiencies (PCEs) still trail those of polymer-based solar cells (PSCs), primarily due to suboptimal charge management and morphology control. By introducing two structurally similar yet functionally complementary isomers, BTP-Br-γ and BTP-Br-δ, into a high-performing host system (MPhS-C2:BTP-eC9), herein we construct quaternary blends that exhibit superior morphological robustness, efficient charge transport, and suppressed energy loss. The optimized quaternary device delivers a record PCE of 19.06% (certified at 18.7%), representing the highest value reported for all-SMOSCs to date. Further investigations reveal that the quaternary devices also exhibit high thickness tolerance to device efficiency and thermal stability, owing to fast exciton dissociation, long carrier diffusion length, low recombination loss, and rapid charge extraction. The work offers a promising strategy to bridge the performance gap between all-SMOSCs and PSCs, paving the way for their practical deployment in high-efficiency organic photovoltaics.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Yuan Gao, Xinrong Yang, Lin‐yong Xu, Xingyu Chen, Biao Xiao, Jianlong Xia, Rui Sun, Jie Min </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Mixed‐Isomers Strategy for Thermally Stable and High‐Performance Thick‐Film All‐Small‐Molecule Organic Solar Cells</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73370</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73454?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Wed, 03 Jun 2026 00:11:50 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-03T12:11:50-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73454</guid>
<title>Direct Imaging Reveals the Atomic Mechanism of Active‐Site Formation in Nanoclusters for Hydrogen Production</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Active sites emerge dynamically in PtNi nanoclusters, revealed by in situ electron microscopy. An entropy‐stabilized alloy transforms into a Pt‐NiO heterostructure under reaction conditions, driven by oxygen availability and a critical nucleus size. This evolution enables record hydrogen evolution activity, identifying interfacial sites as the origin of enhanced catalytic performance. ABSTRACT Understanding how catalytically active sites emerge and evolve under working conditions is a fundamental challenge that limits the rational design of heterogeneous catalysts. Here, we directly visualize the transformation between alloyed PtNi and phase‐separated Pt‐NiO nanoclusters during hydrogen evolution. Using in situ low‐voltage aberration‐corrected electron microscopy, with the electron beam serving as both the stimulus and probe, we track the formation of active sites under low‐water‐vapor conditions. PtNi nanoclusters were assembled with controlled mixing of the atoms, resulting in two distinct configurational entropy states. Under reaction conditions, the transformation of bimetallic nanoclusters shifts from an entropically stabilized alloy to an enthalpically favored phase‐separated configuration, controlled by oxygen availability and by a critical nucleus size. The atomic dynamics observed in real space correlate directly with catalytic performance, where the low‐entropy Pt‐NiO state achieves a record hydrogen evolution mass activity of 11.1 A/mgPt due to a high density of interfacial sites that promote water dissociation on NiO and efficient hydrogen adsorption on Pt atoms. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/5576cede-9900-4e41-b096-67be104ee264/adma73454-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Direct Imaging Reveals the Atomic Mechanism of Active-Site Formation in Nanoclusters for Hydrogen Production"/> <p>Active sites emerge dynamically in PtNi nanoclusters, revealed by in situ electron microscopy. An entropy-stabilized alloy transforms into a Pt-NiO heterostructure under reaction conditions, driven by oxygen availability and a critical nucleus size. This evolution enables record hydrogen evolution activity, identifying interfacial sites as the origin of enhanced catalytic performance. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Understanding how catalytically active sites emerge and evolve under working conditions is a fundamental challenge that limits the rational design of heterogeneous catalysts. Here, we directly visualize the transformation between alloyed PtNi and phase-separated Pt-NiO nanoclusters during hydrogen evolution. Using in situ low-voltage aberration-corrected electron microscopy, with the electron beam serving as both the stimulus and probe, we track the formation of active sites under low-water-vapor conditions. PtNi nanoclusters were assembled with controlled mixing of the atoms, resulting in two distinct configurational entropy states. Under reaction conditions, the transformation of bimetallic nanoclusters shifts from an entropically stabilized alloy to an enthalpically favored phase-separated configuration, controlled by oxygen availability and by a critical nucleus size. The atomic dynamics observed in real space correlate directly with catalytic performance, where the low-entropy Pt-NiO state achieves a record hydrogen evolution mass activity of 11.1 A/mg<sub>Pt</sub> due to a high density of interfacial sites that promote water dissociation on NiO and efficient hydrogen adsorption on Pt atoms.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Emerson C. Kohlrausch, Christopher Leist, Gazi N. Aliev, Mohsen Danaie, Matthew Young, Madasamy Thangamuthu, Yifan Chen, William J. Cull, Wolfgang Theis, Ute Kaiser, Andrei N. Khlobystov, Jesum Alves Fernandes </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Direct Imaging Reveals the Atomic Mechanism of Active‐Site Formation in Nanoclusters for Hydrogen Production</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73454</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73454</prism:doi>

<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>
...
</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73588?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Wed, 03 Jun 2026 00:09:36 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-03T12:09:36-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73588</guid>
<title>Rational Design of Semiconductor Materials for Photocatalytic Conversion of Methane into Value‐Added Chemicals</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Photocatalytic methane conversion provides a low‐temperature route to upgrade inert CH4 into value‐added chemicals. This Review outlines fundamental principles and evaluation protocols, and highlights rational semiconductor design strategies including interfacial engineering and defect modulation. The interplay between catalyst architecture and reaction microenvironment is emphasized to guide selective C1 and C2+ production and future system optimization. ABSTRACT Photocatalytic methane conversion offers a promising, low‐temperature pathway for upgrading this abundant yet highly inert hydrocarbon into value‐added chemicals, circumventing the energy‐intensive nature of conventional thermocatalytic processes. However, achieving high conversion efficiency and precise product selectivity remains a fundamental challenge. This review systematically summarizes recent advances in the rational design of semiconductor photocatalysts for methane valorization. We first outline the fundamentals of light‐driven CH4 activation and standardized protocols for performance evaluation. We then highlight advancements in material engineering, encompassing metal oxides, carbon nitride, and emerging organic frameworks, with a focus on establishing fundamental structure–activity relationships. Particular emphasis is devoted to structural and interfacial engineering strategies, including cocatalyst integration, morphological control, defect engineering, facet regulation, heterojunction construction, and elemental doping, which cooperatively modulate electronic structures and charge dynamics to enhance catalytic performance. Furthermore, we examine the critical interplay between catalyst architecture and the reaction microenvironment in steering the targeted product selectivity toward specific C1 oxygenates (methanol, formaldehyde, formic acid) and C2 products (ethanol, acetic acid, ethane, and ethylene). Finally, we discuss current bottlenecks and future opportunities, providing a strategic framework to guide the atomic‐level design and practical optimization of next‐generation photocatalytic methane utilization systems. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/15ecfacb-7669-4657-aba5-4d3019348bdf/adma73588-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Rational Design of Semiconductor Materials for Photocatalytic Conversion of Methane into Value-Added Chemicals"/> <p>Photocatalytic methane conversion provides a low-temperature route to upgrade inert CH<sub>4</sub> into value-added chemicals. This Review outlines fundamental principles and evaluation protocols, and highlights rational semiconductor design strategies including interfacial engineering and defect modulation. The interplay between catalyst architecture and reaction microenvironment is emphasized to guide selective C<sub>1</sub> and C<sub>2+</sub> production and future system optimization. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Photocatalytic methane conversion offers a promising, low-temperature pathway for upgrading this abundant yet highly inert hydrocarbon into value-added chemicals, circumventing the energy-intensive nature of conventional thermocatalytic processes. However, achieving high conversion efficiency and precise product selectivity remains a fundamental challenge. This review systematically summarizes recent advances in the rational design of semiconductor photocatalysts for methane valorization. We first outline the fundamentals of light-driven CH<sub>4</sub> activation and standardized protocols for performance evaluation. We then highlight advancements in material engineering, encompassing metal oxides, carbon nitride, and emerging organic frameworks, with a focus on establishing fundamental structure–activity relationships. Particular emphasis is devoted to structural and interfacial engineering strategies, including cocatalyst integration, morphological control, defect engineering, facet regulation, heterojunction construction, and elemental doping, which cooperatively modulate electronic structures and charge dynamics to enhance catalytic performance. Furthermore, we examine the critical interplay between catalyst architecture and the reaction microenvironment in steering the targeted product selectivity toward specific C<sub>1</sub> oxygenates (methanol, formaldehyde, formic acid) and C<sub>2</sub> products (ethanol, acetic acid, ethane, and ethylene). Finally, we discuss current bottlenecks and future opportunities, providing a strategic framework to guide the atomic-level design and practical optimization of next-generation photocatalytic methane utilization systems.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Kai Sun, Benxing Gao, Hui Song, Jinhua Ye </dc:creator>
<category>REVIEW</category>
<dc:title>Rational Design of Semiconductor Materials for Photocatalytic Conversion of Methane into Value‐Added Chemicals</dc:title>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73551?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Wed, 03 Jun 2026 00:08:11 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-03T12:08:11-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73551</guid>
<title>Accelerating Charge‐Transfer Kinetics via Triggering Electron Spin Polarization in Open‐Hollow MoS2 Nanospheres for Ultrafast Lithium Storage</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Triggering electron spin polarization via atomic Co incorporation is proposed to accelerate charge transfer kinetics of MoS2 electrodes across full lithiation process. By combining operando characterization with theoretical calculations, we demonstrate that triggering electron spin polarization decreases insertion‐conversion energy barrier, accelerate charge transport at Co/Li2S interfaces, and construct a LiF‐enriched SEI, enabling ultrafast charging of MoS2 electrodes in lithium‐ion batteries. ABSTRACT The development of ultrafast‐charging MoS2 anodes is fundamentally constrained by sluggish charge‐transfer kinetics. This study addresses this limitation by triggering electron spin polarization through atomic‐scale Co doping within open‐hollow MoS2 nanospheres. Structurally, the open‐hollow design minimizes ion diffusion distances and accommodates volume changes of MoS2, providing a robust foundation for rapid ion flux. Electronically, Co incorporation enhances S 3p–Mo 4d–Co 3d orbital hybridization, triggering a marked electron spin polarization that reduces kinetic barriers for insertion and conversion reactions. Post‐conversion stage, in situ generated metallic Co nanoparticles (Co0) act as dynamic mediators for spin‐polarized electron transfer. Specifically, the injection of spin‐polarized electrons into Co° creates a spin‐polarized surface capacitance, boosting charge storage at the Co0/Li2S interfaces. Conversely, the release of these electrons promotes a Co0‐catalyzed construction of solid electrolyte interphase, prioritizing conductive LiF species while suppressing Li2CO3, facilitating ion transport at the electrode‐electrolyte interfaces. Consequently, the MoS2‐based anode exhibits an impressive ultrafast‐charging capability of 30 C (1044.1 mAh g−1) and maintains stability over 10 000 cycles at 15 C with a final capacity of 874.7 mAh g−1. This work demonstrates that triggering electron spin polarization represents a transformative approach to overcoming kinetic barriers in next‐generation ultrafast‐charging batteries. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/5f824f89-2cd0-4756-9f50-f7279e01b7be/adma73551-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Accelerating Charge-Transfer Kinetics via Triggering Electron Spin Polarization in Open-Hollow MoS2 Nanospheres for Ultrafast Lithium Storage"/> <p>Triggering electron spin polarization via atomic Co incorporation is proposed to accelerate charge transfer kinetics of MoS<sub>2</sub> electrodes across full lithiation process. By combining operando characterization with theoretical calculations, we demonstrate that triggering electron spin polarization decreases insertion-conversion energy barrier, accelerate charge transport at Co/Li<sub>2</sub>S interfaces, and construct a LiF-enriched SEI, enabling ultrafast charging of MoS<sub>2</sub> electrodes in lithium-ion batteries. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>The development of ultrafast-charging MoS<sub>2</sub> anodes is fundamentally constrained by sluggish charge-transfer kinetics. This study addresses this limitation by triggering electron spin polarization through atomic-scale Co doping within open-hollow MoS<sub>2</sub> nanospheres. Structurally, the open-hollow design minimizes ion diffusion distances and accommodates volume changes of MoS<sub>2</sub>, providing a robust foundation for rapid ion flux. Electronically, Co incorporation enhances S 3p–Mo 4d–Co 3d orbital hybridization, triggering a marked electron spin polarization that reduces kinetic barriers for insertion and conversion reactions. Post-conversion stage, in situ generated metallic Co nanoparticles (Co<sup>0</sup>) act as dynamic mediators for spin-polarized electron transfer. Specifically, the injection of spin-polarized electrons into Co° creates a spin-polarized surface capacitance, boosting charge storage at the Co<sup>0</sup>/Li<sub>2</sub>S interfaces. Conversely, the release of these electrons promotes a Co<sup>0</sup>-catalyzed construction of solid electrolyte interphase, prioritizing conductive LiF species while suppressing Li<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>, facilitating ion transport at the electrode-electrolyte interfaces. Consequently, the MoS<sub>2</sub>-based anode exhibits an impressive ultrafast-charging capability of 30 C (1044.1 mAh g<sup>−1</sup>) and maintains stability over 10 000 cycles at 15 C with a final capacity of 874.7 mAh g<sup>−1</sup>. This work demonstrates that triggering electron spin polarization represents a transformative approach to overcoming kinetic barriers in next-generation ultrafast-charging batteries.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Kunxiong Zheng, Hengyuan Hu, Zhiyu Zou, Yuankai Huang, Haolin Ju, Yongbiao Mu, Wenjia Li, Lei Wei, Lin Zeng, Tianshou Zhao, Meisheng Han </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Accelerating Charge‐Transfer Kinetics via Triggering Electron Spin Polarization in Open‐Hollow MoS2 Nanospheres for Ultrafast Lithium Storage</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73551</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73551</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73551?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73582?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Wed, 03 Jun 2026 00:06:47 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-03T12:06:47-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73582</guid>
<title>Switching Water Oxidation Pathway via NiFe Dual‐Atoms on BiVO4: An *O─O* Coupling Mechanism Route to Bypass Adsorbate Evolution Mechanism Limitations</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> The NiFe DAs/TiOx/BiVO4 photoanode demonstrates a high photocurrent of 6.13 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE for 150 h, which shifts the oxygen evolution mechanism from adsorbate evolution mechanism (AEM) in Ni (Fe) SAs TiOx/BiVO4 to *O─O* coupling mechanism (OCM) pathway, providing atomic‐level mechanistic insights for efficient photoelectrochemical water oxidation. ABSTRACT Dual‐atom catalysts (DACs) with heterogeneous active sites represent an emerging frontier in photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting. However, the corresponding reaction mechanism on DACs is still unclear. Herein, we present a photoanode architecture comprising NiFe dual atoms (DAs) anchored on a TiOx‐coated BiVO4 photoanode (NiFe DAs/TiOx/BiVO4), which delivers an impressive photocurrent density of 6.13 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE, sustained stability exceeding 150 h, and an applied bias photon‐to‐current efficiency of 2.2%. Our investigation reveals a dual functionality of the NiFe DAs which serve as an efficient oxygen evolution cocatalyst and enhance charge separation—an aspect largely overlooked in previous studies. Through in situ spectroscopic investigations combined with density functional theory calculations, we elucidate that the NiFe DAs/TiOx/BiVO4 enables a mechanistic shift from the conventional adsorbate evolution mechanism (AEM) observed in single atom‐modified‐TiOx/BiVO4 to an *O─O* coupling mechanism (OCM). Specifically, this OCM pathway bypasses the formation of *OOH and produces the *O─O* bridging species, broking the *OOH/*OH scaling limitation in the AEM pathway. This work uncovers PEC water oxidation mechanism at the atomic level, establishing a foundational framework for designing high‐performance photoelectrodes through precise atomic‐scale engineering. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/b161ee38-d860-4cc2-a64c-b4143754f587/adma73582-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Switching Water Oxidation Pathway via NiFe Dual-Atoms on BiVO4: An *O─O* Coupling Mechanism Route to Bypass Adsorbate Evolution Mechanism Limitations"/> <p>The NiFe DAs/TiO<sub>x</sub>/BiVO<sub>4</sub> photoanode demonstrates a high photocurrent of 6.13 mA cm<sup>−2</sup> at 1.23 V<sub>RHE</sub> for 150 h, which shifts the oxygen evolution mechanism from adsorbate evolution mechanism (AEM) in Ni (Fe) SAs TiO<sub>x</sub>/BiVO<sub>4</sub> to *O─O* coupling mechanism (OCM) pathway, providing atomic-level mechanistic insights for efficient photoelectrochemical water oxidation. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Dual-atom catalysts (DACs) with heterogeneous active sites represent an emerging frontier in photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting. However, the corresponding reaction mechanism on DACs is still unclear. Herein, we present a photoanode architecture comprising NiFe dual atoms (DAs) anchored on a TiO<sub>x</sub>-coated BiVO<sub>4</sub> photoanode (NiFe DAs/TiO<sub>x</sub>/BiVO<sub>4</sub>), which delivers an impressive photocurrent density of 6.13 mA cm<sup>−2</sup> at 1.23 V<sub>RHE</sub>, sustained stability exceeding 150 h, and an applied bias photon-to-current efficiency of 2.2%. Our investigation reveals a dual functionality of the NiFe DAs which serve as an efficient oxygen evolution cocatalyst and enhance charge separation—an aspect largely overlooked in previous studies. Through in situ spectroscopic investigations combined with density functional theory calculations, we elucidate that the NiFe DAs/TiO<sub>x</sub>/BiVO<sub>4</sub> enables a mechanistic shift from the conventional adsorbate evolution mechanism (AEM) observed in single atom-modified-TiO<sub>x</sub>/BiVO<sub>4</sub> to an *O─O* coupling mechanism (OCM). Specifically, this OCM pathway bypasses the formation of *OOH and produces the *O─O* bridging species, broking the *OOH/*OH scaling limitation in the AEM pathway. This work uncovers PEC water oxidation mechanism at the atomic level, establishing a foundational framework for designing high-performance photoelectrodes through precise atomic-scale engineering.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Yan Zhang, Zhongrui Min, Hao Jin, Ru Wan, Jiaming Zhang, Fengtao Fan, Sheng Ye </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Switching Water Oxidation Pathway via NiFe Dual‐Atoms on BiVO4: An *O─O* Coupling Mechanism Route to Bypass Adsorbate Evolution Mechanism Limitations</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73582</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73582</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73582?af=R</prism:url>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73577?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Wed, 03 Jun 2026 00:05:14 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-03T12:05:14-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73577</guid>
<title>Uncovering Local Piezoelectric Field Effect in Mechanoluminescent Materials</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> By exploring the differential luminescence responses under various stimuli, this study quantitatively links macroscopic piezoelectric fields to local crystal structure, thereby providing crystallographic experimental evidence for uncovering local piezoelectric fields in mechanoluminescent materials. ABSTRACT Elastic mechanoluminescent (ML) materials have significant potential in intelligent sensing, dynamic displays, and artificial intelligence. However, concrete experimental evidence of localized piezoelectric fields has long been missing in piezoelectrically controlled elastic ML materials. In this work, we propose a strategy for uncovering the local piezoelectric field effect in ML materials and demonstrate that crystallographic site symmetry and local structure determine the local piezoelectric field, which further profoundly influences the ML properties of the material. We screen and design multisite deep‑red to near‑infrared (NIR) self‑recoverable ML materials, Mn2+‐activated MGa2S4 (M = Ca, Sr). Beyond the report of ultrabroadband ML performance, we have defined and calculated, for the first time, the centrosymmetry deviation degree (CDD) of a polyhedron, and elucidated the correlation among site symmetry, CDD, polyhedral distortion degree, local piezoelectric responses, and ML intensity. With the assistance of DFT and DFPT calculations, we have clarified the contribution of local polarization at specific sites and established a dynamic model for local‑piezoelectric‑field‑induced ML in multisite materials. Furthermore, leveraging the ultrabroadband ML emission of this material series, we fabricated various flexible devices, which demonstrated promising application potential in fields such as rehabilitation medicine, deep‐tissue bioimaging, and NIR information encryption. This work holds potential to offer valuable insights for discovering, designing, and yielding novel ML materials. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/417d8ae0-a226-4cd1-91a8-f67a2f6f613c/adma73577-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Uncovering Local Piezoelectric Field Effect in Mechanoluminescent Materials"/> <p>By exploring the differential luminescence responses under various stimuli, this study quantitatively links macroscopic piezoelectric fields to local crystal structure, thereby providing crystallographic experimental evidence for uncovering local piezoelectric fields in mechanoluminescent materials. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Elastic mechanoluminescent (ML) materials have significant potential in intelligent sensing, dynamic displays, and artificial intelligence. However, concrete experimental evidence of localized piezoelectric fields has long been missing in piezoelectrically controlled elastic ML materials. In this work, we propose a strategy for uncovering the local piezoelectric field effect in ML materials and demonstrate that crystallographic site symmetry and local structure determine the local piezoelectric field, which further profoundly influences the ML properties of the material. We screen and design multisite deep‑red to near‑infrared (NIR) self‑recoverable ML materials, Mn<sup>2+</sup>-activated MGa<sub>2</sub>S<sub>4</sub> (M = Ca, Sr). Beyond the report of ultrabroadband ML performance, we have defined and calculated, for the first time, the centrosymmetry deviation degree (<i>CDD</i>) of a polyhedron, and elucidated the correlation among site symmetry, <i>CDD</i>, polyhedral distortion degree, local piezoelectric responses, and ML intensity. With the assistance of DFT and DFPT calculations, we have clarified the contribution of local polarization at specific sites and established a dynamic model for local‑piezoelectric‑field‑induced ML in multisite materials. Furthermore, leveraging the ultrabroadband ML emission of this material series, we fabricated various flexible devices, which demonstrated promising application potential in fields such as rehabilitation medicine, deep-tissue bioimaging, and NIR information encryption. This work holds potential to offer valuable insights for discovering, designing, and yielding novel ML materials.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Yuhe Shao, Jia Shi, Hongzhen Liu, Zhen Song, Quanlin Liu </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Uncovering Local Piezoelectric Field Effect in Mechanoluminescent Materials</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73577</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73577</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73577?af=R</prism:url>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73520?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Wed, 03 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-03T12:00:00-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73520</guid>
<title>Observation of Moiré Trapped Biexciton Through Sub‐Diffraction‐Limit Probing Using Hetero‐Bilayer on Nanopillar</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> This study demonstrates stable moiré biexciton within a strained hetero‐bilayer system, observation of which is made possible by engineering a sub‐diffraction‐limit probing technique. The exciton–exciton exchange interaction overcomes the repulsive dipolar interaction at close inter‐exciton distances, which stabilizes two interacting excitons within a single moiré pocket to a stable biexciton state. ABSTRACT The ability to tune the degree of interaction among particles at the nanoscale is highly intriguing. The spectroscopic signature of such interaction is often subtle and requires special probes to observe. To this end, inter‐layer excitons trapped in the periodic potential wells of a moiré superlattice offer rich interaction physics, specifically due to the presence of both attractive and repulsive components in the interaction. Here we show that the Coulomb force between two inter‐layer excitons switches from repulsive to attractive when the length scale reduces from inter‐moiré‐pocket to intra‐moiré‐pocket in a WS2/WSe2 hetero‐bilayer ‐ thanks to the complex competition between direct and exchange interaction. The finding is a departure from the usual notion of repelling inter‐layer excitons due to layer polarization. This manifests as the simultaneous observation of an anomalous superlinear power‐law of moiré exciton and a stabilization of moiré trapped biexciton. The experimental observation is facilitated by placing the hetero‐bilayer on a polymer‐nanopillar/gold‐film stack which significantly reduces the inhomogeneous spectral broadening by selectively probing a smaller ensemble of moiré pockets compared with a flat sample. This creates an interesting platform to explore interaction among moiré trapped excitons and higher order quasiparticles. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/dfd517ac-5ee1-4e11-bd47-19240a690589/adma73520-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Observation of Moiré Trapped Biexciton Through Sub-Diffraction-Limit Probing Using Hetero-Bilayer on Nanopillar"/> <p>This study demonstrates stable moiré biexciton within a strained hetero-bilayer system, observation of which is made possible by engineering a sub-diffraction-limit probing technique. The exciton–exciton exchange interaction overcomes the repulsive dipolar interaction at close inter-exciton distances, which stabilizes two interacting excitons within a single moiré pocket to a stable biexciton state. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>The ability to tune the degree of interaction among particles at the nanoscale is highly intriguing. The spectroscopic signature of such interaction is often subtle and requires special probes to observe. To this end, inter-layer excitons trapped in the periodic potential wells of a moiré superlattice offer rich interaction physics, specifically due to the presence of both attractive and repulsive components in the interaction. Here we show that the Coulomb force between two inter-layer excitons switches from repulsive to attractive when the length scale reduces from inter-moiré-pocket to intra-moiré-pocket in a WS<sub>2</sub>/WSe<sub>2</sub> hetero-bilayer - thanks to the complex competition between direct and exchange interaction. The finding is a departure from the usual notion of repelling inter-layer excitons due to layer polarization. This manifests as the simultaneous observation of an anomalous superlinear power-law of moiré exciton and a stabilization of moiré trapped biexciton. The experimental observation is facilitated by placing the hetero-bilayer on a polymer-nanopillar/gold-film stack which significantly reduces the inhomogeneous spectral broadening by selectively probing a smaller ensemble of moiré pockets compared with a flat sample. This creates an interesting platform to explore interaction among moiré trapped excitons and higher order quasiparticles.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Mayank Chhaperwal, Suman Chatterjee, Suchithra Puliyassery, Jyothsna Konkada Manattayil, Rabindra Biswas, Patrick Hays, Seth Ariel Tongay, Varun Raghunathan, Kausik Majumdar </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Observation of Moiré Trapped Biexciton Through Sub‐Diffraction‐Limit Probing Using Hetero‐Bilayer on Nanopillar</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73520</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73520</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73520?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>
...
</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73545?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 07:54:56 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T07:54:56-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73545</guid>
<title>Ordered–Disorder Transition Induced Stretchable Collagen Ionic Gels Toward Flexible Sensors</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> This work employs Hofmeister‐ion engineering to disrupt the molecular ordered structure of collagen in its aggregation structure. This entropy‐driven disordering strategy transforms natural collagen into a highly stretchable and conductive ionic gel. The resulting material exhibits exceptional elasticity, durable ionic conductivity, and reliable sensing performance, providing a versatile platform for flexible electronics and implantable systems. ABSTRACT Natural structural proteins such as collagen offer exceptional biocompatibility and mechanical versatility, making them promising candidates for wearable and implantable sensing applications. However, achieving integrated high stretchability, stable electrical performance, and long‐term durability in protein‐based sensing materials remains a critical challenge. Conventional modification strategies are largely confined to molecular‐scale adjustments, which often fail to simultaneously address these functional demands. This study introduces a fundamentally different approach by leveraging Hofmeister‐ion engineering to directly regulate the aggregation structure of tropocollagen, which enables a structural transformation from long‐range order to entropy‐driven disorder. Unlike previous methods that rely on chemical crosslinking or exogenous additives, our strategy selectively disrupts collagen's supramolecular order while preserving its triple‐helical integrity, resulting in a highly stretchable and conductive ionic gel with exceptional elasticity (fracture strain 560.00%, elastic range 300.53%, resilience 97.36%), durable ionic conductivity (0.56 S m−1), and reliable sensing performance over 1500 tensile cycles. By establishing a new paradigm of mesoscale disordering, this work not only deepens the understanding of ion‐modulated hierarchical assembly but also provides a versatile, biocompatible platform for next‐generation flexible electronics, soft robotics, and implantable sensing systems. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/81ab6628-39c0-4c1d-b260-dfa4a1f08e88/adma73545-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Ordered–Disorder Transition Induced Stretchable Collagen Ionic Gels Toward Flexible Sensors"/> <p>This work employs Hofmeister-ion engineering to disrupt the molecular ordered structure of collagen in its aggregation structure. This entropy-driven disordering strategy transforms natural collagen into a highly stretchable and conductive ionic gel. The resulting material exhibits exceptional elasticity, durable ionic conductivity, and reliable sensing performance, providing a versatile platform for flexible electronics and implantable systems. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Natural structural proteins such as collagen offer exceptional biocompatibility and mechanical versatility, making them promising candidates for wearable and implantable sensing applications. However, achieving integrated high stretchability, stable electrical performance, and long-term durability in protein-based sensing materials remains a critical challenge. Conventional modification strategies are largely confined to molecular-scale adjustments, which often fail to simultaneously address these functional demands. This study introduces a fundamentally different approach by leveraging Hofmeister-ion engineering to directly regulate the aggregation structure of tropocollagen, which enables a structural transformation from long-range order to entropy-driven disorder. Unlike previous methods that rely on chemical crosslinking or exogenous additives, our strategy selectively disrupts collagen's supramolecular order while preserving its triple-helical integrity, resulting in a highly stretchable and conductive ionic gel with exceptional elasticity (fracture strain 560.00%, elastic range 300.53%, resilience 97.36%), durable ionic conductivity (0.56 S m<sup>−1</sup>), and reliable sensing performance over 1500 tensile cycles. By establishing a new paradigm of mesoscale disordering, this work not only deepens the understanding of ion-modulated hierarchical assembly but also provides a versatile, biocompatible platform for next-generation flexible electronics, soft robotics, and implantable sensing systems.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Ying Pei, Jiaqi Li, Lele Liu, Yuwei Zhao, Weifang Yang, Keyong Tang, Ang Lu </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Ordered–Disorder Transition Induced Stretchable Collagen Ionic Gels Toward Flexible Sensors</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73545</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73545</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73545?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>
...
</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73473?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 07:45:06 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T07:45:06-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73473</guid>
<title>Breaking the Voltage‐Loss Bottleneck in Organic Photovoltaics via Interfacial Molecular Orientation Engineering</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> A precisely controlled bilayer system using a polydimethylsiloxane film‐transfer method reveals the intrinsic role of donor‐acceptor interfacial molecular orientation in governing voltage loss (Vloss) in organic photovoltaics (OPVs). Non‐radiative voltage loss (ΔV3) increases monotonically face‐on/face‐on, edge‐on/face‐on, face‐on/edge‐on to edge‐on/edge‐on configurations, highlighting donor‐acceptor interfacial orientation as a crucial factor for minimizing ΔV3 and Vloss in OPVs. ABSTRACT Benefiting from non‐fullerene acceptors, organic photovoltaics (OPVs) have achieved power conversion efficiencies exceeding 21%. However, further progress critically depends on suppressing voltage losses (Vloss), particularly non‐radiative voltage losses (ΔV3). Here, we employ a precisely controlled donor/acceptor bilayer system, fabricated via a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) film‐transfer method, to systematically investigate the intrinsic role of interfacial molecular orientation in governing Vloss. Devices with a face‐on/face‐on (P5TCN‐F0/Y6) configuration exhibit markedly reduced ΔV3 and overall Vloss compared with edge‐on/face‐on (P5TCN‐F50/Y6), face‐on/edge‐on (P5TCN‐F0/BTP‐2T), and edge‐on/edge‐on (P5TCN‐F50/BTP‐2T) counterparts. Transient absorption spectroscopy and time‐resolved photoluminescence measurements reveal that the face‐on/face‐on interface enables efficient charge transfer while suppressing charge‐transfer (CT) states recombination. Quantitative analysis further demonstrates that the markedly reduced non‐radiative recombination rate of the CT states is responsible for the enhanced electroluminescence quantum efficiency and the reduced ΔV3 and Vloss. These findings establish interfacial molecular orientation as a key lever for minimizing ΔV3 without compromising high photocurrent and provide material design principles and morphology control requirements for further breaking the performance bottleneck in bulk heterojunction OPVs. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/f2ab00d8-b5b8-4f2f-838f-9636bf46ddf3/adma73473-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Breaking the Voltage-Loss Bottleneck in Organic Photovoltaics via Interfacial Molecular Orientation Engineering"/> <p>A precisely controlled bilayer system using a polydimethylsiloxane film-transfer method reveals the intrinsic role of donor-acceptor interfacial molecular orientation in governing voltage loss (<i>V</i> <sub>loss</sub>) in organic photovoltaics (OPVs). Non-radiative voltage loss (Δ<i>V</i> <sub>3</sub>) increases monotonically face-on/face-on, edge-on/face-on, face-on/edge-on to edge-on/edge-on configurations, highlighting donor-acceptor interfacial orientation as a crucial factor for minimizing Δ<i>V</i> <sub>3</sub> and <i>V</i> <sub>loss</sub> in OPVs. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Benefiting from non-fullerene acceptors, organic photovoltaics (OPVs) have achieved power conversion efficiencies exceeding 21%. However, further progress critically depends on suppressing voltage losses (<i>V</i> <sub>loss</sub>), particularly non-radiative voltage losses (Δ<i>V</i> <sub>3</sub>). Here, we employ a precisely controlled donor/acceptor bilayer system, fabricated via a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) film-transfer method, to systematically investigate the intrinsic role of interfacial molecular orientation in governing <i>V</i> <sub>loss</sub>. Devices with a face-on/face-on (P5TCN-F0/Y6) configuration exhibit markedly reduced Δ<i>V</i> <sub>3</sub> and overall <i>V</i> <sub>loss</sub> compared with edge-on/face-on (P5TCN-F50/Y6), face-on/edge-on (P5TCN-F0/BTP-2T), and edge-on/edge-on (P5TCN-F50/BTP-2T) counterparts. Transient absorption spectroscopy and time-resolved photoluminescence measurements reveal that the face-on/face-on interface enables efficient charge transfer while suppressing charge-transfer (CT) states recombination. Quantitative analysis further demonstrates that the markedly reduced non-radiative recombination rate of the CT states is responsible for the enhanced electroluminescence quantum efficiency and the reduced Δ<i>V</i> <sub>3</sub> and <i>V</i> <sub>loss</sub>. These findings establish interfacial molecular orientation as a key lever for minimizing Δ<i>V</i> <sub>3</sub> without compromising high photocurrent and provide material design principles and morphology control requirements for further breaking the performance bottleneck in bulk heterojunction OPVs.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Weiwei Wu, Rui Sun, Xiaohei Wu, Biao Xiao, Yunlong Ma, Xiyue Yuan, Chunhui Duan, Chaohua Cui, Qingdong Zheng, Jie Min </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Breaking the Voltage‐Loss Bottleneck in Organic Photovoltaics via Interfacial Molecular Orientation Engineering</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73473</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>

<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73473?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73600?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 04:06:45 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T04:06:45-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73600</guid>
<title>Stabilization of Unconventional Body‐Centered Tetragonal Phase in Copper Nanowires for Efficient Carbon Dioxide Electroreduction to Multi‐Carbon Products</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> The internal strain in five‐fold twinned copper nanowires, induced by the need to fill a solid angle deficiency of 7.35°, triggers a phase transformation from face‐centered cubic to body‐centered tetragonal. This phase transformation alters the electronic state of copper nanowires, thereby enhancing their catalytic performance in carbon dioxide electroreduction to high‐value multi‐carbon products compared with common face‐centered cubic copper nanostructures. ABSTRACT Copper nanomaterials with the common face‐centered cubic (fcc) phase have been widely used in the electrocatalytic carbon dioxide (CO2) reduction reaction (CO2RR). However, copper with an unconventional phase is rarely reported as it is thermodynamically unfavorable. Here, through analyzing the strain within copper nanowires, we reveal the phase transition of copper from fcc to body‐centered tetragonal (bct)/fcc heterophase. By systematically investigating copper nanowires with different diameters and copper nanocubes in CO2RR, we explain the relationship between their crystal phase and catalytic performance. Compared with the standard fcc lattice, copper nanowires’ surfaces have different electron states due to a phase transition. Copper nanowires with a diameter of about 30 nm exhibit the optimum catalytic performance, and their Faradaic efficiency of multi‐carbon products is much higher than that of fcc copper nanocubes. Theoretical calculations have demonstrated that the presence of the strained bct phase induces significant upshifts of the d‐band center, which not only improves the overall electroactivity but also optimize the C‐C couplings, leading to improved Faradaic efficiency of multi‐carbon products during CO2RR. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/6475b5dd-2a86-4fbc-8ba6-06fdcb6e3c6b/adma73600-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Stabilization of Unconventional Body-Centered Tetragonal Phase in Copper Nanowires for Efficient Carbon Dioxide Electroreduction to Multi-Carbon Products"/> <p>The internal strain in five-fold twinned copper nanowires, induced by the need to fill a solid angle deficiency of 7.35°, triggers a phase transformation from face-centered cubic to body-centered tetragonal. This phase transformation alters the electronic state of copper nanowires, thereby enhancing their catalytic performance in carbon dioxide electroreduction to high-value multi-carbon products compared with common face-centered cubic copper nanostructures. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Copper nanomaterials with the common face-centered cubic (fcc) phase have been widely used in the electrocatalytic carbon dioxide (CO<sub>2</sub>) reduction reaction (CO<sub>2</sub>RR). However, copper with an unconventional phase is rarely reported as it is thermodynamically unfavorable. Here, through analyzing the strain within copper nanowires, we reveal the phase transition of copper from fcc to body-centered tetragonal (bct)/fcc heterophase. By systematically investigating copper nanowires with different diameters and copper nanocubes in CO<sub>2</sub>RR, we explain the relationship between their crystal phase and catalytic performance. Compared with the standard fcc lattice, copper nanowires’ surfaces have different electron states due to a phase transition. Copper nanowires with a diameter of about 30 nm exhibit the optimum catalytic performance, and their Faradaic efficiency of multi-carbon products is much higher than that of fcc copper nanocubes. Theoretical calculations have demonstrated that the presence of the strained bct phase induces significant upshifts of the <i>d</i>-band center, which not only improves the overall electroactivity but also optimize the C-C couplings, leading to improved Faradaic efficiency of multi-carbon products during CO<sub>2</sub>RR.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Guozhi Wang, Yangbo Ma, Mingzi Sun, Fengkun Hao, Qianhui Wei, Yunhao Wang, Juan Wang, Fu Liu, Xiang Meng, Liang Guo, Mingzheng Shao, Chaohui Wang, Shuheng Hao, Pengyi Lu, Yuecheng Xiong, Yanwei Lum, Shengqi Chu, Bolong Huang, Zhanxi Fan </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Stabilization of Unconventional Body‐Centered Tetragonal Phase in Copper Nanowires for Efficient Carbon Dioxide Electroreduction to Multi‐Carbon Products</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73600</dc:identifier>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73597?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 04:04:30 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T04:04:30-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73597</guid>
<title>Temperature Field‐Driven Directional Space‐Confined Crystallization of Perovskite Melt Toward High‐Performance Optoelectronic Devices</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> A low‐cost, environmentally friendly, and high‐throughput perovskite fabrication method is pioneered via a temperature field‐driven directional space‐confined melt crystallization strategy, realizing the universal integration of high‐crystallinity perovskite single‐crystal films and high‐quality 2D‐3D perovskite heterojunctions, yielding high‐performance optical synaptic devices with linear polarization light response and high‐performance photodetectors with responsivity of 1.19 × 103 A W−1 and response speed of 160 µs. ABSTRACT The development of optoelectronic devices relies heavily on the advancement of semiconductor materials and microfabrication technologies. Notably, metal halide perovskites with superior optoelectronic properties show great prospects for optical sensing technology. However, the current processing techniques of perovskite materials are mainly solution methods and vapor‐phase methods, facing toxic solvents and material waste, which are harmful to the natural environment and human health. Herein, we originally developed the temperature field‐driven directional space‐confined melt crystallization strategy, which perfectly solved the problems of toxic solvent and material waste, yielding highly crystalline, purely oriented 2D perovskite single‐crystal films. Long‐range‐oriented single‐crystal films with fewer crystal domains and defects ensure that optical synaptic devices possess image encryption and high‐fidelity polarization imaging capabilities. Furthermore, the melt processing technique enables the fabrication of high‐quality 2D‐3D perovskite heterojunctions, which notably improve the photodetector performance of 2D perovskite. This pioneering work provides a new strategy and opportunity for the environmentally friendly, high‐throughput integration of perovskite optoelectronic devices. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/6fb53c3d-0926-448d-b47c-2969056da92c/adma73597-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Temperature Field-Driven Directional Space-Confined Crystallization of Perovskite Melt Toward High-Performance Optoelectronic Devices"/> <p>A low-cost, environmentally friendly, and high-throughput perovskite fabrication method is pioneered via a temperature field-driven directional space-confined melt crystallization strategy, realizing the universal integration of high-crystallinity perovskite single-crystal films and high-quality 2D-3D perovskite heterojunctions, yielding high-performance optical synaptic devices with linear polarization light response and high-performance photodetectors with responsivity of 1.19 × 10<sup>3</sup> A W<sup>−1</sup> and response speed of 160 µs. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>The development of optoelectronic devices relies heavily on the advancement of semiconductor materials and microfabrication technologies. Notably, metal halide perovskites with superior optoelectronic properties show great prospects for optical sensing technology. However, the current processing techniques of perovskite materials are mainly solution methods and vapor-phase methods, facing toxic solvents and material waste, which are harmful to the natural environment and human health. Herein, we originally developed the temperature field-driven directional space-confined melt crystallization strategy, which perfectly solved the problems of toxic solvent and material waste, yielding highly crystalline, purely oriented 2D perovskite single-crystal films. Long-range-oriented single-crystal films with fewer crystal domains and defects ensure that optical synaptic devices possess image encryption and high-fidelity polarization imaging capabilities. Furthermore, the melt processing technique enables the fabrication of high-quality 2D-3D perovskite heterojunctions, which notably improve the photodetector performance of 2D perovskite. This pioneering work provides a new strategy and opportunity for the environmentally friendly, high-throughput integration of perovskite optoelectronic devices.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Yi Hao, Kaixin Dong, Xinzhang Lin, Junjie Liu, Zhonghao Wei, Jinjin Zhao, Jingyuan Zhang, Xiangxiang Yang, Dan Li, Jingcheng Hu, Kaiqing Jiang, Mengru Zhang, Jiawang Zou, Zihao Zhu, Kebin Lin, Lei Jiang, Yuchen Wu, Yanlin Song, Yingjie Zhao </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Temperature Field‐Driven Directional Space‐Confined Crystallization of Perovskite Melt Toward High‐Performance Optoelectronic Devices</dc:title>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73561?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 03:06:59 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T03:06:59-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73561</guid>
<title>2D Perovskite Engineering Enables Robust Self‐Assembled Monolayers for High‐Performance Perovskite Solar Cells</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> An in situ interfacial protection strategy stabilizes self‐assembled monolayers (SAMs) via transient 2D perovskite formation, suppressing interfacial degradation and enabling uniform crystallization. This approach delivers high‐efficiency, large‐area perovskite modules (30 cm × 30 cm) with improved operational stability, providing a scalable pathway toward reliable and commercially viable perovskite photovoltaics. ABSTRACT Self‐assembled monolayers (SAMs) are critical hole‐selective contacts in inverted perovskite solar cells (PSCs), yet their practical implementation is hindered by desorption and aggregation during solution‐based perovskite deposition. Here, we report a transient two‐dimensional (2D) perovskite protection strategy using volatile propylammonium chloride, which temporarily shields SAMs from solvent‐induced degradation and subsequently decouples during thermal annealing. This mechanism preserves the integrity of the SAM interface while enabling uniform crystallization of large‐area perovskite films and efficient charge extraction. Consequently, PSCs achieve a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 26.14% for 0.05 cm2 devices, 23.27% for a 5 × 5 cm2 mini‐module, and 22.34% for a 30 × 30 cm2 module (615.7 cm2 active area). Notably, the large‐area module retains 90% of its initial efficiency after 2000 h of continuous maximum power point operation, demonstrating both high performance and operational stability, highlighting a scalable route for reliable perovskite photovoltaics. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/d7c06d1e-3964-49b1-a81d-2a9b89d48076/adma73561-gra-0001-m.png" alt="2D Perovskite Engineering Enables Robust Self-Assembled Monolayers for High-Performance Perovskite Solar Cells"/> <p>An in situ interfacial protection strategy stabilizes self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) via transient 2D perovskite formation, suppressing interfacial degradation and enabling uniform crystallization. This approach delivers high-efficiency, large-area perovskite modules (30 cm × 30 cm) with improved operational stability, providing a scalable pathway toward reliable and commercially viable perovskite photovoltaics. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) are critical hole-selective contacts in inverted perovskite solar cells (PSCs), yet their practical implementation is hindered by desorption and aggregation during solution-based perovskite deposition. Here, we report a transient two-dimensional (2D) perovskite protection strategy using volatile propylammonium chloride, which temporarily shields SAMs from solvent-induced degradation and subsequently decouples during thermal annealing. This mechanism preserves the integrity of the SAM interface while enabling uniform crystallization of large-area perovskite films and efficient charge extraction. Consequently, PSCs achieve a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 26.14% for 0.05 cm<sup>2</sup> devices, 23.27% for a 5 × 5 cm<sup>2</sup> mini-module, and 22.34% for a 30 × 30 cm<sup>2</sup> module (615.7 cm<sup>2</sup> active area). Notably, the large-area module retains 90% of its initial efficiency after 2000 h of continuous maximum power point operation, demonstrating both high performance and operational stability, highlighting a scalable route for reliable perovskite photovoltaics.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Zihan Gu, Kaiyu Wang, Ruigang Yan, Guang Yang, Xingyu Gao, Qingxun Guo, Yingdong Xia, Zhelu Hu, Shuhong Xie, Lingfeng Chao, Yonghua Chen </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>2D Perovskite Engineering Enables Robust Self‐Assembled Monolayers for High‐Performance Perovskite Solar Cells</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73561</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73561</prism:doi>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73461?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 01:20:54 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T01:20:54-07:00</dc:date>

<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73461</guid>
<title>Zwitterionic Polymers: Synthesis, Architectures, Properties, and Biomedical Applications</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> This review is organized around the hierarchical “synthesis–structure–application” relationship and outlines the progressive evolution from synthetic strategies to polymer structures, material architectures, and biomedical functions. Building on this framework, it further proposes application‐driven design principles, in which specific functional demands guide the rational selection of architectures, synthesis methods, and zwitterionic polymer systems. ABSTRACT Zwitterionic polymers are a class of polymeric materials characterized by repeating units bearing equal numbers of positively and negatively charged groups. Their unique chemical structure confers a strong hydration effect via ionic solvation, leading to distinctive properties such as resistance to protein adsorption and cell adhesion, effective interfacial lubrication, enhanced drug stability, and antifreezing capability. Consequently, zwitterionic polymers have garnered significant attention in biomedical applications in recent years. This review summarizes zwitterionic polymers through an application‐oriented “synthesis–architecture–application” framework. We emphasize that their biomedical performance is not determined solely by types of zwitterionic polymers, but by the hierarchical relationship among synthetic strategy, chain‐level topology, material architecture, and application‐specific biological interactions. Conversely, application requirements guide the selection of material architecture, polymer topology, synthetic strategy, and zwitterionic polymers. From this perspective, synthetic routes are discussed as tools for controlling polymer topology and architecture, while biomedical applications are analyzed in terms of the material forms and interfacial properties they require. We further discuss the current translational status of zwitterionic polymers, their major clinical challenges, and potential solutions. This review provides an application‐oriented design roadmap for linking zwitterionic chemistry, polymer synthesis, material architecture, and biomedical translation. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/365dffc7-5d68-4bd2-b923-0c849b15e061/adma73461-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Zwitterionic Polymers: Synthesis, Architectures, Properties, and Biomedical Applications"/> <p>This review is organized around the hierarchical “synthesis–structure–application” relationship and outlines the progressive evolution from synthetic strategies to polymer structures, material architectures, and biomedical functions. Building on this framework, it further proposes application-driven design principles, in which specific functional demands guide the rational selection of architectures, synthesis methods, and zwitterionic polymer systems. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Zwitterionic polymers are a class of polymeric materials characterized by repeating units bearing equal numbers of positively and negatively charged groups. Their unique chemical structure confers a strong hydration effect via ionic solvation, leading to distinctive properties such as resistance to protein adsorption and cell adhesion, effective interfacial lubrication, enhanced drug stability, and antifreezing capability. Consequently, zwitterionic polymers have garnered significant attention in biomedical applications in recent years. This review summarizes zwitterionic polymers through an application-oriented “synthesis–architecture–application” framework. We emphasize that their biomedical performance is not determined solely by types of zwitterionic polymers, but by the hierarchical relationship among synthetic strategy, chain-level topology, material architecture, and application-specific biological interactions. Conversely, application requirements guide the selection of material architecture, polymer topology, synthetic strategy, and zwitterionic polymers. From this perspective, synthetic routes are discussed as tools for controlling polymer topology and architecture, while biomedical applications are analyzed in terms of the material forms and interfacial properties they require. We further discuss the current translational status of zwitterionic polymers, their major clinical challenges, and potential solutions. This review provides an application-oriented design roadmap for linking zwitterionic chemistry, polymer synthesis, material architecture, and biomedical translation.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Hongying Wang, Kuan Cheng, Hanqi Zheng, Xiawei Zhao, Xiao Han, Danyang Chen, Xu Tian, Jianwei Sun, Yuqing Cheng, Liping Lang, Siyu Bao, Yongmao Li, Wenguang Liu, Jianhai Yang </dc:creator>
<category>REVIEW</category>
<dc:title>Zwitterionic Polymers: Synthesis, Architectures, Properties, and Biomedical Applications</dc:title>
