<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202600020?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 01:18:47 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T01:18:47-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202600020</guid>
<title>Weak In‐Plane Ferromagnetism and Electronic Nematicity in the Distorted Triple‐Q Magnetic Phase of Co1/3TaS2</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$ hosts a triple‐Q noncoplanar antiferromagnetic state with coexisting Z3${\rm Z}_3$ electronic nematicity. We report rotational hysteresis observed in both magnetoresistance and magnetic torque, revealing strongly pinned in‐plane weak ferromagnetic moments in the triple‐Q phase and the magnetism‐driven nature of the co‐existing nematicity. These results demonstrate that the weak in‐plane ferromagnetic moment offers an additional handle on the spin‐driven electronic nematicity. ABSTRACT An intercalated transition‐metal dichalcogenide Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$ hosts a triple‐Q (3Q) noncoplanar antiferromagnetic state that co‐exists with Z3$Z_{3}$ electronic nematicity, indicating broken threefold rotational symmetry. This nematicity exhibits versatile field‐ and strain‐tunability, making it promising for spintronics applications. However, its microscopic connection to magnetism has remained unclear. Here, we report rotational hysteresis observed in both magnetoresistance and magnetic torque, revealing strongly pinned in‐plane weak ferromagnetic moments in the triple‐Q phase and the magnetism‐driven nature of the co‐existing nematicity. In particular, when fields are rotated within a narrow angular range to restrict magnetization reversal, we observe additional hysteresis loops. This hysteresis can be well explained by domain repopulation, further supported by our theoretical simulations based on the spin Hamiltonian of Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$. These results demonstrate that the weak in‐plane ferromagnetic moment offers an additional handle on the spin‐driven electronic nematicity, providing a microscopic picture of the field‐tunable electronic responses in the 3Q state of Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/b3635f2b-4874-49b3-98d7-9006ca8682f0/adma73538-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Weak In-Plane Ferromagnetism and Electronic Nematicity in the Distorted Triple-Q Magnetic Phase of Co1/3TaS2"/> <p>Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$ hosts a triple-Q noncoplanar antiferromagnetic state with coexisting Z3${\rm Z}_3$ electronic nematicity. We report rotational hysteresis observed in both magnetoresistance and magnetic torque, revealing strongly pinned in-plane weak ferromagnetic moments in the triple-Q phase and the magnetism-driven nature of the co-existing nematicity. These results demonstrate that the weak in-plane ferromagnetic moment offers an additional handle on the spin-driven electronic nematicity. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>An intercalated transition-metal dichalcogenide Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$ hosts a triple-<b>Q</b> (3<b>Q</b>) noncoplanar antiferromagnetic state that co-exists with Z3$Z_{3}$ electronic nematicity, indicating broken threefold rotational symmetry. This nematicity exhibits versatile field- and strain-tunability, making it promising for spintronics applications. However, its microscopic connection to magnetism has remained unclear. Here, we report rotational hysteresis observed in both magnetoresistance and magnetic torque, revealing strongly pinned in-plane weak ferromagnetic moments in the triple-<b>Q</b> phase and the magnetism-driven nature of the co-existing nematicity. In particular, when fields are rotated within a narrow angular range to restrict magnetization reversal, we observe additional hysteresis loops. This hysteresis can be well explained by domain repopulation, further supported by our theoretical simulations based on the spin Hamiltonian of Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$. These results demonstrate that the weak in-plane ferromagnetic moment offers an additional handle on the spin-driven electronic nematicity, providing a microscopic picture of the field-tunable electronic responses in the 3<b>Q</b> state of Co1/3TaS2${\rm Co}_{1/3}{\rm TaS}_{2}$.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Joonyoung Choi, Woonghee Cho, Pyeongjae Park, Woun Kang, Je‐Geun Park, Younjung Jo </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Weak In‐Plane Ferromagnetism and Electronic Nematicity in the Distorted Triple‐Q Magnetic Phase of Co1/3TaS2</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.202600020</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.202600020</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202600020?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>
...
</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73585?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 01:18:44 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T01:18:44-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73585</guid>
<title>Stable and Low‐Cost Organic Photovoltaics Without Conjugated Donors</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> The bulk heterojunction based on low‑cost inorganic p‑type semiconductor CuSCN and organic n‐type non‐fullerene acceptor L8‑BO delivers a champion power conversion efficiency of 7.21%, together with excellent thermal stability and operational durability, offering a practical materials approach for high‑performance and stable organic photovoltaics, thereby advancing their potential for industrial‐scale application. ABSTRACT Despite significant advances in power conversion efficiency (PCE) exceeding 20%, organic photovoltaics (OPVs) face industrialization challenges due to inherent stability issues associated with conventional organic donors and hole transport layers. Here, we employ an inorganic p‐type semiconductor copper(I) thiocyanate (CuSCN) as a multifunctional component concurrently serving as donor and hole transport layer. Combined with the non‐fullerene acceptor L8‐BO, the bulk heterojunction device achieves a champion PCE of 7.21%. More importantly, the CuSCN‐based devices exhibit exceptional thermal stability, maintaining 80% of their initial efficiency (T80) for nearly 400 h at 85°C, far exceeding the T80 of 3 to 33 h for OPVs based on conventional all‐organic active layers. Moreover, under ISOS‐L‐3 protocol (maximum power point tracking under 100 mW cm−2 illumination, 65°C, 50% relative humidity), the devices exhibit outstanding operational durability, sustaining approximately 50% of their initial efficiency after 900 h, in stark contrast to the rapid degradation observed in D18:L8‐BO and PM6:L8‐BO reference systems, which retain only 15% and 19%, respectively. This work underscores the potential of CuSCN in enabling efficient, durable, and industrially viable OPVs. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/1050b733-e415-4761-a7ec-f92915844fd2/adma73585-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Stable and Low-Cost Organic Photovoltaics Without Conjugated Donors"/> <p>The bulk heterojunction based on low‑cost inorganic p‑type semiconductor CuSCN and organic n-type non-fullerene acceptor L8‑BO delivers a champion power conversion efficiency of 7.21%, together with excellent thermal stability and operational durability, offering a practical materials approach for high‑performance and stable organic photovoltaics, thereby advancing their potential for industrial-scale application. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Despite significant advances in power conversion efficiency (PCE) exceeding 20%, organic photovoltaics (OPVs) face industrialization challenges due to inherent stability issues associated with conventional organic donors and hole transport layers. Here, we employ an inorganic p-type semiconductor copper(I) thiocyanate (CuSCN) as a multifunctional component concurrently serving as donor and hole transport layer. Combined with the non-fullerene acceptor L8-BO, the bulk heterojunction device achieves a champion PCE of 7.21%. More importantly, the CuSCN-based devices exhibit exceptional thermal stability, maintaining 80% of their initial efficiency (<i>T</i> <sub>80</sub>) for nearly 400 h at 85°C, far exceeding the <i>T</i> <sub>80</sub> of 3 to 33 h for OPVs based on conventional all-organic active layers. Moreover, under ISOS-L-3 protocol (maximum power point tracking under 100 mW cm<sup>−2</sup> illumination, 65°C, 50% relative humidity), the devices exhibit outstanding operational durability, sustaining approximately 50% of their initial efficiency after 900 h, in stark contrast to the rapid degradation observed in D18:L8-BO and PM6:L8-BO reference systems, which retain only 15% and 19%, respectively. This work underscores the potential of CuSCN in enabling efficient, durable, and industrially viable OPVs.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Siru Guo, Jiayu Wang, Xue Yan, Jie Xiong, Hongxiang Li, Bowei Li, Yakun He, Junyi Li, Weilin Zhou, Yufei Gong, Hua Tang, Cenqi Yan, Jiaqiang Qin, Min Deng, Xiaopeng Xu, Lei Meng, Frédéric Laquai, Qiang Peng, Yongfang Li, Pei Cheng </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Stable and Low‐Cost Organic Photovoltaics Without Conjugated Donors</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73585</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73585</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73585?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>
...
</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73599?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 01:18:03 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T01:18:03-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73599</guid>
<title>Energetic All‐Polymer Fiber Batteries Enabled by Interface‐Interlocked Water‐In‐Network Electrolytes for Wearable Electronics</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> A “water‐in‐network” strategy that simultaneously addresses narrow electrochemical stability windows and interfacial instability. By modulating crosslinking density to engineer water confinement and employing in situ polymerization to construct mechanically interlocked interfaces that suppress water‐induced delamination, this approach yields an all‐polymer sodium‐ion fiber battery featuring high energy density and exceptional deformability for wearable applications. ABSTRACT All‐polymer batteries configured in a fibrous form by integrating polymeric electrodes with aqueous quasi‐solid polymer electrolytes (AQPEs) represent a promising solution for wearable electronics with safety and sustainability. However, their practical development is constrained by the narrow electrochemical stability window (ESW) of AQPEs and the structural instability of electrode–electrolyte interfaces under deformation. Here, we present a “water‐in‐network” (WIN) electrolyte that precisely engineers water activity and dynamics by modulating the crosslinking density of the polymer network. We unanticipatedly discover a distinct “water confinement” effect wherein water activity exhibits a non‐monotonic dependence on crosslinking density, originating from the structural evolution of the network. Benefiting from this mechanism, the ESW significantly expands to 3.4 V, endowing an all‐polymer sodium‐ion fiber battery with an energy density of 92.4 Wh kg−1. Crucially, in situ interfacial polymerization following electrolyte pre‐infiltration forms a mechanically interlocked electrode–electrolyte interface, effectively suppressing water‐induced delamination and preserving 78% capacity after 12 000 bending cycles.When seamlessly woven into a shirt, the fiber batteries can power the fabric‐based chemical sensor, enabling real‐time, on‐body health monitoring across various activities. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/9260c6ae-ec43-4168-9c45-4a93e49359bc/adma73599-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Energetic All-Polymer Fiber Batteries Enabled by Interface-Interlocked Water-In-Network Electrolytes for Wearable Electronics"/> <p>A “water-in-network” strategy that simultaneously addresses narrow electrochemical stability windows and interfacial instability. By modulating crosslinking density to engineer water confinement and employing in situ polymerization to construct mechanically interlocked interfaces that suppress water-induced delamination, this approach yields an all-polymer sodium-ion fiber battery featuring high energy density and exceptional deformability for wearable applications. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>All-polymer batteries configured in a fibrous form by integrating polymeric electrodes with aqueous quasi-solid polymer electrolytes (AQPEs) represent a promising solution for wearable electronics with safety and sustainability. However, their practical development is constrained by the narrow electrochemical stability window (ESW) of AQPEs and the structural instability of electrode–electrolyte interfaces under deformation. Here, we present a “water-in-network” (WIN) electrolyte that precisely engineers water activity and dynamics by modulating the crosslinking density of the polymer network. We unanticipatedly discover a distinct “water confinement” effect wherein water activity exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on crosslinking density, originating from the structural evolution of the network. Benefiting from this mechanism, the ESW significantly expands to 3.4 V, endowing an all-polymer sodium-ion fiber battery with an energy density of 92.4 Wh kg<sup>−1</sup>. Crucially, in situ interfacial polymerization following electrolyte pre-infiltration forms a mechanically interlocked electrode–electrolyte interface, effectively suppressing water-induced delamination and preserving 78% capacity after 12 000 bending cycles.When seamlessly woven into a shirt, the fiber batteries can power the fabric-based chemical sensor, enabling real-time, on-body health monitoring across various activities.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Kangkang Jia, Qimin Liang, Yang Hong, Jiahao Zhu, Jinze Wang, Xin Wang, Weijing Zuo, Jiajun Tan, Min Wang, Xiaohua Zhong, Longbin Qiu, Yan Huang, Ruhong Li, Xiulin Fan, Sisi He </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Energetic All‐Polymer Fiber Batteries Enabled by Interface‐Interlocked Water‐In‐Network Electrolytes for Wearable Electronics</dc:title>

<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73425</guid>
<title>Hydrogelation via Supramolecular Copolymerization of Structural Water within Adaptive Metal–Organic Fibers (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> Metal–Organic Fibers In their Research Article (DOI: 10.1002/adma.202519933), Merlin R. Stühler, Alex J. Plajer, and co‐workers report metal–organic fibres in which confined structural water acts as a co‐monomer to drive supramolecular polymerisation. These tubular assemblies form thermoresponsive hydrogels and enable selective chiral recognition via water‐mediated information transfer, establishing structural water as a functional design element in adaptive supramolecular materials. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/d0838232-a55f-446b-9213-241f5f735a16/adma73425-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Hydrogelation via Supramolecular Copolymerization of Structural Water within Adaptive Metal–Organic Fibers (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)"/> <p><b>Metal–Organic Fibers</b></p> <p>In their Research Article (DOI: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202519933">10.1002/adma.202519933</a>), Merlin R. Stühler, Alex J. Plajer, and co-workers report metal–organic fibres in which confined structural water acts as a co-monomer to drive supramolecular polymerisation. These tubular assemblies form thermoresponsive hydrogels and enable selective chiral recognition via water-mediated information transfer, establishing structural water as a functional design element in adaptive supramolecular materials. </p> <br/> </content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Merlin R. Stühler, Hesam Makki, Tarek Hilal, Debsena Chakraboty, Mathias Dimde, Kai Ludwig, Rainer Haag, Sabine Rosenfeldt, Dorothee Silbernagl, Andreas Schäfer, Alex J. Plajer </dc:creator>
<category>Cover Picture</category>

<title>Synaptic Functionality and Neuromorphic Information Processing in Membrane Ion Channel Junctions (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> Membrane Ion Channel Synapses In their Research Article (DOI: 10.1002/adma.202519525), Aleksandr Noy and co‐workers report membrane ion channel synapses (MICS), artificial ionic synapses based on droplet interface junctions doped with biological ion channel Gramicidin A. MICS exhibits robust memristive behavior and synaptic plasticity and enables neuromorphic applications including associative learning and reservoir computing. In one application, researchers used MICS to play a board game of tic‐tac‐toe against an ideal opponent. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/67844c16-cbf5-4166-afdb-0fc2298dcda0/adma73427-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Synaptic Functionality and Neuromorphic Information Processing in Membrane Ion Channel Junctions (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)"/> <p><b>Membrane Ion Channel Synapses</b></p> <p>In their Research Article (DOI: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202519525">10.1002/adma.202519525</a>), Aleksandr Noy and co-workers report membrane ion channel synapses (MICS), artificial ionic synapses based on droplet interface junctions doped with biological ion channel Gramicidin A. MICS exhibits robust memristive behavior and synaptic plasticity and enables neuromorphic applications including associative learning and reservoir computing. In one application, researchers used MICS to play a board game of tic-tac-toe against an ideal opponent. </p> <br/> </content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Zhongwu Li, Jiachen Feng, Jingyi Xiao, Anton Leuski, Aleksandr Noy </dc:creator>
<category>Inside Back Cover</category>
<dc:title>Synaptic Functionality and Neuromorphic Information Processing in Membrane Ion Channel Junctions (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)</dc:title>

<title>Flexible Multiband Photonic Synapses for Nociceptive Perception and Neuromorphic Computation via Fluorinated InP Quantum Dots (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> Flexible InP Quantum Dot Photonic Synaptic Transistors Bio‐inspired visual perception is realized using flexible organic photonic synaptic transistors based on fluorinated InP quantum dots. The cover illustrates the coupling of biological vision and artificial synapses, enabling efficient damage perception, information processing, and neuromorphic computation. More details can be found in the Research Article by Yang Li and co‐workers (DOI: 10.1002/adma.202518057). </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/0b0b8f21-9518-42ec-8bdc-77605f7e513a/adma73428-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Flexible Multiband Photonic Synapses for Nociceptive Perception and Neuromorphic Computation via Fluorinated InP Quantum Dots (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)"/> <p><b>Flexible InP Quantum Dot Photonic Synaptic Transistors</b></p> <p>Bio-inspired visual perception is realized using flexible organic photonic synaptic transistors based on fluorinated InP quantum dots. The cover illustrates the coupling of biological vision and artificial synapses, enabling efficient damage perception, information processing, and neuromorphic computation. More details can be found in the Research Article by Yang Li and co-workers (DOI: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202518057">10.1002/adma.202518057</a>). </p> <br/> </content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Wanlong Lu, Peixian Li, Bin Zeng, Jing Wang, Ning Dai, Yang Li </dc:creator>

<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>

<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73432</guid>
<title>Beyond Earth: Resilience of Quasi‐2D Perovskite Solar Cells in Space (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> Perovskite Solar Cells in Low Earth Orbit In the Research Article (DOI: 10.1002/adma.202520433), Christoph Putz, Martin Kaltenbrunner, and co‐workers demonstrate rigid quasi‐2D perovskite solar cells delivering stable power for over 100 days under real space conditions. Correlated in‐orbit data and extensive ground‐based thermal and proton‐irradiation studies reveal high intrinsic radiation tolerance, substrate‐driven losses, and key challenges toward lightweight perovskite space photovoltaics. The illustration depicts a perovskite‐structured satellite in low Earth orbit, symbolizing the deployment of quasi‐2D perovskite solar cells for space‐based power generation. Cover art by Florian Loimayr. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/93cd8c90-e03f-4907-9512-53d377085008/adma73432-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Beyond Earth: Resilience of Quasi-2D Perovskite Solar Cells in Space (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)"/> <p><b>Perovskite Solar Cells in Low Earth Orbit</b></p> <p>In the Research Article (DOI: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202520433">10.1002/adma.202520433</a>), Christoph Putz, Martin Kaltenbrunner, and co-workers demonstrate rigid quasi-2D perovskite solar cells delivering stable power for over 100 days under real space conditions. Correlated in-orbit data and extensive ground-based thermal and proton-irradiation studies reveal high intrinsic radiation tolerance, substrate-driven losses, and key challenges toward lightweight perovskite space photovoltaics. The illustration depicts a perovskite-structured satellite in low Earth orbit, symbolizing the deployment of quasi-2D perovskite solar cells for space-based power generation. Cover art by Florian Loimayr. </p> <br/> </content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Christoph Putz, Lukas E. Lehner, Stepan Demchyshyn, Bekele Hailegnaw, Phillip Jahelka, Magdalena Breitwieser, Sercan Özen, Andrea Denker, Jürgen Bundesmann, Alina Hanna Dittwald, Dilara Karabulut, Philipp Tockhorn, Steve Albrecht, Felix Lang, Markus C. Scharber, Michael D. Kelzenberg, Harry A. Atwater, Martin Kaltenbrunner </dc:creator>

<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00</dc:date>

<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73433</guid>
<title>3D‐Printable, Honeycomb‐Inspired Tissue‐Like Bioelectrodes for Patient‐Specific Neural Interface (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> Patient‐Specific HiPGE This image illustrates a first‐of‐its‐kind soft neural interface inspired by the honeycomb architecture found in nature. The honeycomb‐inspired, patient‐specific design enables a neural interface that combines tissue‐like softness with mechanical robustness, advancing next‐generation brain‐computer interfaces and neuromodulation therapies. More details can be found in the Research Article by Tao Zhou, Nanyin Zhang, Sulin Zhang, and co‐workers (DOI: 10.1002/adma.202516291). </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/2a9e708a-6b8a-47a7-b17a-751243a0a607/adma73433-gra-0001-m.png" alt="3D-Printable, Honeycomb-Inspired Tissue-Like Bioelectrodes for Patient-Specific Neural Interface (Adv. Mater. 31/2026)"/> <p><b>Patient-Specific HiPGE</b></p> <p>This image illustrates a first-of-its-kind soft neural interface inspired by the honeycomb architecture found in nature. The honeycomb-inspired, patient-specific design enables a neural interface that combines tissue-like softness with mechanical robustness, advancing next-generation brain-computer interfaces and neuromodulation therapies. More details can be found in the Research Article by Tao Zhou, Nanyin Zhang, Sulin Zhang, and co-workers (DOI: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.202516291">10.1002/adma.202516291</a>). </p> <br/> </content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Marzia Momin, Luyi Feng, Xiaoai Chen, Salahuddin Ahmed, Basma AlMahmood, Li‐Pang Huang, Jiashu Ren, Xinyi Wang, Hyunjin Lee, Samuel R. Cramer, Nanyin Zhang, Sulin Zhang, Tao Zhou </dc:creator>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202522834?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00</dc:date>

<prism:coverDisplayDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDisplayDate>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202522834</guid>
<title>Recent Advances in Electrospun Nanofibers for Triboelectric Nanogenerators: Performance Enhancement Strategies and Emerging Applications</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> This review highlights recent advances in electrospun nanofiber‐based triboelectric nanogenerators, emphasizing how material design, fiber architecture, and interface engineering collectively enhance output performance and mechanical durability. Key developments in porous, aligned, core‐shell, and hierarchical nanofibers are discussed alongside emerging applications in wearable electronics, biomedical systems, and self‐powered IoT. Current challenges and future opportunities for scalable, sustainable, and high‐performance energy harvesting are also outlined. ABSTRACT In the quest for next‐generation sustainable energy systems, triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) have emerged as a pivotal technology for converting mechanical energy into electrical power, especially at the micro and nanoscale. Among various structural platforms, fibrous materials, particularly those produced via electrospinning, have demonstrated exceptional potential for TENG development due to their tunable morphology, lightweight nature, mechanical flexibility, and large surface‐to‐volume ratio. These characteristics are critical for enhancing the interfacial charge generation and transfer that underpin triboelectric performance. This review offers a comprehensive examination of the latest advances in fibrous materials for TENGs, with a particular focus on material innovations, structural design strategies, and performance optimization techniques. Emphasis is placed on the role of hybrid nanocomposites, core–shell configurations, surface functionalization, and alignment control in maximizing electrical output and operational durability. Additionally, the paper surveys emerging application areas such as wearable electronics, self‐powered sensors, smart textiles, biomedical monitoring, and human‐machine interaction systems. By bridging fundamental material science with practical design paradigms, this review identifies critical bottlenecks and lays out future research directions toward scalable, efficient, and environmentally friendly fibrous TENG technologies. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/df6fe6eb-7d11-4edc-84fe-4b696a47e5e3/adma73144-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Recent Advances in Electrospun Nanofibers for Triboelectric Nanogenerators: Performance Enhancement Strategies and Emerging Applications"/> <p>This review highlights recent advances in electrospun nanofiber-based triboelectric nanogenerators, emphasizing how material design, fiber architecture, and interface engineering collectively enhance output performance and mechanical durability. Key developments in porous, aligned, core-shell, and hierarchical nanofibers are discussed alongside emerging applications in wearable electronics, biomedical systems, and self-powered IoT. Current challenges and future opportunities for scalable, sustainable, and high-performance energy harvesting are also outlined. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>In the quest for next-generation sustainable energy systems, triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) have emerged as a pivotal technology for converting mechanical energy into electrical power, especially at the micro and nanoscale. Among various structural platforms, fibrous materials, particularly those produced via electrospinning, have demonstrated exceptional potential for TENG development due to their tunable morphology, lightweight nature, mechanical flexibility, and large surface-to-volume ratio. These characteristics are critical for enhancing the interfacial charge generation and transfer that underpin triboelectric performance. This review offers a comprehensive examination of the latest advances in fibrous materials for TENGs, with a particular focus on material innovations, structural design strategies, and performance optimization techniques. Emphasis is placed on the role of hybrid nanocomposites, core–shell configurations, surface functionalization, and alignment control in maximizing electrical output and operational durability. Additionally, the paper surveys emerging application areas such as wearable electronics, self-powered sensors, smart textiles, biomedical monitoring, and human-machine interaction systems. By bridging fundamental material science with practical design paradigms, this review identifies critical bottlenecks and lays out future research directions toward scalable, efficient, and environmentally friendly fibrous TENG technologies.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> MD Fajla Rabbi, Jee Hwan Ahn, Duy Linh Vu, Kyoung Kwan Ahn </dc:creator>

<dc:title>Recent Advances in Electrospun Nanofibers for Triboelectric Nanogenerators: Performance Enhancement Strategies and Emerging Applications</dc:title>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202523422?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDate>
<prism:coverDisplayDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDisplayDate>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202523422</guid>
<title>From Bio‐Interface Materials to Neural Integration: The Next‐Generation Brain–Machine Interfaces Powered by Hydrogels</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> This review summarizes recent advances in hydrogel‐based brain‐machine interfaces (BMIs), with a focus on key material attributes such as mechanical compliance, adhesion, conductivity, and biocompatibility, which address the limitations of rigid metal electrodes. We classify BMIs into non‐invasive and invasive systems based on their neural access modality and describe their fundamental operating principles. Additionally, we discuss emerging applications in neurological disorders and neurorehabilitation, and outline current challenges and future strategies aimed at achieving high‐fidelity, long‐term stability, and clinical translation of hydrogel‐based neural interfaces. ABSTRACT Brain–machine interfaces (BMIs), which serve as revolutionary tools for neural recording, modulation, and rehabilitation, are highly dependent on the biocompatibility and mechanical suitability of their electrode materials. Although traditional metal electrodes possess excellent conductivity, their inherent rigidity causes a substantial mechanical mismatch with soft neural tissue, leading to chronic inflammatory responses and poor long‐term stability. The emergence of hydrogel electrodes has provided a breakthrough solution to this fundamental limitation. Hydrogels, characterized by their softness, high ionic conductivity, and tissue‐like compliance, offer a viable solution to mitigate these issues. This review systematically explores the material properties of hydrogel‐integrated BMIs, providing an in‐depth investigation of key hydrogel characteristics, including toughness, adhesion, conductivity, and biocompatibility. Additionally, hydrogel‐based BMIs are categorized into non‐invasive and invasive systems, each defined by its characteristic operational principles and signal‐acquisition mechanisms. The study further reviews critical issues, including surgical implantation strategies, multimodal data fusion, integration of artificial intelligence, as well as system integration and clinical translation. From a therapeutic perspective, this work highlights the application of BMIs in treating neurological disorders such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, epilepsy, stroke, neuropathic pain, and depression. Furthermore, this review critically examines the persistent challenges faced by hydrogel‐based BMIs and proposes innovative strategies for future development. Ultimately, it outlines a developmental roadmap for next‐generation hydrogel‐based biotherapeutic technologies aimed at achieving high‐fidelity, stable and clinically translatable BMI systems. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/1795fb54-b1cc-4469-be5e-a65493989af5/adma73140-gra-0001-m.png" alt="From Bio-Interface Materials to Neural Integration: The Next-Generation Brain–Machine Interfaces Powered by Hydrogels"/> <p>This review summarizes recent advances in hydrogel-based brain-machine interfaces (BMIs), with a focus on key material attributes such as mechanical compliance, adhesion, conductivity, and biocompatibility, which address the limitations of rigid metal electrodes. We classify BMIs into non-invasive and invasive systems based on their neural access modality and describe their fundamental operating principles. Additionally, we discuss emerging applications in neurological disorders and neurorehabilitation, and outline current challenges and future strategies aimed at achieving high-fidelity, long-term stability, and clinical translation of hydrogel-based neural interfaces. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Brain–machine interfaces (BMIs), which serve as revolutionary tools for neural recording, modulation, and rehabilitation, are highly dependent on the biocompatibility and mechanical suitability of their electrode materials. Although traditional metal electrodes possess excellent conductivity, their inherent rigidity causes a substantial mechanical mismatch with soft neural tissue, leading to chronic inflammatory responses and poor long-term stability. The emergence of hydrogel electrodes has provided a breakthrough solution to this fundamental limitation. Hydrogels, characterized by their softness, high ionic conductivity, and tissue-like compliance, offer a viable solution to mitigate these issues. This review systematically explores the material properties of hydrogel-integrated BMIs, providing an in-depth investigation of key hydrogel characteristics, including toughness, adhesion, conductivity, and biocompatibility. Additionally, hydrogel-based BMIs are categorized into non-invasive and invasive systems, each defined by its characteristic operational principles and signal-acquisition mechanisms. The study further reviews critical issues, including surgical implantation strategies, multimodal data fusion, integration of artificial intelligence, as well as system integration and clinical translation. From a therapeutic perspective, this work highlights the application of BMIs in treating neurological disorders such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, epilepsy, stroke, neuropathic pain, and depression. Furthermore, this review critically examines the persistent challenges faced by hydrogel-based BMIs and proposes innovative strategies for future development. Ultimately, it outlines a developmental roadmap for next-generation hydrogel-based biotherapeutic technologies aimed at achieving high-fidelity, stable and clinically translatable BMI systems.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Zhenchun Li, Rongfeng Ge, Zhiyuan Zhao, He Xiao, Chuang Du, Yuekun Lai, Lei Wang </dc:creator>

<dc:title>From Bio‐Interface Materials to Neural Integration: The Next‐Generation Brain–Machine Interfaces Powered by Hydrogels</dc:title>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202516173?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDate>
<prism:coverDisplayDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDisplayDate>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202516173</guid>
<title>Triply‐Twinned Metamaterials: Unraveling the Mechanics and Failure Pathways Through High‐Resolution XCT</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> Triply‐twinned architected lattices transform deformation from bending to stretching of struts, delivering up to threefold increases in stiffness and strength across polymeric and metallic systems. High‐resolution synchrotron XCT and image‐based simulations reveal how meta‐grain architecture, defects, and AM build orientation govern failure pathways. This study demonstrates a design strategy for lightweight metamaterials with enhanced performance and reliability. ABSTRACT We designed and engineered a novel class of triply‐twinned Body‐Centred Cubic (BCCT) lattices that achieved up to three‐fold improvements in mechanical performance over conventional BCC lattice architecture. Inefficient strut deformation and defect‐sensitive failure limit the performance and reliability of architected metamaterials. Triply‐twinned meta‐crystal architectures transform the dominant strut‐scale deformation from bending to stretching in both polymeric (Rigid 4K) and metallic (Ti‐6Al‐4V) Additively Manufactured (AM) BCCT lattices, significantly enhancing their stiffness (+380%) and strength (+279%). Using high‐resolution synchrotron X‐ray computed tomography, image‐based finite element models, scanning electron microscopy, and pyrometry, we correlate fracture mechanisms to the architecture design and as‐built defects in these AM lattices. We further reduce defect‐driven fracture by 50% without altering the global failure mode by adjusting the build orientation of the lattices. This integrated, multi‐scale approach links fundamental deformation mechanics to manufacturability, providing a broadly applicable design strategy for next‐generation architected metamaterials with exceptional performance and reliability. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/7c67cd05-1f63-416a-afcb-78da21408d66/adma73075-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Triply-Twinned Metamaterials: Unraveling the Mechanics and Failure Pathways Through High-Resolution XCT"/> <p>Triply-twinned architected lattices transform deformation from bending to stretching of struts, delivering up to threefold increases in stiffness and strength across polymeric and metallic systems. High-resolution synchrotron XCT and image-based simulations reveal how meta-grain architecture, defects, and AM build orientation govern failure pathways. This study demonstrates a design strategy for lightweight metamaterials with enhanced performance and reliability. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>We designed and engineered a novel class of triply-twinned Body-Centred Cubic (BCCT) lattices that achieved up to three-fold improvements in mechanical performance over conventional BCC lattice architecture. Inefficient strut deformation and defect-sensitive failure limit the performance and reliability of architected metamaterials. Triply-twinned meta-crystal architectures transform the dominant strut-scale deformation from bending to stretching in both polymeric (Rigid 4K) and metallic (Ti-6Al-4V) Additively Manufactured (AM) BCCT lattices, significantly enhancing their stiffness (+380%) and strength (+279%). Using high-resolution synchrotron X-ray computed tomography, image-based finite element models, scanning electron microscopy, and pyrometry, we correlate fracture mechanisms to the architecture design and as-built defects in these AM lattices. We further reduce defect-driven fracture by 50% without altering the global failure mode by adjusting the build orientation of the lattices. This integrated, multi-scale approach links fundamental deformation mechanics to manufacturability, providing a broadly applicable design strategy for next-generation architected metamaterials with exceptional performance and reliability.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> David McArthur, George Maddison, Jaianth Vijayakumar, Paul Tafforeau, Kathy Christofidou, Peter David Lee, PJ Tan, Chu Lun Alex Leung </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Triply‐Twinned Metamaterials: Unraveling the Mechanics and Failure Pathways Through High‐Resolution XCT</dc:title>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202521048?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDate>
<prism:coverDisplayDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDisplayDate>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202521048</guid>
<title>Side Chains Override Crystallinity in n‐Type Organic Mixed Conductors</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> Thermal annealing often improves charge transport in polymeric semiconductors by enhancing crystallinity, but does crystallinity help for organic transistors operating in water? By annealing three n‐type polymer backbones bearing either linear or branched ethylene glycol side chains, this work uncovers a paradox: crystallinity increases in every film, yet performance improves only for those with linear side chains, while branched analogues degrade. ABSTRACT In organic semiconductors, crystallinity is commonly associated with enhanced charge transport. For organic mixed ionic‐electronic conductors (OMIECs), materials at the core of bioelectronic devices, whether higher crystallinity consistently translates into improved performance remains unresolved. Here, we use thermal annealing to control the crystallinity of three electron‐transporting OMIECs bearing either branched or linear ethylene glycol side chains that are used to promote ion transport. Although annealing uniformly enhances crystallinity across all materials, it improves mixed charge transport only in polymers with linear side chains by doubling electronic charge mobility. Specifically, annealed films with branched side chains exhibit reduced mobility and low water uptake, coinciding with a pronounced bipolaron formation, which we uncovered using a combination of in‐operando physicochemical characterization methods. Thermal annealing is also used to sterilize these materials for interfacing with living cells, with the benefit of improved sensor performance. These results reveal that crystallinity can hinder mixed conductivity depending on side‐chain architecture, independent of the backbone chemistry. By challenging the prevailing assumption that crystallinity is universally beneficial for charge transport, this work establishes design rules for developing OMIECs that combine high performance with compatibility for fabrication and sterilization processes involving high temperatures, paving the way for reliable, scalable bioelectronic devices. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/6596915d-230d-40e4-a4b3-1dbf6ddf5174/adma73165-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Side Chains Override Crystallinity in n-Type Organic Mixed Conductors"/> <p>Thermal annealing often improves charge transport in polymeric semiconductors by enhancing crystallinity, but does crystallinity help for organic transistors operating in water? By annealing three n-type polymer backbones bearing either linear or branched ethylene glycol side chains, this work uncovers a paradox: crystallinity increases in every film, yet performance improves only for those with linear side chains, while branched analogues degrade. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>In organic semiconductors, crystallinity is commonly associated with enhanced charge transport. For organic mixed ionic-electronic conductors (OMIECs), materials at the core of bioelectronic devices, whether higher crystallinity consistently translates into improved performance remains unresolved. Here, we use thermal annealing to control the crystallinity of three electron-transporting OMIECs bearing either branched or linear ethylene glycol side chains that are used to promote ion transport. Although annealing uniformly enhances crystallinity across all materials, it improves mixed charge transport only in polymers with linear side chains by doubling electronic charge mobility. Specifically, annealed films with branched side chains exhibit reduced mobility and low water uptake, coinciding with a pronounced bipolaron formation, which we uncovered using a combination of in-operando physicochemical characterization methods. Thermal annealing is also used to sterilize these materials for interfacing with living cells, with the benefit of improved sensor performance. These results reveal that crystallinity can hinder mixed conductivity depending on side-chain architecture, independent of the backbone chemistry. By challenging the prevailing assumption that crystallinity is universally beneficial for charge transport, this work establishes design rules for developing OMIECs that combine high performance with compatibility for fabrication and sterilization processes involving high temperatures, paving the way for reliable, scalable bioelectronic devices.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Tania Cecilia Hidalgo Castillo, James F. Ponder Jr., Kui Feng, Arianna Magni, Adam Marks, Danilo Arcangeli, Johana Uribe, Abdul‐Hamid Emwas, Rajendar Sheelraamanthula, Lewis M. Cowen, Bob C. Schroeder, Iain McCulloch, Xugang Guo, Alberto Salleo, Sahika Inal </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Side Chains Override Crystallinity in n‐Type Organic Mixed Conductors</dc:title>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73223?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDate>
<prism:coverDisplayDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDisplayDate>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73223</guid>
<title>Intrinsic Coordination Architecture Governing Selectivity Divergence Between Extended and Single‐Site Electrocatalysts</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> Catalyst coordination acts as a loaded dice biasing reaction selectivity. Single‐atom site catalysts lock intermediates in exposed configurations, enabling continued hydrogenation and favoring oxygen‐retaining products. In contrast, extended metal surfaces fully coordinate and shield reactive centers, promoting deoxygenation pathways. This work reveals a coordination‐constrained exposure principle governing product distributions across catalytic architectures. ABSTRACT Distinct material architectures, ranging from extended metal surfaces to single‐atom sites, exhibit characteristic and often divergent selectivity patterns in complex electrochemical transformations, such as CO2 and nitrate reduction. While thermodynamic descriptors effectively rank catalysts within homologous families, they often fail to rationalize why fundamentally different material classes intrinsically favor distinct reaction pathways. Here, we identify local coordination geometry as a key structural factor that shapes this selectivity bifurcation. By establishing a general coordination‐constraint framework, we show that extended surfaces stabilize intermediates through multi‐atom coordination (ensemble effects). This imposes structural constraints on hydrogenation access to carbon or nitrogen centers while oxygen remains bound, thereby biasing reactions toward fully deoxygenated products (e.g., ethylene and ammonia). In contrast, the unilateral coordination of single‐atom and molecular sites leaves the reactive center spatially exposed, enabling hydrogenation pathways to oxygen‐retaining products (e.g., methanol and hydroxylamine) that are rarely accessible on close‐packed surfaces. Importantly, this geometric effect operates in conjunction with electronic structure and interfacial factors. While coordination geometry defines the set of structurally accessible reaction pathways, electronic and electrochemical conditions govern their relative energetics and kinetic competition. This insight provides a transferable principle for the rational design of selective electrocatalysts. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/ab5c06c9-daa7-44c4-9599-c3004abc8fff/adma73223-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Intrinsic Coordination Architecture Governing Selectivity Divergence Between Extended and Single-Site Electrocatalysts"/> <p>Catalyst coordination acts as a loaded dice biasing reaction selectivity. Single-atom site catalysts lock intermediates in exposed configurations, enabling continued hydrogenation and favoring oxygen-retaining products. In contrast, extended metal surfaces fully coordinate and shield reactive centers, promoting deoxygenation pathways. This work reveals a coordination-constrained exposure principle governing product distributions across catalytic architectures. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Distinct material architectures, ranging from extended metal surfaces to single-atom sites, exhibit characteristic and often divergent selectivity patterns in complex electrochemical transformations, such as CO<sub>2</sub> and nitrate reduction. While thermodynamic descriptors effectively rank catalysts within homologous families, they often fail to rationalize why fundamentally different material classes intrinsically favor distinct reaction pathways. Here, we identify local coordination geometry as a key structural factor that shapes this selectivity bifurcation. By establishing a general coordination-constraint framework, we show that extended surfaces stabilize intermediates through multi-atom coordination (ensemble effects). This imposes structural constraints on hydrogenation access to carbon or nitrogen centers while oxygen remains bound, thereby biasing reactions toward fully deoxygenated products (e.g., ethylene and ammonia). In contrast, the unilateral coordination of single-atom and molecular sites leaves the reactive center spatially exposed, enabling hydrogenation pathways to oxygen-retaining products (e.g., methanol and hydroxylamine) that are rarely accessible on close-packed surfaces. Importantly, this geometric effect operates in conjunction with electronic structure and interfacial factors. While coordination geometry defines the set of structurally accessible reaction pathways, electronic and electrochemical conditions govern their relative energetics and kinetic competition. This insight provides a transferable principle for the rational design of selective electrocatalysts.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Chengyi Zhang, Ziyun Wang </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Intrinsic Coordination Architecture Governing Selectivity Divergence Between Extended and Single‐Site Electrocatalysts</dc:title>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202518697?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDate>
<prism:coverDisplayDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDisplayDate>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202518697</guid>
<title>Non‐Invasive Brain Targeted Delivery of Cannabidiol for Alleviating Neuroinflammatory Disease</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> The glucose‐functionalized nanoparticles (GNPs) are developed for the brain‐targeted delivery of cannabidiol (CBD). GNPs bypass the blood‐brain barrier (BBB) via glucose transporter‐1 (GLUT‐1) and release CBD in response to reactive oxygen species (ROS). This repolarizes microglia from M1 to M2 phenotypes, alleviating neuroinflammation in Parkinson's disease and depression. ABSTRACT Chronic neuroinflammation is a known etiopathogenic factor in neurodegenerative disease. While cannabidiol (CBD) has demonstrated anti‐inflammatory effects, unfavorable pharmacokinetics and poor blood‐brain barrier (BBB) permeability lead to low brain exposure. Here, we greatly improve the efficacy of CBD to treat neuroinflammation by incorporating CBD in a BBB‐permeable glucose nanoparticle (GNPs) that by design also incorporates tissue‐targeting moieties and uses reactive oxygen species responsive polymer to selectively target neuroinflammatory lesions. We achieved a high drug concentration over 20 times higher than naked cargo and demonstrate potent therapeutic effects in two mouse models of neuroinflammatory disease. Mechanistically, disease amelioration resulted from repolarizing microglia from the neurotoxic M1 to the neuroprotective M2 phenotype, leading to neuronal cell regeneration by enhanced secretion of brain derived neurotropic and anti‐inflammatory factors. Our approach for brain targeted CBD delivery may provide a versatile platform for treating other CNS disorders characterized by neuroinflammation. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/a7654d7d-3bbc-478b-b252-2fb0ab952532/adma73187-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Non-Invasive Brain Targeted Delivery of Cannabidiol for Alleviating Neuroinflammatory Disease"/> <p>The glucose-functionalized nanoparticles (GNPs) are developed for the brain-targeted delivery of cannabidiol (CBD). GNPs bypass the blood-brain barrier (BBB) via glucose transporter-1 (GLUT-1) and release CBD in response to reactive oxygen species (ROS). This repolarizes microglia from M1 to M2 phenotypes, alleviating neuroinflammation in Parkinson's disease and depression. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Chronic neuroinflammation is a known etiopathogenic factor in neurodegenerative disease. While cannabidiol (CBD) has demonstrated anti-inflammatory effects, unfavorable pharmacokinetics and poor blood-brain barrier (BBB) permeability lead to low brain exposure. Here, we greatly improve the efficacy of CBD to treat neuroinflammation by incorporating CBD in a BBB-permeable glucose nanoparticle (GNPs) that by design also incorporates tissue-targeting moieties and uses reactive oxygen species responsive polymer to selectively target neuroinflammatory lesions. We achieved a high drug concentration over 20 times higher than naked cargo and demonstrate potent therapeutic effects in two mouse models of neuroinflammatory disease. Mechanistically, disease amelioration resulted from repolarizing microglia from the neurotoxic M1 to the neuroprotective M2 phenotype, leading to neuronal cell regeneration by enhanced secretion of brain derived neurotropic and anti-inflammatory factors. Our approach for brain targeted CBD delivery may provide a versatile platform for treating other CNS disorders characterized by neuroinflammation.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Yibin Wang, Yundong Li, Xin Wang, Yaning Hou, Wei Deng, Meng Zheng, Yan Zou, Bingyang Shi </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Non‐Invasive Brain Targeted Delivery of Cannabidiol for Alleviating Neuroinflammatory Disease</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.202518697</dc:identifier>

<prism:number>31</prism:number>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202520209?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDate>
<prism:coverDisplayDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDisplayDate>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202520209</guid>
<title>A Nano‐Interception Strategy for Chronic Heart Failure: Prussian Blue Nanoparticles Disrupt Fibroblast‐Immune Communication via CCL2 Sequestration</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> A nano‐interception strategy disrupts pathogenic fibroblast–macrophage crosstalk in chronic heart failure. Scalable Prussian blue nanoparticles selectively sequester CCL2 via ultrahigh‐affinity binding, preventing CCR2+ macrophage recruitment and breaking a key fibro‐inflammatory circuit. This approach demonstrates robust efficacy in murine and porcine models, offering a clinically viable paradigm for network‐driven diseases. ABSTRACT Chronic heart failure (HF) remains a global health challenge due to the lack of therapies that effectively disrupt the pathological fibro‐inflammatory networks driving disease progression. While current nanomedicine strategies often target intracellular pathways in isolated cell types, they overlook the multicellular crosstalk central to HF. Here, we develop scalably synthesized Prussian blue (PB) nanoparticles that selectively intercept the CCL2‐CCR2 chemokine axis, a key pathway in fibroblast‐macrophage communication. Single‐nucleus RNA sequencing of murine and human failing hearts identifies a conserved pro‐fibroinflammatory cardiac fibroblast subpopulation (POSTNhi CCL2hi) that recruits CCR2+ macrophages via CCL2 secretion. PB nanoparticles exhibit ultrahigh affinity (KD = 1.11 × 10−10 m) for free CCL2, inducing conformational distortion in its N‐terminal domain via specific C≡N interface interactions with CRS1 residues, thereby blocking CCR2 engagement, a mechanism distinct from conventional nanomaterials. Although ineffective in monocultures, PB nanoparticles robustly improve cardiac function and remodeling in murine and translational porcine pressure‐overload HF models, reducing left ventricular end‐diastolic volume by 56.2% and fibrosis by 40.5%, while selectively depleting CCR2+ macrophages without systemic immunosuppression. Supported by scalable production (> 100 g/batch), long‐term stability, and biosafety, this work establishes a cell communication‐targeting nanomedicine strategy for network‐driven diseases like HF. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/1c9a52fc-4086-497e-9508-f3fd8baaa673/adma73141-gra-0001-m.png" alt="A Nano-Interception Strategy for Chronic Heart Failure: Prussian Blue Nanoparticles Disrupt Fibroblast-Immune Communication via CCL2 Sequestration"/> <p>A nano-interception strategy disrupts pathogenic fibroblast–macrophage crosstalk in chronic heart failure. Scalable Prussian blue nanoparticles selectively sequester CCL2 via ultrahigh-affinity binding, preventing CCR2<sup>+</sup> macrophage recruitment and breaking a key fibro-inflammatory circuit. This approach demonstrates robust efficacy in murine and porcine models, offering a clinically viable paradigm for network-driven diseases. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Chronic heart failure (HF) remains a global health challenge due to the lack of therapies that effectively disrupt the pathological fibro-inflammatory networks driving disease progression. While current nanomedicine strategies often target intracellular pathways in isolated cell types, they overlook the multicellular crosstalk central to HF. Here, we develop scalably synthesized Prussian blue (PB) nanoparticles that selectively intercept the CCL2-CCR2 chemokine axis, a key pathway in fibroblast-macrophage communication. Single-nucleus RNA sequencing of murine and human failing hearts identifies a conserved pro-fibroinflammatory cardiac fibroblast subpopulation (POSTN<sup>hi</sup> CCL2<sup>hi</sup>) that recruits CCR2<sup>+</sup> macrophages via CCL2 secretion. PB nanoparticles exhibit ultrahigh affinity (K<sub>D</sub> = 1.11 × 10<sup>−10</sup> <span class="smallCaps">m</span>) for free CCL2, inducing conformational distortion in its N-terminal domain via specific C≡N interface interactions with CRS1 residues, thereby blocking CCR2 engagement, a mechanism distinct from conventional nanomaterials. Although ineffective in monocultures, PB nanoparticles robustly improve cardiac function and remodeling in murine and translational porcine pressure-overload HF models, reducing left ventricular end-diastolic volume by 56.2% and fibrosis by 40.5%, while selectively depleting CCR2<sup>+</sup> macrophages without systemic immunosuppression. Supported by scalable production (&gt; 100 g/batch), long-term stability, and biosafety, this work establishes a cell communication-targeting nanomedicine strategy for network-driven diseases like HF.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Bo Chen, Gao Wei, Guowei Zeng, Haoyu Li, Rongrong Wu, Hongyu Yan, Zijie Zhou, Kai Luo, Xiaoyang Zhang, Cong Li, Longming Huang, Lingyan Cheng, Xinjie Zhang, Jinghao Zheng, Bozhong Shi, Xiaojun Cai, Xiaomin He </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>A Nano‐Interception Strategy for Chronic Heart Failure: Prussian Blue Nanoparticles Disrupt Fibroblast‐Immune Communication via CCL2 Sequestration</dc:title>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202520216?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDate>
<prism:coverDisplayDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDisplayDate>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202520216</guid>
<title>APE1‐Triggered Inhalable Microsphere (ATIM) for In Situ Non‐Small Cell Lung Cancer Theranostics</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> This study presents a novel nanomaterial (ATIM) that combines self‐assembling DNA structures and EVs to achieve theranostics of NSCLC. ATIM achieves precise delivery to NSCLC lesions via inhalation administration, homologous targeting, and APE1 responsive targeting. Leveraging an EDC molecular circuit, ATIM enables real‐time imaging and precise therapy of NSCLC, exhibiting substantial potential for future clinical translation. ABSTRACT Distinguishing benign from malignant pulmonary nodules remains a major clinical challenge, where misdiagnosis may lead to either delayed cancer treatment or unnecessary invasive procedures. Here, we report apurinic/apyrimidinic endonuclease 1 (APE1)‐triggered inhalable microsphere (ATIM) for non‐small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) theranostics by leveraging inhalation delivery and homotypic targeting to accelerate the local enrichment of DNA tetrahedrons (TDNs) in pulmonary tumors, which enables in situ NSCLC theranostics. Upon intracellular recognition of the APE1, entropy‐driven catalytic circuits are activated, triggering nanoparticle aggregation to amplify fluorescence for real‐time tumor imaging and releasing miR‐126‐3p to induce tumor cell apoptosis by suppressing ADAM9. In a mouse orthotopic NSCLC model, the tumor‐bearing group showed a fluorescent intensity 1.67‐fold higher than the healthy group, and the pulmonary accumulation of the ATIM system via inhalation was 3.12‐fold higher than that via intravenous injection, while ATIM therapy significantly reduced the tumor burden to a relative area of 45.3 ± 1.6%. Our results demonstrate that ATIM achieves accurate discrimination between benign and malignant pulmonary nodules while effectively inducing apoptosis in tumor cells. This theranostic system offers a promising dual‐functional platform for precision diagnosis and targeted therapy in early‐stage NSCLC. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/0ce4514c-96e6-4828-b058-84d2ce7ddc17/adma73143-gra-0001-m.png" alt="APE1-Triggered Inhalable Microsphere (ATIM) for In Situ Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer Theranostics"/> <p>This study presents a novel nanomaterial (ATIM) that combines self-assembling DNA structures and EVs to achieve theranostics of NSCLC. ATIM achieves precise delivery to NSCLC lesions via inhalation administration, homologous targeting, and APE1 responsive targeting. Leveraging an EDC molecular circuit, ATIM enables real-time imaging and precise therapy of NSCLC, exhibiting substantial potential for future clinical translation. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Distinguishing benign from malignant pulmonary nodules remains a major clinical challenge, where misdiagnosis may lead to either delayed cancer treatment or unnecessary invasive procedures. Here, we report apurinic/apyrimidinic endonuclease 1 (APE1)-triggered inhalable microsphere (ATIM) for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) theranostics by leveraging inhalation delivery and homotypic targeting to accelerate the local enrichment of DNA tetrahedrons (TDNs) in pulmonary tumors, which enables in situ NSCLC theranostics. Upon intracellular recognition of the APE1, entropy-driven catalytic circuits are activated, triggering nanoparticle aggregation to amplify fluorescence for real-time tumor imaging and releasing miR-126-3p to induce tumor cell apoptosis by suppressing ADAM9. In a mouse orthotopic NSCLC model, the tumor-bearing group showed a fluorescent intensity 1.67-fold higher than the healthy group, and the pulmonary accumulation of the ATIM system via inhalation was 3.12-fold higher than that via intravenous injection, while ATIM therapy significantly reduced the tumor burden to a relative area of 45.3 ± 1.6%. Our results demonstrate that ATIM achieves accurate discrimination between benign and malignant pulmonary nodules while effectively inducing apoptosis in tumor cells. This theranostic system offers a promising dual-functional platform for precision diagnosis and targeted therapy in early-stage NSCLC.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Xinlin Guo, Xiaopei Qiu, Xiaolin Hu, Fei Yang, Heng Li, Xiaoxing Wang, Linxi Lv, Jie Xie, Zhengheng Yu, Pingyun Li, Hui Xiao, Tian Wang, Yongzhong Wang, Sergio Bernardini, Wei Gu, Hong Zhang, Yang Luo </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>APE1‐Triggered Inhalable Microsphere (ATIM) for In Situ Non‐Small Cell Lung Cancer Theranostics</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.202520216</dc:identifier>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202521047?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDate>
<prism:coverDisplayDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDisplayDate>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202521047</guid>
<title>Ultrabright, Efficient, and Stable Green Quantum Dot Light‐Emitting Diodes Through Effective Heat Dissipation</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> A degradation mechanism in QD‐LEDs is proposed, whereby accumulated heat reduce charge density for radiative recombination in QDs, driven by carrier delocalization in the QD layer and charge accumulation in the non‐recombination region. This degradation is suppressed by a rapid heat‐dissipation encapsulation strategy using silicon carbide, enabling record‐performance green QD‐LEDs with a luminance of 2 036 000 cd m−2, an EQE of 32.1%, and a T95 lifetime exceeding 32 000 h at 1000 cd m−2. ABSTRACT Heat generation induced by electrical driving is primarily responsible for the device failure in quantum dot light‐emitting diodes (QD‐LEDs). Elucidating the thermally induced degeneration mechanism is crucial to enhancing operational lifetime and luminescent efficiency of the devices. Heightening heat dissipation can mitigate device degradation by utilizing high thermal conductivity materials, allowing for ultrahigh luminance and enhanced efficiency at high driving voltages. Here we constructively propose an underlying mechanism by which heat accumulation within the device enhances carrier delocalization in QDs, and induces the accumulation of numerous holes and electrons in non‐recombination regions, which reduce the charge density available for recombination, resulting in restricted peak brightness and device failure. On this basis, a universally feasible encapsulation strategy is developed to accelerate heat dissipation, enabling record‐breaking green QD‐LEDs with a luminance of 2 036 000 cd m−2, an external quantum efficiency of 32.1%, and a T95 operation lifetime (time for the luminance decreasing by 95%) of more than 32 000 h at 1000 cd m−2. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/79c940ca-f50a-45ff-b612-0c4a1800dcb4/adma73180-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Ultrabright, Efficient, and Stable Green Quantum Dot Light-Emitting Diodes Through Effective Heat Dissipation"/> <p>A degradation mechanism in QD-LEDs is proposed, whereby accumulated heat reduce charge density for radiative recombination in QDs, driven by carrier delocalization in the QD layer and charge accumulation in the non-recombination region. This degradation is suppressed by a rapid heat-dissipation encapsulation strategy using silicon carbide, enabling record-performance green QD-LEDs with a luminance of 2 036 000 cd m<sup>−2</sup>, an EQE of 32.1%, and a <i>T</i> <sub>95</sub> lifetime exceeding 32 000 h at 1000 cd m<sup>−2</sup>. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Heat generation induced by electrical driving is primarily responsible for the device failure in quantum dot light-emitting diodes (QD-LEDs). Elucidating the thermally induced degeneration mechanism is crucial to enhancing operational lifetime and luminescent efficiency of the devices. Heightening heat dissipation can mitigate device degradation by utilizing high thermal conductivity materials, allowing for ultrahigh luminance and enhanced efficiency at high driving voltages. Here we constructively propose an underlying mechanism by which heat accumulation within the device enhances carrier delocalization in QDs, and induces the accumulation of numerous holes and electrons in non-recombination regions, which reduce the charge density available for recombination, resulting in restricted peak brightness and device failure. On this basis, a universally feasible encapsulation strategy is developed to accelerate heat dissipation, enabling record-breaking green QD-LEDs with a luminance of 2 036 000 cd m<sup>−2</sup>, an external quantum efficiency of 32.1%, and a <i>T</i> <sub>95</sub> operation lifetime (time for the luminance decreasing by 95%) of more than 32 000 h at 1000 cd m<sup>−2</sup>.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Han Zhang, Xiaosuo Wang, Zhenjiang Zuo, Huaiyu Xu, Bo Li, Fengjuan Zhang, Liqin Lian, Zhuorang Shen, Wenjing Zhang, Fengjia Fan, Huaibin Shen </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Ultrabright, Efficient, and Stable Green Quantum Dot Light‐Emitting Diodes Through Effective Heat Dissipation</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.202521047</dc:identifier>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202521716?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDate>
<prism:coverDisplayDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDisplayDate>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202521716</guid>
<title>Achieving Zero Phase Transition in P2‐Type Layered Oxides via Targeted Chemical Design for Zero‐Strain Sodium Storage</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> A stage‐specific chemical design strategy is proposed to eliminate the structural driving force behind the phase transitions of P2‐type cathodes by mitigating interlayer oxygen repulsion during deep desodiation. The designed Na0.67Ni0.05Fe0.05Ti0.05Cu0.2Mn0.65O2 cathode, featuring an expanded Na‐layer spacing and reduced oxygen anionic charge density, ensures a zero‐phase transition and achieves an ultra‐low volume variation of 0.062% throughout cycling. ABSTRACT P2‐type layered oxide cathodes dominate sodium‐ion batteries (SIBs) due to exceptional sodium ion kinetics. However, longstanding phase transitions (e.g., P2‐to‐O2) not only compromise this inherent kinetic advantage but also cause severe stress strain undermining structural stability. Here, we propose a stage‐specific chemical design that targetly addresses de‐sodiated interlayer O2− repulsion, the structural origin of phase transitions in P2 cathodes. The designed Na0.67Ni0.05Fe0.05Ti0.05Cu0.2Mn0.65O2 (NFTCM) cathode shows a record Na‐layer spacing (3.67 Å) with reduced negative charge on oxygen ions, maximally lowering O2−–O2− repulsion during the entire desodiation process. As evidenced by in situ X‐ray diffraction, the NFTCM cathode shows a true zero‐phase‐transition behavior with a record‐low volume variation of 0.062% upon cycling. This stable, zero‐strain Na ions storage behavior contributes to exceptional rate capability (121 mA h/g at 10C) and remarkably stable cycling, retaining 93.7% capacity after 600 cycles. Furthermore, operando neutron diffraction data indicate that the eliminated phase transition also enables a robust oxygen framework, a crucial factor in stabilizing the ion storage process of layered oxides. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/ce2d4652-6674-4d07-aeb4-a9b823ba0c98/adma73240-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Achieving Zero Phase Transition in P2-Type Layered Oxides via Targeted Chemical Design for Zero-Strain Sodium Storage"/> <p>A stage-specific chemical design strategy is proposed to eliminate the structural driving force behind the phase transitions of P2-type cathodes by mitigating interlayer oxygen repulsion during deep desodiation. The designed Na<sub>0.67</sub>Ni<sub>0.05</sub>Fe<sub>0.05</sub>Ti<sub>0.05</sub>Cu<sub>0.2</sub>Mn<sub>0.65</sub>O<sub>2</sub> cathode, featuring an expanded Na-layer spacing and reduced oxygen anionic charge density, ensures a zero-phase transition and achieves an ultra-low volume variation of 0.062% throughout cycling. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>P2-type layered oxide cathodes dominate sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) due to exceptional sodium ion kinetics. However, longstanding phase transitions (e.g., P2-to-O2) not only compromise this inherent kinetic advantage but also cause severe stress strain undermining structural stability. Here, we propose a stage-specific chemical design that targetly addresses de-sodiated interlayer O<sup>2−</sup> repulsion, the structural origin of phase transitions in P2 cathodes. The designed Na<sub>0.67</sub>Ni<sub>0.05</sub>Fe<sub>0.05</sub>Ti<sub>0.05</sub>Cu<sub>0.2</sub>Mn<sub>0.65</sub>O<sub>2</sub> (NFTCM) cathode shows a record Na-layer spacing (3.67 Å) with reduced negative charge on oxygen ions, maximally lowering O<sup>2−</sup>–O<sup>2−</sup> repulsion during the entire desodiation process. As evidenced by in situ X-ray diffraction, the NFTCM cathode shows a true zero-phase-transition behavior with a record-low volume variation of 0.062% upon cycling. This stable, zero-strain Na ions storage behavior contributes to exceptional rate capability (121 mA h/g at 10C) and remarkably stable cycling, retaining 93.7% capacity after 600 cycles. Furthermore, <i>operando</i> neutron diffraction data indicate that the eliminated phase transition also enables a robust oxygen framework, a crucial factor in stabilizing the ion storage process of layered oxides.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Na Li, Pengfei Liu, Juping Xu, Huaican Chen, Yuanguang Xia, Fangwei Wang, Wen Yin, Jinkui Zhao, Enyue Zhao </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Achieving Zero Phase Transition in P2‐Type Layered Oxides via Targeted Chemical Design for Zero‐Strain Sodium Storage</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.202521716</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>

<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>
<prism:volume>38</prism:volume>
<prism:number>31</prism:number>
...
