<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202520433?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:55:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:55:05-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDate>
<prism:coverDisplayDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</prism:coverDisplayDate>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202520433</guid>
<title>Beyond Earth: Resilience of Quasi‐2D Perovskite Solar Cells in Space</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, Volume 38, Issue 31, 2 June 2026. </description>
<dc:description> In the article (DOI: 10.1002/adma.202520433), Christoph Putz and co‐workers demonstrate rigid quasi‐2D perovskite solar cells operating in low Earth orbit, delivering stable power for more than 100 days under real‐space conditions. In‐orbit performance is correlated with extensive ground‐based thermal and proton‐irradiation studies on rigid and ultrathin devices, revealing high intrinsic radiation tolerance, substrate‐driven losses, and key challenges for future lightweight perovskite space photovoltaics. ABSTRACT Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) offer unique advantages for space‐based energy harvesting, combining cost‐effective manufacturing with flexible, high power‐to‐weight devices that can reduce payload mass in deployable structures. Despite this promise, few reports have demonstrated the viability of this technology in realistic, space‐based scenarios, where they are subjected to large temperature variations and hard radiation. Here, we present a comprehensive analysis of PSC performance in low Earth orbit (LEO). The champion rigid cell exhibited relatively stable in‐orbit performance at ∼80% of initial efficiency over a 44‐day measurement interval that concluded nearly 100 days after launch, corresponding to ∼1600 orbital eclipse cycles and temperature ranges from −25 to 35°C. Mission data was systematically compared with laboratory measurements of rigid and ultrathin flexible PSCs across temperatures from −80 to +80°C and upon exposure to high‐energy proton radiation. Flexible devices retained over 92% efficiency after a proton dose equivalent to 50 years in orbit. Despite this radiation tolerance, mitigating pre‐flight environmental degradation remains a challenge for ultrathin substrates. Combined, this study bridges the gap between short suborbital demonstrations and long‐term orbital performance, highlighting the potential of PSCs as a low‐cost, resilient alternative for light harvesting, even in harsh space environments. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/e371dadd-4f49-4ff5-a744-71d237e54723/adma72485-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Beyond Earth: Resilience of Quasi-2D Perovskite Solar Cells in Space"/> <p>In the article (DOI: 10.1002/adma.202520433), Christoph Putz and co-workers demonstrate rigid quasi-2D perovskite solar cells operating in low Earth orbit, delivering stable power for more than 100 days under real-space conditions. In-orbit performance is correlated with extensive ground-based thermal and proton-irradiation studies on rigid and ultrathin devices, revealing high intrinsic radiation tolerance, substrate-driven losses, and key challenges for future lightweight perovskite space photovoltaics. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) offer unique advantages for space-based energy harvesting, combining cost-effective manufacturing with flexible, high power-to-weight devices that can reduce payload mass in deployable structures. Despite this promise, few reports have demonstrated the viability of this technology in realistic, space-based scenarios, where they are subjected to large temperature variations and hard radiation. Here, we present a comprehensive analysis of PSC performance in low Earth orbit (LEO). The champion rigid cell exhibited relatively stable in-orbit performance at ∼80% of initial efficiency over a 44-day measurement interval that concluded nearly 100 days after launch, corresponding to ∼1600 orbital eclipse cycles and temperature ranges from −25 to 35°C. Mission data was systematically compared with laboratory measurements of rigid and ultrathin flexible PSCs across temperatures from −80 to +80°C and upon exposure to high-energy proton radiation. Flexible devices retained over 92% efficiency after a proton dose equivalent to 50 years in orbit. Despite this radiation tolerance, mitigating pre-flight environmental degradation remains a challenge for ultrathin substrates. Combined, this study bridges the gap between short suborbital demonstrations and long-term orbital performance, highlighting the potential of PSCs as a low-cost, resilient alternative for light harvesting, even in harsh space environments.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Christoph Putz, Lukas E. Lehner, Stepan Demchyshyn, Bekele Hailegnaw, Phillip Jahelka, Magdalena Breitwieser, Sercan Özen, Andrea Denker, Jürgen Bundesmann, Alina Hanna Dittwald, Dilara Karabulut, Philipp Tockhorn, Steve Albrecht, Felix Lang, Markus C. Scharber, Michael D. Kelzenberg, Harry A. Atwater, Martin Kaltenbrunner </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Beyond Earth: Resilience of Quasi‐2D Perovskite Solar Cells in Space</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.202520433</dc:identifier>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73456?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:25:24 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:25:24-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73456</guid>
<title>Artificially Designed 2D Composite Material With Tailored Thermal Expansion Toward Temperature‐Stable and Accurate Nanomechanical Pressure Sensors</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Graphene‐MoS2 heterostructures with tailored thermal expansion enable 2D NEMS resonators with near‐zero temperature‐induced frequency drift from 200 to 400 K. The resulting pressure sensors show two orders of magnitude better thermal stability and nearly 2000‐fold higher signal‐to‐error ratio, opening a practical route to accurate sensing under realistic operating conditions. ABSTRACT Two‐dimensional (2D) materials are suitable for constructing resonant nanoelectromechanical systems (NEMS) toward advanced sensing applications, due to their ultrasmall mass, high Young's modulus, and large strain limit. However, there exists a fundamental limitation: the thermal expansion of 2D materials leads to intrinsic frequency susceptibility of 2D NEMS to temperature variation, which has significantly impaired their deployment in realistic sensing applications. Here, leveraging the layer degree of freedom in 2D materials, we realize a type of artificially designed 2D composite materials, in this case specifically designed graphene–molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) van der Waals heterostructures, with tailored thermal expansion, and successfully suppress temperature‐induced frequency drift by leveraging the near‐zero net thermal expansion. With this approach, we demonstrate temperature‐stable and highly‐accurate NEMS pressure sensors: within 200–400 K range, we improve the temperature stability by 2‐orders‐of‐magnitude compared with individual MoS2 and graphene devices, and achieve nearly 2000‐fold enhancement of signal‐to‐error ratio over the entire temperature range required for automotive grade sensors. Our strategy offers an effective solution, with clear design guideline, for tailoring the thermal expansion of 2D materials, opening new possibilities in temperature‐stable 2D NEMS sensing applications under realistic environmental settings. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/a3320ef6-1192-48be-8c15-1ef08399991d/adma73456-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Artificially Designed 2D Composite Material With Tailored Thermal Expansion Toward Temperature-Stable and Accurate Nanomechanical Pressure Sensors"/> <p>Graphene-MoS<sub>2</sub> heterostructures with tailored thermal expansion enable 2D NEMS resonators with near-zero temperature-induced frequency drift from 200 to 400 K. The resulting pressure sensors show two orders of magnitude better thermal stability and nearly 2000-fold higher signal-to-error ratio, opening a practical route to accurate sensing under realistic operating conditions. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Two-dimensional (2D) materials are suitable for constructing resonant nanoelectromechanical systems (NEMS) toward advanced sensing applications, due to their ultrasmall mass, high Young's modulus, and large strain limit. However, there exists a fundamental limitation: the thermal expansion of 2D materials leads to intrinsic frequency susceptibility of 2D NEMS to temperature variation, which has significantly impaired their deployment in realistic sensing applications. Here, leveraging the layer degree of freedom in 2D materials, we realize a type of artificially designed 2D composite materials, in this case specifically designed graphene–molybdenum disulfide (MoS<sub>2</sub>) van der Waals heterostructures, with tailored thermal expansion, and successfully suppress temperature-induced frequency drift by leveraging the near-zero net thermal expansion. With this approach, we demonstrate temperature-stable and highly-accurate NEMS pressure sensors: within 200–400 K range, we improve the temperature stability by 2-orders-of-magnitude compared with individual MoS<sub>2</sub> and graphene devices, and achieve nearly 2000-fold enhancement of signal-to-error ratio over the entire temperature range required for automotive grade sensors. Our strategy offers an effective solution, with clear design guideline, for tailoring the thermal expansion of 2D materials, opening new possibilities in temperature-stable 2D NEMS sensing applications under realistic environmental settings.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Pengcheng Zhang, Jiankai Zhu, Ziluo Su, Yueyang Jia, Zejuan Zhang, Luming Wang, Zuheng Liu, Yalan Wang, Shuang Cai, Bo Xu, Zenghui Wang, Rui Yang </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Artificially Designed 2D Composite Material With Tailored Thermal Expansion Toward Temperature‐Stable and Accurate Nanomechanical Pressure Sensors</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73456</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73526?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:11:30 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:11:30-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73526</guid>
<title>NiMo Dual‐Atom Dimers on Pd Nanosheets for Selective C─H and C─C Bond Cleavage of Ethylene Glycol From Waste Plastics</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> CO2 laser–assisted synthesis enables rapid immobilization of NiMo dual‐atom dimers on Pd nanosheets (NiMo‐DA/PdNS) for electro‐reforming of PET‐derived ethylene glycol. The catalyst achieves potential‐tunable selectivity toward glycolate (C2) and formate (C1) with high Faradaic efficiency, while simultaneously recovering high‐purity terephthalic acid and demonstrating promising techno‐economic viability for plastic upcycling. ABSTRACT Herein, we report the CO gas–mediated synthesis of palladium nanosheets (PdNS) as the first palladene support for atomically dispersed dimer catalysts. We further present the first report of NiMo dual‐atom dimer immobilization on PdNS (NiMo‐DA/PdNS) using rapid CO2 laser irradiation within 10 min. The NiMo‐DA/PdNS platform exhibits enhanced ethylene glycol (EG) oxidation reaction (EGOR) activity with potential‐dependent product selectivity. At 1.0 V vs. RHE, glycolate (C2 product from selective C─H bond cleavage) was obtained with a Faradaic efficiency (FE) of 81% and a yield rate (YR) of 1.14 mmol h−1 cm−2. At 1.6 V vs. RHE, formate (C1 product from C─C bond cleavage) was selectively produced with an FE of 77.9% and a YR of 0.39 mmol h−1 cm−2. Theoretical calculations further corroborate these performance trends and elucidate the mechanism that C─C bond scission is facilitated by electron transfer to the catalyst surface at higher potentials, lowering the reaction free energy and enhancing C1 formation. This study demonstrates concurrent high selectivity toward both glycolate and formate while efficiently recovering high‐purity terephthalic acid (TPA) monomers from polyethylene terephthalate (PET) hydrolysate electrolysis over 50 h, highlighting the practical potential of atomically engineered catalysts with real‐world PET upcycling. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/7d25d31b-7535-46ad-a85d-607409205452/adma73526-gra-0001-m.png" alt="NiMo Dual-Atom Dimers on Pd Nanosheets for Selective C─H and C─C Bond Cleavage of Ethylene Glycol From Waste Plastics"/> <p>CO<sub>2</sub> laser–assisted synthesis enables rapid immobilization of NiMo dual-atom dimers on Pd nanosheets (NiMo-DA/PdNS) for electro-reforming of PET-derived ethylene glycol. The catalyst achieves potential-tunable selectivity toward glycolate (C2) and formate (C1) with high Faradaic efficiency, while simultaneously recovering high-purity terephthalic acid and demonstrating promising techno-economic viability for plastic upcycling. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Herein, we report the CO gas–mediated synthesis of palladium nanosheets (PdNS) as the first palladene support for atomically dispersed dimer catalysts. We further present the first report of NiMo dual-atom dimer immobilization on PdNS (NiMo-DA/PdNS) using rapid CO<sub>2</sub> laser irradiation within 10 min. The NiMo-DA/PdNS platform exhibits enhanced ethylene glycol (EG) oxidation reaction (EGOR) activity with potential-dependent product selectivity. At 1.0 V vs. RHE, glycolate (C2 product from selective C─H bond cleavage) was obtained with a Faradaic efficiency (FE) of 81% and a yield rate (YR) of 1.14 mmol h<sup>−1</sup> cm<sup>−2</sup>. At 1.6 V vs. RHE, formate (C1 product from C─C bond cleavage) was selectively produced with an FE of 77.9% and a YR of 0.39 mmol h<sup>−1</sup> cm<sup>−2</sup>. Theoretical calculations further corroborate these performance trends and elucidate the mechanism that C─C bond scission is facilitated by electron transfer to the catalyst surface at higher potentials, lowering the reaction free energy and enhancing C1 formation. This study demonstrates concurrent high selectivity toward both glycolate and formate while efficiently recovering high-purity terephthalic acid (TPA) monomers from polyethylene terephthalate (PET) hydrolysate electrolysis over 50 h, highlighting the practical potential of atomically engineered catalysts with real-world PET upcycling.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Seongbo Lee, Jayaraman Theerthagiri, Shih‐Huang Pan, Wanwisa Limphirat, Jyh‐Chiang Jiang, Myong Yong Choi </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>NiMo Dual‐Atom Dimers on Pd Nanosheets for Selective C─H and C─C Bond Cleavage of Ethylene Glycol From Waste Plastics</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73526</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73526</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73526?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>
...
</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73570?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:08:32 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:08:32-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73570</guid>
<title>Electronic Delocalization‐Confinement Coupling in Edge‐Coordinated CQDs@MXene Enables Hydrogen‐Bond Modulation for Ultrafast Proton Transport</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> To address interfacial ion‐transport issues in 2D confined spaces, the electron delocalization of surface O‐terminations in MXene nanosheets is precisely tuned through CQDs edge‐coordination bridging. This weakens the interfacial rigid HB network of confined water, accelerating proton transport. As a result, the optimized CQDs@MXene electrode achieves a volumetric capacitance of 2507.2 F cm−3 and retains 65.8% at 1000 mV s−1. ABSTRACT Efficient proton transport in 2D confined electrodes critically depends on the flexibility of interfacial hydrogen‐bond networks. However, the interfacial issue becomes more pronounced in the 2D confined space. The strong hydrogen bonds between confined water molecules and surface ─O terminations of MXenes immobilize protons and hinder charge storage kinetics. Here, we introduce an edge‐coordination strategy to achieve precise electronic delocalization modulation in Ti3C2Tx MXene by anchoring carboxyl‐functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) at positively charged edges. The CQDs induce substantial electron delocalization on surface ─O sites, which simultaneously weakens rigid hydrogen bonds and facilitates interfacial charge transfer. This regulation establishes a dynamic hydrogen‐bond network that supports continuous Grotthuss‐type proton migration within the confined channels. Consequently, the optimized CQDs@MXene electrode delivers a volumetric capacitance of 2507.2 F cm−3, retains 65.8% at 1000 mV s−1, and maintains nearly 100% stability over 10 000 cycles. In situ vibrational spectroscopy and density functional theory reveal that the electron delocalization drives the weak hydrogen‐bond interface and charge transfer coupling governs proton transport kinetics. This work establishes electronic delocalization as an effective paradigm for manipulating hydrogen‐bond dynamics and interfacial charge transport for ultrafast ion transport in confined electrochemical systems. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/7f0346fc-510c-4497-a857-729c7d5d11d0/adma73570-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Electronic Delocalization-Confinement Coupling in Edge-Coordinated CQDs@MXene Enables Hydrogen-Bond Modulation for Ultrafast Proton Transport"/> <p>To address interfacial ion-transport issues in 2D confined spaces, the electron delocalization of surface O-terminations in MXene nanosheets is precisely tuned through CQDs edge-coordination bridging. This weakens the interfacial rigid HB network of confined water, accelerating proton transport. As a result, the optimized CQDs@MXene electrode achieves a volumetric capacitance of 2507.2 F cm<sup>−3</sup> and retains 65.8% at 1000 mV s<sup>−1</sup>. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Efficient proton transport in 2D confined electrodes critically depends on the flexibility of interfacial hydrogen-bond networks. However, the interfacial issue becomes more pronounced in the 2D confined space. The strong hydrogen bonds between confined water molecules and surface ─O terminations of MXenes immobilize protons and hinder charge storage kinetics. Here, we introduce an edge-coordination strategy to achieve precise electronic delocalization modulation in Ti<sub>3</sub>C<sub>2</sub>T<i> <sub>x</sub> </i> MXene by anchoring carboxyl-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) at positively charged edges. The CQDs induce substantial electron delocalization on surface ─O sites, which simultaneously weakens rigid hydrogen bonds and facilitates interfacial charge transfer. This regulation establishes a dynamic hydrogen-bond network that supports continuous Grotthuss-type proton migration within the confined channels. Consequently, the optimized CQDs@MXene electrode delivers a volumetric capacitance of 2507.2 F cm<sup>−3</sup>, retains 65.8% at 1000 mV s<sup>−1</sup>, and maintains nearly 100% stability over 10 000 cycles. In situ vibrational spectroscopy and density functional theory reveal that the electron delocalization drives the weak hydrogen-bond interface and charge transfer coupling governs proton transport kinetics. This work establishes electronic delocalization as an effective paradigm for manipulating hydrogen-bond dynamics and interfacial charge transport for ultrafast ion transport in confined electrochemical systems.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Che Liu, Kaiyang Guo, Yan Liu, Huiyin Zheng, Jun Ma, Zhuosen Wang, Xinwei Cui, Yapeng Tian </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Electronic Delocalization‐Confinement Coupling in Edge‐Coordinated CQDs@MXene Enables Hydrogen‐Bond Modulation for Ultrafast Proton Transport</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73570</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73570</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73570?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.202521775?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Tue, 02 Jun 2026 00:00:00 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-02T12:00:00-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.202521775</guid>
<title>Large Exchange Bias Effect in Geometrically Frustrated Spin Glass Through High‐Density Coherent Chemical Interfaces</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Through the tailored design of high‐density coherent chemical interfaces, we synthesized an intermetallic frustrated spin glass exhibiting a large exchange bias effect. This effect originates from the field responses of local clusters linked by coherent interfaces, specifically those with fluctuating ferromagnetic‐like components, which are pinned within a frustrated antiferromagnetic matrix in the three‐dimensional interlaced configuration. ABSTRACT The pursuit of next‐generation spintronic devices has focused heavily on compensated ferrimagnets and non‐collinear antiferromagnets, thanks to their significant exchange bias (EB) effects and minimal stray fields. However, spin glass (SG) systems are reemerging as compelling candidates despite their historically weak EB responses and poorly understood pinning mechanisms. Herein, we report the discovery of a geometrically frustrated SG intermetal material (Mn32Co5In15) exhibiting large EB effects (∼0.3 T), achieved by engineering high‐density coherent interfaces. Specifically, this material features a 3D interlaced structure consisting of frustrated antiferromagnetic (f‐AFM) Mn‐rich clusters and ferromagnetic‐like (FML) In‐dominant clusters, consistent with topological spin glass states. Using field‐dependent neutron scattering combined with theoretical calculations, we established the first experimental evidence that the large EB arises from the local pinning of FML states within frozen, coherent f‐AFM matrices. This pinning breaks the ergodicity inherent to typical SG systems, while spin polarization of FML clusters creates an energetically favorable anisotropic channel in response to external fields. These findings offer new possibilities for leveraging frustrated spin glasses as pivotal functional materials in spintronic devices. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/74b9d1ec-08a4-4ec0-a785-a6a2e74056f4/adma73437-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Large Exchange Bias Effect in Geometrically Frustrated Spin Glass Through High-Density Coherent Chemical Interfaces"/> <p>Through the tailored design of high-density coherent chemical interfaces, we synthesized an intermetallic frustrated spin glass exhibiting a large exchange bias effect. This effect originates from the field responses of local clusters linked by coherent interfaces, specifically those with fluctuating ferromagnetic-like components, which are pinned within a frustrated antiferromagnetic matrix in the three-dimensional interlaced configuration. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>The pursuit of next-generation spintronic devices has focused heavily on compensated ferrimagnets and non-collinear antiferromagnets, thanks to their significant exchange bias (EB) effects and minimal stray fields. However, spin glass (SG) systems are reemerging as compelling candidates despite their historically weak EB responses and poorly understood pinning mechanisms. Herein, we report the discovery of a geometrically frustrated SG intermetal material (Mn<sub>32</sub>Co<sub>5</sub>In<sub>15</sub>) exhibiting large EB effects (∼0.3 T), achieved by engineering high-density coherent interfaces. Specifically, this material features a 3D interlaced structure consisting of frustrated antiferromagnetic (f-AFM) Mn-rich clusters and ferromagnetic-like (FML) In-dominant clusters, consistent with topological spin glass states. Using field-dependent neutron scattering combined with theoretical calculations, we established the first experimental evidence that the large EB arises from the local pinning of FML states within frozen, coherent f-AFM matrices. This pinning breaks the ergodicity inherent to typical SG systems, while spin polarization of FML clusters creates an energetically favorable anisotropic channel in response to external fields. These findings offer new possibilities for leveraging frustrated spin glasses as pivotal functional materials in spintronic devices.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Hankun Xu, Sergii Khmelevskyi, Wenjie Li, Haowei Zhou, Chuanying Xi, Langsheng Ling, Ze Wang, Junfeng Wang, Chao Dong, Kenichi Kato, Shuki Torii, Jing Chen, Qinghua Zhang, Yili Cao, Qiang Li, Sihao Deng, Lunhua He, Kun Lin, Xianran Xing </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Large Exchange Bias Effect in Geometrically Frustrated Spin Glass Through High‐Density Coherent Chemical Interfaces</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.202521775</dc:identifier>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73602?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 23:20:37 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-01T11:20:37-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73602</guid>
<title>Excitation Dependent Nonconventional Afterglow for 6D Dynamic Encryptions</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Endowing engineering polymers with brand‐new functions remains a substantial challenge, especially through physical modifications. This work proposes a simple strategy to utilize a series of aliphatic polyesters as luminophores and constructs inorganic‐polymer composites with excitation‐dependent afterglow. Based on these unique properties, 6D dynamic encryption and multimodal anti‐counterfeiting platforms are successfully prepared. ABSTRACT Nonconventional luminescent materials without traditional aromatic chromophores have attracted significant research interest. However, achieving persistent luminescence under visible‐light excitation in those systems is inherently difficult. Herein, we report a simple and general strategy to develop intrinsic excitation‐dependent afterglow in aliphatic polyesters via hybridization with metaboric acid (MBA), in which the afterglow peak shifts from 431 to 540 nm upon tuning the excitation source from 254 to 405 nm, owing to the formation of clusters with varied sizes during the fabrication. Moreover, the afterglow emission color could be tuned from deep blue to red via triplet‐to‐singlet Förster resonance energy transfer (TS‐FRET) by doping with a commercial dye. These inorganic‐polymer composites could be fabricated into 3D artwares and multi‐mode anti‐counterfeiting labels. Leveraging the multicolor afterglow and spatial manipulation, a 6D dynamic encryption platform using a luminescent magic cube was demonstrated. This work not only provided a novel composite‐induced strategy for creating intrinsic excitation‐dependent afterglow from aliphatic polyesters but also successfully demonstrated their pioneering application in advanced information encryption. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/4f8af684-1071-49a9-bc5e-27c6b3b50111/adma73602-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Excitation Dependent Nonconventional Afterglow for 6D Dynamic Encryptions"/> <p>Endowing engineering polymers with brand-new functions remains a substantial challenge, especially through physical modifications. This work proposes a simple strategy to utilize a series of aliphatic polyesters as luminophores and constructs inorganic-polymer composites with excitation-dependent afterglow. Based on these unique properties, 6D dynamic encryption and multimodal anti-counterfeiting platforms are successfully prepared. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Nonconventional luminescent materials without traditional aromatic chromophores have attracted significant research interest. However, achieving persistent luminescence under visible-light excitation in those systems is inherently difficult. Herein, we report a simple and general strategy to develop intrinsic excitation-dependent afterglow in aliphatic polyesters via hybridization with metaboric acid (MBA), in which the afterglow peak shifts from 431 to 540 nm upon tuning the excitation source from 254 to 405 nm, owing to the formation of clusters with varied sizes during the fabrication. Moreover, the afterglow emission color could be tuned from deep blue to red via triplet-to-singlet Förster resonance energy transfer (TS-FRET) by doping with a commercial dye. These inorganic-polymer composites could be fabricated into 3D artwares and multi-mode anti-counterfeiting labels. Leveraging the multicolor afterglow and spatial manipulation, a 6D dynamic encryption platform using a luminescent magic cube was demonstrated. This work not only provided a novel composite-induced strategy for creating intrinsic excitation-dependent afterglow from aliphatic polyesters but also successfully demonstrated their pioneering application in advanced information encryption.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Shu‐Yue Yang, Wen‐Ting Wu, Guang‐Yue Li, Zhipei Zhang, Jiuyuan Li, Tong‐Yu Zhang, Yi‐Lu Zhang, Zi‐Qing Xiong, Zhi‐Yuan Zhang, Chunju Li, Sheng‐Hua Li, Qian Zhao </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Excitation Dependent Nonconventional Afterglow for 6D Dynamic Encryptions</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73602</dc:identifier>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73543?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 23:00:10 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-01T11:00:10-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73543</guid>
<title>Engineering Interfacial Water for Advanced Electrocatalytic CO2 Reduction: From Molecular Understanding to Materials Design</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Interfacial water crucially dictates the kinetics and selectivity of electrocatalytic CO2 reduction. This review examines the dynamic evolution of interfacial hydrogen‐bond networks and systematically highlights multi‐scale modulation strategies. Integrating advanced in situ spectroscopic characterizations, it provides a fundamental framework for designing microenvironments to optimize catalytic performance. ABSTRACT Electrocatalytic CO2 reduction (CO2RR) offers a direct pathway for converting CO2 gas into fuels and value‐added chemicals. However, achieving CO2RR with high activity and stability remains a significant challenge. While previous research has predominantly focused on catalyst design, the role of interfacial water has often been overlooked. Interfacial water can act as the primary proton donor, directly influencing the competition between CO2RR and the hydrogen evolution reaction. Moreover, the dynamic hydrogen‐bond network of interfacial water can significantly alter the water dissociation barrier, thereby enhancing selectivity toward CO2RR products. This review systematically summarizes the fundamental properties of interfacial water in CO2RR, classifies the strategies for modulating interfacial water, and discusses the applicability of these strategies and their impact on CO2RR performance. Particular emphasis is placed on the effect of interfacial water structural evolution on the CO2RR mechanism, as well as the corresponding characterization techniques. This review provides a structured framework for the study of interfacial water in CO2RR and offers guidance for the rational design of high‐efficiency catalysts. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/48a40aa1-e39b-43de-b81b-9602b9d926f5/adma73543-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Engineering Interfacial Water for Advanced Electrocatalytic CO2 Reduction: From Molecular Understanding to Materials Design"/> <p>Interfacial water crucially dictates the kinetics and selectivity of electrocatalytic CO<sub>2</sub> reduction. This review examines the dynamic evolution of interfacial hydrogen-bond networks and systematically highlights multi-scale modulation strategies. Integrating advanced in situ spectroscopic characterizations, it provides a fundamental framework for designing microenvironments to optimize catalytic performance. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Electrocatalytic CO<sub>2</sub> reduction (CO<sub>2</sub>RR) offers a direct pathway for converting CO<sub>2</sub> gas into fuels and value-added chemicals. However, achieving CO<sub>2</sub>RR with high activity and stability remains a significant challenge. While previous research has predominantly focused on catalyst design, the role of interfacial water has often been overlooked. Interfacial water can act as the primary proton donor, directly influencing the competition between CO<sub>2</sub>RR and the hydrogen evolution reaction. Moreover, the dynamic hydrogen-bond network of interfacial water can significantly alter the water dissociation barrier, thereby enhancing selectivity toward CO<sub>2</sub>RR products. This review systematically summarizes the fundamental properties of interfacial water in CO<sub>2</sub>RR, classifies the strategies for modulating interfacial water, and discusses the applicability of these strategies and their impact on CO<sub>2</sub>RR performance. Particular emphasis is placed on the effect of interfacial water structural evolution on the CO<sub>2</sub>RR mechanism, as well as the corresponding characterization techniques. This review provides a structured framework for the study of interfacial water in CO<sub>2</sub>RR and offers guidance for the rational design of high-efficiency catalysts.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Yongzhe Xia, Ping Guan, Kaian Sun, Xin Tan, Zewen Zhuang, Wei Yan, Jiujun Zhang, Chen Chen </dc:creator>
<category>REVIEW</category>
<dc:title>Engineering Interfacial Water for Advanced Electrocatalytic CO2 Reduction: From Molecular Understanding to Materials Design</dc:title>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73575?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 22:52:44 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-01T10:52:44-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73575</guid>
<title>An Absorption‐Transport Reconfigurable 2D/3D/2D Architecture Enables Multispectral‐Adaptive Bifacial Perovskite Photovoltaics</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> An absorption–transport reconfigurable 2D/3D/2D perovskite heterostructure enables a single bifacial device to operate efficiently under distinct illumination spectra. The photovoltaics achieve 24.69% under AM 1.5G and maintain high efficiencies under indoor lighting (38.45% under warm‐white LED and 37.55% under TL84), together with ∼92% bifaciality and >2000 h MPPT stability. ABSTRACT Tandem architectures have emerged as the prevailing strategy to overcome the fundamental trade‐off between broadband photon absorption and efficient photon‐energy utilization in photovoltaic devices. However, only the complex and implementation‐demanding multi‐terminal tandem configuration offers adaptability to diverse light sources. Here, we introduce an absorption‐transport reconfigurable architecture implemented in a bifacial single‐junction device, which harnesses the previously overlooked parasitic absorption of a 2D perovskite passivation layer to achieve spectral absorption separation between high‐energy blue‐violet and low‐energy green‐to‐near‐infrared photons. This design not only enables efficient bifacial operation with near‐omnidirectional light harvesting but also supports broadly applicable photon‐energy harvesting under both sunlight and artificial lighting. Notably, the Voc under blue‐light illumination reaches 1.67 V, exceeding the Voc ceiling of a 1.55 eV single‐junction absorber. In terms of applicability, a single device can deliver PCEs of 24.69% (AM 1.5G), 38.45% (warm‐white LED), and 37.55% (cool‐white TL84 fluorescent), alongside a record Voc of 1.081 V under weak illumination (1122 lux/346 µW cm−2). Owing to the robust interfacial anchoring design, the champion device retains >90% of its initial performance after 2000 h of continuous operation, thereby offering a viable pathway toward rapid, scenario‐agnostic deployment. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/ad414b05-5e63-4f59-85dd-91debe3fcae4/adma73575-gra-0001-m.png" alt="An Absorption-Transport Reconfigurable 2D/3D/2D Architecture Enables Multispectral-Adaptive Bifacial Perovskite Photovoltaics"/> <p>An absorption–transport reconfigurable 2D/3D/2D perovskite heterostructure enables a single bifacial device to operate efficiently under distinct illumination spectra. The photovoltaics achieve 24.69% under AM 1.5G and maintain high efficiencies under indoor lighting (38.45% under warm-white LED and 37.55% under TL84), together with ∼92% bifaciality and &gt;2000 h MPPT stability. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Tandem architectures have emerged as the prevailing strategy to overcome the fundamental trade-off between broadband photon absorption and efficient photon-energy utilization in photovoltaic devices. However, only the complex and implementation-demanding multi-terminal tandem configuration offers adaptability to diverse light sources. Here, we introduce an absorption-transport reconfigurable architecture implemented in a bifacial single-junction device, which harnesses the previously overlooked parasitic absorption of a 2D perovskite passivation layer to achieve spectral absorption separation between high-energy blue-violet and low-energy green-to-near-infrared photons. This design not only enables efficient bifacial operation with near-omnidirectional light harvesting but also supports broadly applicable photon-energy harvesting under both sunlight and artificial lighting. Notably, the <i>V</i> <sub>oc</sub> under blue-light illumination reaches 1.67 V, exceeding the <i>V</i> <sub>oc</sub> ceiling of a 1.55 eV single-junction absorber. In terms of applicability, a single device can deliver PCEs of 24.69% (AM 1.5G), 38.45% (warm-white LED), and 37.55% (cool-white TL84 fluorescent), alongside a record <i>V</i> <sub>oc</sub> of 1.081 V under weak illumination (1122 lux/346 µW cm<sup>−2</sup>). Owing to the robust interfacial anchoring design, the champion device retains &gt;90% of its initial performance after 2000 h of continuous operation, thereby offering a viable pathway toward rapid, scenario-agnostic deployment.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Siyang Zhang, Haoxuan Sun, Min Wang, Da Dou, Chen Li, Liang Li </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>An Absorption‐Transport Reconfigurable 2D/3D/2D Architecture Enables Multispectral‐Adaptive Bifacial Perovskite Photovoltaics</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73575</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73575</prism:doi>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73548?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 22:51:19 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-01T10:51:19-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73548</guid>
<title>Multi‐Dimensional Insights Into the Surface and Interfaces of Battery Materials by Time‐of‐Flight Secondary Ion Mass Spectrometry</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Time‐of‐flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (ToF‐SIMS) provides multi‐dimensional insights into the complex surface and interfacial chemistry of battery materials. This review article comprehensively discusses the fundamental principles and unique attributes of ToF‐SIMS, highlighting its applications in the characterization of electrode‐electrolyte interphases (EEIs). Current limitations and challenges of using ToF‐SIMS for battery study are also discussed. ABSTRACT Time‐of‐flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (ToF‐SIMS) has emerged as a pivotal analytical technique for characterizing the complex surface and interfacial chemistry of battery materials. This review article comprehensively discusses the fundamental principles and unique attributes of ToF‐SIMS, highlighting its advantages such as ultrahigh surface sensitivity (ppm‐ppb), high spatial resolution (< 50 nm laterally, and < 1 nm in depth), and high mass resolution for hydrogen and isotope study. In the field of battery study, these attributes enable precise, multi‐dimensional characterization of electrode‐electrolyte interphases (EEIs). In the previous works, ToF‐SIMS has been employed to resolve the composition and structures at the nanoscale of EEIs, track the dynamic evolution and degradation of electrode surface, differentiate artificial coating interphases from the bulk materials, and detect isotopes for mechanistic studies. The burgeoning field of in situ liquid ToF‐SIMS for probing the authentic solvation structures and aging mechanism of electrolytes is also highlighted. Challenges of using ToF‐SIMS for battery study, such as the matrix effects, beam‐induced damage, and need for higher sensitivity and depth resolution, are also discussed. In summary, ToF‐SIMS provides multi‐dimensional insights into battery interfaces, which help to establish structure‐property correlations and will continuously guide the rational design of the next‐generation high‐performance energy storage systems. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/20c33253-ce31-4bcc-8c55-975b935b3339/adma73548-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Multi-Dimensional Insights Into the Surface and Interfaces of Battery Materials by Time-of-Flight Secondary Ion Mass Spectrometry"/> <p>Time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (ToF-SIMS) provides multi-dimensional insights into the complex surface and interfacial chemistry of battery materials. This review article comprehensively discusses the fundamental principles and unique attributes of ToF-SIMS, highlighting its applications in the characterization of electrode-electrolyte interphases (EEIs). Current limitations and challenges of using ToF-SIMS for battery study are also discussed. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (ToF-SIMS) has emerged as a pivotal analytical technique for characterizing the complex surface and interfacial chemistry of battery materials. This review article comprehensively discusses the fundamental principles and unique attributes of ToF-SIMS, highlighting its advantages such as ultrahigh surface sensitivity (ppm-ppb), high spatial resolution (&lt; 50 nm laterally, and &lt; 1 nm in depth), and high mass resolution for hydrogen and isotope study. In the field of battery study, these attributes enable precise, multi-dimensional characterization of electrode-electrolyte interphases (EEIs). In the previous works, ToF-SIMS has been employed to resolve the composition and structures at the nanoscale of EEIs, track the dynamic evolution and degradation of electrode surface, differentiate artificial coating interphases from the bulk materials, and detect isotopes for mechanistic studies. The burgeoning field of in situ liquid ToF-SIMS for probing the authentic solvation structures and aging mechanism of electrolytes is also highlighted. Challenges of using ToF-SIMS for battery study, such as the matrix effects, beam-induced damage, and need for higher sensitivity and depth resolution, are also discussed. In summary, ToF-SIMS provides multi-dimensional insights into battery interfaces, which help to establish structure-property correlations and will continuously guide the rational design of the next-generation high-performance energy storage systems.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Yao Zhao, Yu Zhang, Sen Xin, Fuyi Wang </dc:creator>
<category>REVIEW</category>
<dc:title>Multi‐Dimensional Insights Into the Surface and Interfaces of Battery Materials by Time‐of‐Flight Secondary Ion Mass Spectrometry</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73548</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>

<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73548?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>REVIEW</prism:section>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73568?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 22:50:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-01T10:50:05-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73568</guid>
<title>Ultrathin Li Metal Anodes: Quantitative Design Principles and Manufacturability Across Liquid and Solid‐State Batteries</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Ultrathin lithium metal anodes (≤15 µm) offer a promising route to high‐energy‐density batteries due to their high capacity and low potential. This review presents design principles for ultrathin Li, evaluates fabrication strategies, and discusses challenges in liquid and solid‐state cells. Future perspectives highlight their potential to enhance energy density and enable next‐generation energy storage technologies. ABSTRACT Li metal anode shows significant potential for advancing high‐energy‐density and commercially viable lithium batteries due to its high specific capacity and low electrochemical potential. However, thinning Li metal encounters serious challenges owing to its mechanical stickiness and fragility during the mechanical rolling process, which severely restricts its practical utilization. Consequently, most current Li metal batteries rely on excessively thick Li foils, leading to substantial resource waste and undermining the pursuit of high energy density. This review highlights the quantitative design principles of ultrathin Li metal (≤15 µm) and elucidates its critical roles in realizing the true potential of Li metal batteries. Emerging strategies for the fabrication of ultrathin Li metal, followed by a critical evaluation of recent advances and persistent challenges in their deployment for both liquid and solid‐state batteries, are summarized. A perspective on future directions for ultrathin Li metal is also presented. Ultrathin Li metal anodes are poised to deliver transformative improvements in energy density, unlocking new opportunities for advanced energy storage systems. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/3f071e57-5764-4eb4-8949-b7e9f924f98d/adma73568-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Ultrathin Li Metal Anodes: Quantitative Design Principles and Manufacturability Across Liquid and Solid-State Batteries"/> <p>Ultrathin lithium metal anodes (≤15 µm) offer a promising route to high-energy-density batteries due to their high capacity and low potential. This review presents design principles for ultrathin Li, evaluates fabrication strategies, and discusses challenges in liquid and solid-state cells. Future perspectives highlight their potential to enhance energy density and enable next-generation energy storage technologies. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Li metal anode shows significant potential for advancing high-energy-density and commercially viable lithium batteries due to its high specific capacity and low electrochemical potential. However, thinning Li metal encounters serious challenges owing to its mechanical stickiness and fragility during the mechanical rolling process, which severely restricts its practical utilization. Consequently, most current Li metal batteries rely on excessively thick Li foils, leading to substantial resource waste and undermining the pursuit of high energy density. This review highlights the quantitative design principles of ultrathin Li metal (≤15 µm) and elucidates its critical roles in realizing the true potential of Li metal batteries. Emerging strategies for the fabrication of ultrathin Li metal, followed by a critical evaluation of recent advances and persistent challenges in their deployment for both liquid and solid-state batteries, are summarized. A perspective on future directions for ultrathin Li metal is also presented. Ultrathin Li metal anodes are poised to deliver transformative improvements in energy density, unlocking new opportunities for advanced energy storage systems.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Cheng Wang, Caoyu Wang, Shuixin Xia, Jodie A. Yuwono, Mingnan Li, Yanqiu Lyu, Ruizhi Zhang, Jun Wang, Jianfeng Mao, Zaiping Guo </dc:creator>
<category>REVIEW</category>
<dc:title>Ultrathin Li Metal Anodes: Quantitative Design Principles and Manufacturability Across Liquid and Solid‐State Batteries</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73568</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73568</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73568?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>REVIEW</prism:section>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73555?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 22:49:19 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-01T10:49:19-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73555</guid>
<title>Chirality‐Induced Twisted Supramolecular Assembly Unlocks Ultrahigh Energy Density at Elevated Temperatures</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> Chiral molecules and their chiral enantiomer are incorporated into polyethersulfone (PES) to construct supramolecular chiral co‐assembly structure. Through π–π stacking interactions, the chiral molecules induce chiral expression at the macroscopic polymer chain level. The resulting locally twisted structure enlarges the torsional angle between the sulfone‐flanking phenyl rings and constructs carrier traps, which significantly enhance the breakdown strength. ABSTRACT To meet the demands of high‐power‐density electronics, high‐temperature‐resistant polymer dielectrics are essential for ensuring reliable operation. Although doping with molecular semiconductors effectively improves the high‐temperature performance of polymer dielectrics, the semiconductors often exist in an isolated, discontinuous distribution. Beyond the inherent issues of poor dispersion and interfacial incompatibility, this morphology may also form conductive pathways under excessive doping, resulting in severe degradation of dielectric properties. Herein, we incorporated chiral molecules e.g., (13bR)‐5,6‐dihydro‐5‐(trans‐4‐propylcyclohexyl)‐4H‐dinaphtho[2,1‐f:1’,2’‐h][1,5]dioxonin (R5011) and its chiral enantiomer (S5011) into polyethersulfone (PES) to construct supramolecular chiral co‐assembly structure. Through π–π stacking interactions, the chiral molecules give rise to an amplified and emergent chiral expression at the macroscopic polymer chain level. The local twisted structure formed by chiral amplification leads to an enlarged torsional angle between the sulfone‐flanking phenyl rings and constructs carrier traps, thereby significantly enhancing the breakdown strength. Ultimately, the co‐assembly PES achieved high energy densities of 8.73 J cm−3 (150°C) with efficiency of 90% and maximum discharge energy density of 10.08 J cm−3 at 150°C. Furthermore, stacked film capacitors based on the PES composite films demonstrated excellent high‐temperature capacitance stability at 150°C. This work demonstrates a novel approach to designing advanced polymer dielectrics through supramolecular interactions. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/be635c4f-33ef-414e-b46e-61acbdf7c756/adma73555-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Chirality-Induced Twisted Supramolecular Assembly Unlocks Ultrahigh Energy Density at Elevated Temperatures"/> <p>Chiral molecules and their chiral enantiomer are incorporated into polyethersulfone (PES) to construct supramolecular chiral co-assembly structure. Through <i>π–π</i> stacking interactions, the chiral molecules induce chiral expression at the macroscopic polymer chain level. The resulting locally twisted structure enlarges the torsional angle between the sulfone-flanking phenyl rings and constructs carrier traps, which significantly enhance the breakdown strength. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>To meet the demands of high-power-density electronics, high-temperature-resistant polymer dielectrics are essential for ensuring reliable operation. Although doping with molecular semiconductors effectively improves the high-temperature performance of polymer dielectrics, the semiconductors often exist in an isolated, discontinuous distribution. Beyond the inherent issues of poor dispersion and interfacial incompatibility, this morphology may also form conductive pathways under excessive doping, resulting in severe degradation of dielectric properties. Herein, we incorporated chiral molecules e.g., (13bR)-5,6-dihydro-5-(trans-4-propylcyclohexyl)-4H-dinaphtho[2,1-f:1’,2’-h][1,5]dioxonin (R5011) and its chiral enantiomer (S5011) into polyethersulfone (PES) to construct supramolecular chiral co-assembly structure. Through <i>π</i>–<i>π</i> stacking interactions, the chiral molecules give rise to an amplified and emergent chiral expression at the macroscopic polymer chain level. The local twisted structure formed by chiral amplification leads to an enlarged torsional angle between the sulfone-flanking phenyl rings and constructs carrier traps, thereby significantly enhancing the breakdown strength. Ultimately, the co-assembly PES achieved high energy densities of 8.73 J cm<sup>−3</sup> (150°C) with efficiency of 90% and maximum discharge energy density of 10.08 J cm<sup>−3</sup> at 150°C. Furthermore, stacked film capacitors based on the PES composite films demonstrated excellent high-temperature capacitance stability at 150°C. This work demonstrates a novel approach to designing advanced polymer dielectrics through supramolecular interactions.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Shuo Zhao, Le Zhou, Bingyu Zou, Yang Zhao, Huanyu Lei, Mufeng Zhang, Shiyi Wu, Erxiang Xu, Xin Li, Junshang Zhang, Fan Ye, Mingjun Huang, Ce‐Wen Nan, Yang Shen </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Chirality‐Induced Twisted Supramolecular Assembly Unlocks Ultrahigh Energy Density at Elevated Temperatures</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73555</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73555</prism:doi>
<prism:url>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73555?af=R</prism:url>
<prism:section>RESEARCH ARTICLE</prism:section>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73533?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 22:48:41 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-01T10:48:41-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73533</guid>
<title>Dendritic Lipopeptide Nanovaccines Orchestrate Multi‐Pattern Recognition Receptors Activation and Potentiate Antitumor Immunity</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> We developed dendritic lipopeptide‐based, self‐adjuvanting TLR2/4 agonists. These nanoscale assemblies drive robust dendritic cell maturation and efficient antigen cross‐presentation, triggering sustained cytokine production and potent T‐cell activation. This platform demonstrated remarkable therapeutic efficacy by eradicating established melanoma and colorectal tumors, preventing pulmonary metastasis, and eliciting long‐lasting memory T‐cell immunity. ABSTRACT Precise control over the spatial and temporal delivery of antigens and adjuvants is essential for eliciting effective and durable immune responses. Here, we report a self‐assembling nanovaccine platform based on dendritic lipopeptides (DLPs) that integrate delivery and immune‐stimulation through a structurally defined design. DLPs are constructed by conjugating structurally optimized lipid tails to second‐generation lysine‐ or arginine‐rich dendritic scaffolds. Systematic engineering of the lipid domain allows for precise fine‐tuning of the amphiphilic structure, yielding molecular candidates with significantly enhanced TLR2/4 agonist activity. These structures facilitate antigen uptake and cross‐presentation by APCs in a spatiotemporally regulated manner. Co‐assembly with lipidated peptide antigens and a lipid‐modified TLR7/8 agonist yields nanoscale vaccines capable of orchestrating synergistic multi‐pattern recognition receptor (PRR) activation. In murine melanoma and colorectal tumor models, these nanovaccines elicit robust cytotoxic T lymphocyte responses, enhance antigen‐specific killing by up to 40%, and induce significant tumor regression. This work presents a chemically defined, modular nanoplatform that mimics key features of pathogen‐like immunity and provides a rational strategy for engineering minimalist cancer vaccines with high efficacy and translational potential. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/3f9ebd0f-94dd-4db3-b133-8cc25acd6038/adma73533-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Dendritic Lipopeptide Nanovaccines Orchestrate Multi-Pattern Recognition Receptors Activation and Potentiate Antitumor Immunity"/> <p>We developed dendritic lipopeptide-based, self-adjuvanting TLR2/4 agonists. These nanoscale assemblies drive robust dendritic cell maturation and efficient antigen cross-presentation, triggering sustained cytokine production and potent T-cell activation. This platform demonstrated remarkable therapeutic efficacy by eradicating established melanoma and colorectal tumors, preventing pulmonary metastasis, and eliciting long-lasting memory T-cell immunity. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Precise control over the spatial and temporal delivery of antigens and adjuvants is essential for eliciting effective and durable immune responses. Here, we report a self-assembling nanovaccine platform based on dendritic lipopeptides (DLPs) that integrate delivery and immune-stimulation through a structurally defined design. DLPs are constructed by conjugating structurally optimized lipid tails to second-generation lysine- or arginine-rich dendritic scaffolds. Systematic engineering of the lipid domain allows for precise fine-tuning of the amphiphilic structure, yielding molecular candidates with significantly enhanced TLR2/4 agonist activity. These structures facilitate antigen uptake and cross-presentation by APCs in a spatiotemporally regulated manner. Co-assembly with lipidated peptide antigens and a lipid-modified TLR7/8 agonist yields nanoscale vaccines capable of orchestrating synergistic multi-pattern recognition receptor (PRR) activation. In murine melanoma and colorectal tumor models, these nanovaccines elicit robust cytotoxic T lymphocyte responses, enhance antigen-specific killing by up to 40%, and induce significant tumor regression. This work presents a chemically defined, modular nanoplatform that mimics key features of pathogen-like immunity and provides a rational strategy for engineering minimalist cancer vaccines with high efficacy and translational potential.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Muhetaerjiang Mamuti, Dan Zhu, Chenxi Yin, Xuejian Zhang, Qinran Yu, Zhetong Jin, Lei Jiang, Weizhi Chen, Xiqun Jiang </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Dendritic Lipopeptide Nanovaccines Orchestrate Multi‐Pattern Recognition Receptors Activation and Potentiate Antitumor Immunity</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73533</dc:identifier>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73589?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 22:47:05 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-01T10:47:05-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73589</guid>
<title>A Library of Dipolar Skyrmion Bags Observed in a Centrosymmetric van der Waals Magnet</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> A library of skyrmion bag states is realized in a centrosymmetric van der Waals magnet via femtosecond laser excitation. It includes multiple type‐I configurations with distinct loops and circularities, along with their corresponding type‐II states. Their energetic stability is systematically explored, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding dipolar skyrmion bag textures. ABSTRACT Topological magnetic solitons, such as skyrmions, exhibit topological protection and emergent electrodynamics that make them compelling for both fundamental research and spintronic applications. Beyond the canonical skyrmion, higher‐order and composite textures, such as skyrmion bag, offer arbitrary topological charges and internal morphology that critically governs their energetics and excitation dynamics. However, despite numerous theoretical proposals, experimental access to distinct skyrmion‐bag morphologies has so far been scarce and mainly confined to Dzyaloshinskii‐Moriya‐interaction (DMI)‐based systems. Here, we demonstrate a broad spectrum of skyrmion bags by using femtosecond‐laser excitation in a centrosymmetric van der Waals magnet Cr1.6Ti0.03Te2, referred to as dipolar skyrmion bags. The observed skyrmion bags are composed of closed domain‐wall loops with different circularities and varying loop numbers, as well as their corresponding type‐II counterparts, far beyond the limited skyrmion bags in DMI systems. Combining Lorentz transmission electron microscopy with micromagnetic simulations, we investigate in details their stability, circularity‐dependent field evolution, and distinct annihilation channels. This work substantially expands the experimentally accessible family of topological spin textures and pave the way toward multi‐state, reconfigurable spintronic functionalities based on dipolar skyrmion bags. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/9d019047-f3a3-48f4-adeb-fdd67ee65053/adma73589-gra-0001-m.png" alt="A Library of Dipolar Skyrmion Bags Observed in a Centrosymmetric van der Waals Magnet"/> <p>A library of skyrmion bag states is realized in a centrosymmetric van der Waals magnet via femtosecond laser excitation. It includes multiple type-I configurations with distinct loops and circularities, along with their corresponding type-II states. Their energetic stability is systematically explored, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding dipolar skyrmion bag textures. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Topological magnetic solitons, such as skyrmions, exhibit topological protection and emergent electrodynamics that make them compelling for both fundamental research and spintronic applications. Beyond the canonical skyrmion, higher-order and composite textures, such as skyrmion bag, offer arbitrary topological charges and internal morphology that critically governs their energetics and excitation dynamics. However, despite numerous theoretical proposals, experimental access to distinct skyrmion-bag morphologies has so far been scarce and mainly confined to Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya-interaction (DMI)-based systems. Here, we demonstrate a broad spectrum of skyrmion bags by using femtosecond-laser excitation in a centrosymmetric van der Waals magnet Cr<sub>1.6</sub>Ti<sub>0.03</sub>Te<sub>2</sub>, referred to as dipolar skyrmion bags. The observed skyrmion bags are composed of closed domain-wall loops with different circularities and varying loop numbers, as well as their corresponding type-II counterparts, far beyond the limited skyrmion bags in DMI systems. Combining Lorentz transmission electron microscopy with micromagnetic simulations, we investigate in details their stability, circularity-dependent field evolution, and distinct annihilation channels. This work substantially expands the experimentally accessible family of topological spin textures and pave the way toward multi-state, reconfigurable spintronic functionalities based on dipolar skyrmion bags.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Yue Hu, Donghai Yang, Xuange Hu, Huai Zhang, Jiangteng Guo, Ying Deng, Zhipeng Hou, Fengshan Zheng, Xuewen Fu </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>A Library of Dipolar Skyrmion Bags Observed in a Centrosymmetric van der Waals Magnet</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73589</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>
<prism:doi>10.1002/adma.73589</prism:doi>

<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73573?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 22:47:00 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-01T10:47:00-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73573</guid>
<title>Direct White–Light Reconfiguration of Dynamic Covalent Polymer Networks via Photoinsertable Spirothiopyran</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> ABSTRACT Light offers a powerful, noninvasive tool for polymer reprocessing and recycling. However, existing strategies predominantly rely on high–energy ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, which often induces material degradation and compromises the integrity of dynamic covalent networks. Herein, we report a white–light–activated addition reaction between spirothiopyran (STP) and poly(disulfide) that enables the direct structural reconfiguration of polymer networks. Upon illumination (λ > 400 nm), STP isomerizes to its ring–opened thiomerocyanine (TMC) form, exposing a reactive ene moiety, while the poly(disulfide) backbone homolytically dissociates into sulfur–centered radicals. These intermediates undergo rapid thiol–ene coupling, establishing dynamic C–S linkages. Notably, this process operates efficiently within bulk poly(disulfide) matrices, allowing the direct incorporation of STP into the polymer backbone and subsequent topological rearrangement of the network. The resulting photoinsertion introduces additional crosslinks, which not only enhance the mechanical properties and photostability but also enable spatiotemporal control over material welding, adhesion, remolding, and recycling. Furthermore, the synergy between STP photochromism and spatially controlled photocrosslinking unlocks advanced functionalities such as dual–color encryption and biomimetic morphogenesis. This work establishes a sustainable, visible–light–driven platform for polymer reconfiguration and opens new avenues toward smart, adaptable functional materials. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/f094e4bd-91c3-45a3-ab37-2d99320495a4/adma73573-gra-0001-m.png" alt="Direct White–Light Reconfiguration of Dynamic Covalent Polymer Networks via Photoinsertable Spirothiopyran"/> <p> </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Light offers a powerful, noninvasive tool for polymer reprocessing and recycling. However, existing strategies predominantly rely on high–energy ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, which often induces material degradation and compromises the integrity of dynamic covalent networks. Herein, we report a white–light–activated addition reaction between spirothiopyran (STP) and poly(disulfide) that enables the direct structural reconfiguration of polymer networks. Upon illumination (λ &gt; 400 nm), STP isomerizes to its ring–opened thiomerocyanine (TMC) form, exposing a reactive ene moiety, while the poly(disulfide) backbone homolytically dissociates into sulfur–centered radicals. These intermediates undergo rapid thiol–ene coupling, establishing dynamic C–S linkages. Notably, this process operates efficiently within bulk poly(disulfide) matrices, allowing the direct incorporation of STP into the polymer backbone and subsequent topological rearrangement of the network. The resulting photoinsertion introduces additional crosslinks, which not only enhance the mechanical properties and photostability but also enable spatiotemporal control over material welding, adhesion, remolding, and recycling. Furthermore, the synergy between STP photochromism and spatially controlled photocrosslinking unlocks advanced functionalities such as dual–color encryption and biomimetic morphogenesis. This work establishes a sustainable, visible–light–driven platform for polymer reconfiguration and opens new avenues toward smart, adaptable functional materials.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Shuailong Zhou, Mengqi Du, Zhaomiao Chu, Xuehan Yang, Chuang Li </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>Direct White–Light Reconfiguration of Dynamic Covalent Polymer Networks via Photoinsertable Spirothiopyran</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73573</dc:identifier>
<prism:publicationName>Advanced Materials</prism:publicationName>

</item>
<item>
<link>https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/adma.73560?af=R</link>
<pubDate>Mon, 01 Jun 2026 22:45:13 -0700</pubDate>
<dc:date>2026-06-01T10:45:13-07:00</dc:date>
<source url="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214095?af=R">Wiley: Advanced Materials: Table of Contents</source>
<prism:coverDate/>
<prism:coverDisplayDate/>
<guid isPermaLink="false">10.1002/adma.73560</guid>
<title>A Self‐Cascading Immunomodulatory Hydrogel for Remodeling Infected Diabetic Wounds</title>
<description>Advanced Materials, EarlyView. </description>
<dc:description> A self‐cascading hypoglycemic hydrogel to treat MRSA‐infected diabetic foot ulcers. By integrating Fe3O4@Au nanozymes, the platform converts glucose into antimicrobial agents, eradicating 99.99% of bacteria. Crucially, it neutralizes inflammatory PAMPs and reprograms macrophages to a pro‐healing phenotype, significantly accelerating wound closure and tissue regeneration. ABSTRACT Methicillin‐resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA)‐infected diabetic foot ulcers (DFUs) remain refractory to healing owing to persistent inflammation, hyperglycemia, and impaired tissue regeneration. Current antimicrobial strategies primarily eliminate viable bacteria but overlook pathogen‐associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) released upon bacterial death, which sustain NF‐κB/NLRP3 activation and prevent immune resolution. However, suppressing microbial burden without neutralizing PAMP‐driven inflammation fails to restore the regenerative wound microenvironment. Here, we report a self‐cascading hypoglycemic immunomodulatory hydrogel integrating Fe3O4@Au nanozymes with phenylboronic acid‐modified hyaluronic acid and dopamine‐functionalized silk fibroin. The platform exploits endogenous glucose to drive cascade catalysis for efficient MRSA eradication (∼99.99% with near‐infrared assistance), while dynamically exposing catechol and boronic acid motifs to sequester PAMPs and suppress inflammatory signaling. Consequently, macrophages are reprogrammed from pro‐inflammatory M1 to pro‐healing M2 phenotypes, enabling immune microenvironment remodeling. In MRSA‐infected DFU models, this coordinated antibacterial‐immunomodulatory strategy markedly accelerates wound closure, leaving only ∼13.97% residual area after 14 days. These results establish a design paradigm that couples biocatalysis with immune regulation for treating complex infected wounds. </dc:description>
<content:encoded><img src="https://advanced.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/cms/asset/5e803010-25fa-430f-a05c-d1db8eea7cfb/adma73560-gra-0001-m.png" alt="A Self-Cascading Immunomodulatory Hydrogel for Remodeling Infected Diabetic Wounds"/> <p>A self-cascading hypoglycemic hydrogel to treat MRSA-infected diabetic foot ulcers. By integrating Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Au nanozymes, the platform converts glucose into antimicrobial agents, eradicating 99.99% of bacteria. Crucially, it neutralizes inflammatory PAMPs and reprograms macrophages to a pro-healing phenotype, significantly accelerating wound closure and tissue regeneration. </p> <br/> <h2>ABSTRACT</h2> <p>Methicillin-resistant <i>Staphylococcus aureus</i> (MRSA)-infected diabetic foot ulcers (DFUs) remain refractory to healing owing to persistent inflammation, hyperglycemia, and impaired tissue regeneration. Current antimicrobial strategies primarily eliminate viable bacteria but overlook pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) released upon bacterial death, which sustain NF-κB/NLRP3 activation and prevent immune resolution. However, suppressing microbial burden without neutralizing PAMP-driven inflammation fails to restore the regenerative wound microenvironment. Here, we report a self-cascading hypoglycemic immunomodulatory hydrogel integrating Fe<sub>3</sub>O<sub>4</sub>@Au nanozymes with phenylboronic acid-modified hyaluronic acid and dopamine-functionalized silk fibroin. The platform exploits endogenous glucose to drive cascade catalysis for efficient MRSA eradication (∼99.99% with near-infrared assistance), while dynamically exposing catechol and boronic acid motifs to sequester PAMPs and suppress inflammatory signaling. Consequently, macrophages are reprogrammed from pro-inflammatory M1 to pro-healing M2 phenotypes, enabling immune microenvironment remodeling. In MRSA-infected DFU models, this coordinated antibacterial-immunomodulatory strategy markedly accelerates wound closure, leaving only ∼13.97% residual area after 14 days. These results establish a design paradigm that couples biocatalysis with immune regulation for treating complex infected wounds.</p></content:encoded>
<dc:creator> Yulong Lan, Xiaoliang Qi, Jing Chen, Lifeng Tan, Jianliang Shen </dc:creator>
<category>RESEARCH ARTICLE</category>
<dc:title>A Self‐Cascading Immunomodulatory Hydrogel for Remodeling Infected Diabetic Wounds</dc:title>
<dc:identifier>10.1002/adma.73560</dc:identifier>

</channel>

</rss>
